**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Results** and **Discussion** sections of a research paper. Discuss the writing conventions, tense usage, and typical errors committed by students in each section, with suitable examples from an engineering study. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

A well-structured abstract contains five components:

1. **Background** — 1–2 sentences establishing the domain and problem.
2. **Objective** — a clear statement of the research aim.
3. **Methods** — a concise description of approach, materials, and procedure.
4. **Results** — the principal numerical or qualitative finding.
5. **Conclusion** — the implication, application, or contribution.

**Why an abstract must be self-contained and free of citations:**
- The abstract is indexed independently in databases (Scopus, Web of Science, Google Scholar). Readers often decide whether to download the full paper **based on the abstract alone**.
- A self-contained abstract allows comprehension without external references.
- Citations in the abstract break the standalone nature and consume the limited word budget.

**Standard tense conventions:**

$$\text{Abstract tense mapping} = \begin{cases} \text{Past tense: Methods, Results ("We measured... We found...")} \\ \text{Present tense: Conclusion ("The findings indicate... suggest...")} \end{cases}$$

(Marking scheme: 3 marks for components + 2 marks for self-contained reasoning + 2 marks for tense conventions = **7 marks**)

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

| Aspect | Results Section | Discussion Section |
|---|---|---|
| **Communicative purpose** | Presents findings objectively | Interprets and contextualizes findings |
| **Tense** | Past tense | Present tense |
| **Voice** | Active or passive | Mostly active |
| **Numerical detail** | Full statistics reported | Selective, supporting interpretation |
| **Citations to literature** | None | Extensive |
| **Interpretation** | Avoided | Central |

**Example from engineering study:**

In a study on the flexural strength of bamboo-reinforced concrete beams:

- **Results write-up (correct):**
*"The average flexural strength of bamboo-reinforced beams was 14.2 MPa (SD = 1.1), compared to 17.8 MPa (SD = 0.9) for steel-reinforced control beams. The difference was statistically significant (t(58) = 12.4, p < 0.001)."*

- **Discussion write-up (correct):**
*"The lower flexural strength of bamboo-reinforced beams is consistent with the findings of Ghavami (2005), who reported a 20–25% reduction. This reduction can be attributed to the lower modulus of elasticity of bamboo fibers, which results in larger deflection at peak load. Despite this limitation, bamboo-reinforced beams remain viable for low-cost rural housing, where the strength-to-cost ratio justifies the trade-off."*

- **Common error in Results (incorrect):**
*"The bamboo-reinforced beams were weaker than steel-reinforced ones, which suggests that bamboo is not a good replacement for steel."* — This is **interpretation leakage** and should not appear in Results.

- **Common error in Discussion (incorrect):**
*"The flexural strength was 14.2 MPa. The standard deviation was 1.1. The p-value was less than 0.001."* — This is **mere restatement of Results** without analysis.

(Marking scheme: 3 marks for comparison + 2 marks for tense conventions + 2 marks for engineering examples = **7 marks**)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Writing sections in chronological order of the research process** instead of in logical IMRaD order is a frequent mistake. Students often describe *"first we collected data, then we analyzed it"* in the Results section, which is narrative writing, not scientific reporting.
> 2. **Conflating Results and Discussion** — the most common error in KTU M.Tech thesis evaluation. Students paste interpretive sentences into the Results section, losing 2–3 marks per instance.
> 3. **Generic, unfocused introductions** that fail to identify the research gap are penalized heavily. An introduction without a clear research gap is judged incomplete.
> 4. **Inconsistent reference styles** (mixing APA and IEEE formats within the same paper) lead to automatic deduction.
> 5. **Exceeding word limits** in abstracts is a desk-rejection criterion in journals and is treated similarly in KTU valuation.

---

## Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

- **IMRaD is the gold standard**: Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion — every section has a unique job and must not be confused with another.
- **Title**: 8–15 words, specific, accurate, no filler phrases like *"A Study of"* unless necessary.
- **Abstract**: 150–300 words, self-contained, no citations, IMRaD micro-structure, past tense for methods/results and present tense for conclusion.
- **Introduction**: Follow the **funnel logic** — broad → narrow → gap → objective. End with a clearly stated research aim or hypothesis.
- **Methods**: Past tense, passive voice, sufficient detail to enable **replication**, include statistical analysis with significance level $\alpha = 0.05$.
- **Results**: Past tense, **objective**, no interpretation, supported by tables and figures, report descriptive and inferential statistics.
- **Discussion**: Present tense, **interpretive**, compare with prior literature, explain mechanisms, acknowledge limitations, suggest future scope.
- **Conclusion**: Brief synthesis, not a verbatim copy of the abstract.
- **References**: Follow a single consistent style (APA / IEEE / Harvard) throughout.
- **Drafting order**: Most researchers write **Methods → Results → Discussion → Introduction → Abstract → Title**, in reverse of the final paper order.
- **Tense map**: Introduction (present + past) → Methods (past) → Results (past) → Discussion (present) → Conclusion (present).
- **Voice map**: Methods traditionally use passive voice; all other sections favor active voice in modern journals.
- **Visual aids**: Every table and figure must be referenced in the text and must be self-explanatory.
- **Common pitfalls**: Vague titles, citations in abstracts, missing research gaps, interpretation leakage into Results, restating Results in Discussion, inconsistent references.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Revising a Paper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Revising a Paper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Revising a Paper** is the systematic, iterative process of critically reviewing, refining, and restructuring a research manuscript prior to formal submission (or after peer review) to enhance its **scientific rigor**, **clarity of communication**, **logical coherence**, and **alignment with the target journal's editorial standards**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, revision is treated as a **non-linear, multi-stage cognitive operation** that integrates self-evaluation, peer feedback, and editorial compliance into a single unified workflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> *Revision is the process of re-examining the structure, content, language, and presentation of a research paper to ensure it effectively communicates the research findings, meets the standards of academic writing, and addresses potential concerns of reviewers and readers.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pottery Wheel" of Scholarship

Imagine you have just sculpted a clay pot.

- **Drafting** = pressing the wet clay onto the wheel for the first time. The shape exists, but it's rough, lopsided, and full of imperfections.
- **Revising** = spinning the wheel, dipping your hands in water, and patiently smoothing out the bulges, evening the walls, and redefining the rim.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Revisions Analogy (Donald Murray's Framework):**
> 1. **First Revision — "The Reader's Eye"**: Read as a *naive reader*. Does the story make sense?
> 2. **Second Revision — "The Editor's Eye"**: Check structure, flow, and logical sequencing.
> 3. **Third Revision — "The Proofreader's Eye"**: Check grammar, spelling, and citation accuracy.

## 1.3 Why Revision Matters — The "Cost of Poor Revision"

| Aspect | Without Revision | With Systematic Revision |
|---|---|---|
| **Acceptance Rate** | **15–20%** (baseline) | **35–45%** (industry average) |
| **Reviewer Rounds** | 3–4 rounds | 1–2 rounds |
| **Time to Publication** | 12–18 months | 6–9 months |
| **Citation Impact** | Lower | Higher (clarity = citability) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** A well-revised paper is not a "perfect" paper. It is a paper where every flaw has been either *fixed* or *consciously justified*.

## 1.4 The Two Universes of Revision

Revision occurs in **two distinct phases**, each requiring different mental models:

1. **Pre-Submission Revision (Author-Driven)** — performed *before* sending the manuscript to a journal. Self-revision + peer feedback from colleagues/supervisors.
2. **Post-Review Revision (Author-Editor-Reviewer Loop)** — performed *after* receiving peer review comments. Requires constructing a **point-by-point response letter**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often treat revision as a *cosmetic grammar-check* exercise. In reality, KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation rewards **structural, conceptual, and analytical revision** — not just surface-level editing.

## 1.5 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Substantive Revision** | Changes to ideas, arguments, evidence, structure |
| **Surface Revision** | Changes to grammar, punctuation, spelling, formatting |
| **Peer Review** | Evaluation by independent experts in the field |
| **Response Letter** | Formal document addressing each reviewer comment |
| **Rebuttal** | Author's defense against reviewer criticism |
| **Track Changes** | Word-processor feature showing additions/deletions |
| **Version Control** | Systematic naming/numbering of manuscript drafts (v1, v2, v3) |
| **Line Edits** | Sentence-level corrections within paragraphs |
| **Developmental Editing** | Big-picture structural revision of argument flow |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Hierarchical Levels of Revision

Revision is not monolithic. The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEFTT755) explicitly recognizes a **three-tier hierarchy**, moving from macro to micro concerns:

### Level 1: Global / Substantive Revision (Big Picture)

- **Focus:** Argument strength, logical flow, research question alignment
- **Questions to ask:**
  - Does the introduction clearly state the research gap?
  - Is the methodology appropriate for the research question?
  - Are results presented in a way that answers the research question?
  - Does the discussion interpret results (not just restate them)?

### Level 2: Structural / Organizational Revision (Mid Picture)

- **Focus:** Paragraph order, section balance, transitions, coherence
- **Questions to ask:**
  - Does each paragraph have a single, clear topic sentence?
  - Are transitions between sections smooth ("however", "subsequently", "in contrast")?
  - Is the IMRaD structure correctly followed?
  - Are figures/tables referenced in sequence in the text?

### Level 3: Local / Surface Revision (Micro Picture)

- **Focus:** Grammar, word choice, citations, formatting, numerical consistency
- **Questions to ask:**
  - Are subject-verb agreements correct?
  - Is tense consistent (past for methods/results, present for established facts)?
  - Are all in-text citations present in the reference list?
  - Does the formatting match the journal's template?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The 2024 Scheme Module 3 explicitly emphasizes that **substantive revision (Level 1) carries 60% weightage** in practical assessments, structural revision carries 25%, and surface revision carries 15%.

## 2.2 The "SPACE" Framework for Self-Revision

A mnemonic widely used in academic writing pedagogy for systematic self-revision:

| Letter | Element | Self-Check Question |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Structure** | Does the paper follow IMRaD / journal format? |
| **P** | **Purpose** | Is the research objective clear in the abstract and introduction? |
| **A** | **Audience** | Is the language pitched at the right technical level? |
| **C** | **Clarity** | Are sentences free from ambiguity and jargon? |
| **E** | **Evidence** | Is every claim supported by data or citation? |

## 2.3 The Post-Review Revision Workflow

When reviewers return comments, the author must execute a **structured response protocol**:

### Step-by-Step Response Protocol

1. **Triage Comments** — Categorize each comment as:
   - ✅ *Accept and implement* (major or minor)
   - 🔄 *Clarify/misunderstanding* (reviewer likely misread)
   - ❌ *Respectfully disagree* (with strong justification)

2. **Construct Response Table** — Use a two-column format:
   - Column 1: Reviewer comment (verbatim or paraphrased)
   - Column 2: Author response + page/line number of revision

3. **Implement Revisions** — Make all changes in the manuscript **before** writing the response letter (so you can cite line numbers accurately).

4. **Highlight Changes** — Use track changes, colored text, or a "marked-up" version showing all modifications.

5. **Write Cover Letter to Editor** — Summarize major changes, thank reviewers, and reaffirm suitability.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Revision Checklist (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Checklist Item | Verified (✓/✗) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Title is concise (10–15 words), specific, and contains key terms |  |
| 2 | Abstract follows structured format (Background, Aim, Methods, Results, Conclusions) |  |
| 3 | Keywords (3–6) are specific and not from the title |  |
| 4 | Introduction ends with a clear research gap + objective statement |  |
| 5 | Methods section enables replication (sufficient detail) |  |
| 6 | Results are presented objectively without interpretation |  |
| 7 | Discussion interprets results, not just restates them |  |
| 8 | Limitations are honestly acknowledged |  |
| 9 | All figures/tables are referenced in text in numerical order |  |
| 10 | Conclusion states contribution, not just summary |  |
| 11 | References follow target journal style (APA/IEEE/Harvard) consistently |  |
| 12 | Plagiarism check completed (similarity < 15–20%) |  |
| 13 | All co-authors have approved the final version |  |
| 14 | Journal submission guidelines followed (word count, format) |  |
| 15 | Response letter addresses *every* reviewer comment |  |

> [!TIP]
> **Pearson & Field (2011) - Writing and Research Note:** "Revision is not rewriting; it is *re-seeing* the work from a distance."

## 2.5 Revision Heuristics (Quantitative Rules of Thumb)

| Parameter | Recommended Standard | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Time gap before revision** | Minimum 24–48 hours | Allows "cognitive defamiliarization" |
| **Number of self-revision passes** | At least 3 (one per level) | Each pass targets different cognitive layer |
| **Number of peer reviewers (informal)** | 2–3 minimum | Diverse perspectives catch diverse errors |
| **Plagiarism similarity index** | < 15% (excluding references) | Most journals' threshold |
| **Response letter length per major comment** | 200–400 words | Allows substantive defense |
| **Manuscript revision cycle (typical)** | 2–4 rounds before acceptance | Industry norm for Q1/Q2 journals |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For M.Tech / Ph.D. scholars:** Mastery of revision is essential for **Scopus/SCI publication**, which is mandatory for KTU Ph.D. completion.
- **For industry R&D engineers:** Revision skills translate to **technical report writing, patent drafting, and proposal writing**.
- **For project documentation:** The same SPACE framework applies to **final-year project reports, conference papers, and grant applications**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic "Revising a Paper" is a **process-oriented research methodology concept** (not a mathematical theorem), this section presents a **detailed algorithmic workflow** with structured analytical matrices instead of algebraic derivations. This aligns with KTU 2024 PEFTT755 Module 3 learning outcomes.

## 3.1 The 10-Step Algorithmic Revision Workflow

### Step 1: The "Cold Storage" Phase
- **Action:** Place the completed draft aside for **minimum 48 hours** (72 hours ideal).
- **Why:** Cognitive bias research (Kahneman's *Thinking, Fast and Slow*) shows that **psychological distance** improves self-criticism.
- **Output:** A mentally "fresh" version of the author.

### Step 2: Read the Entire Paper Aloud
- **Action:** Read every sentence out loud. Mark anywhere you hesitate, stumble, or feel confused.
- **Why:** The ear catches errors (run-on sentences, awkward phrasing) that the eye misses.
- **Output:** A "stumble-list" of problematic passages.

### Step 3: Verify the "Argument Spine"
- **Action:** Write a one-sentence summary of each section. Chain them: Section 1 → Section 2 → … → Conclusion.
- **Why:** Ensures logical flow; each section should *naturally lead to* the next.
- **Output:** A "section chain map" (see Section 4).

### Step 4: Apply the "So What?" Test to Every Claim
- **Action:** For every assertion, ask *"So what? Why does this matter?"*
- **Why:** Forces justification of every sentence; eliminates filler.
- **Output:** A revised claims inventory.

### Step 5: Check Internal Consistency
- **Action:** Cross-check that:
  - Numbers in abstract = numbers in results.
  - Figures cited in text = figures that exist.
  - Author names in citations = author names in reference list.
- **Why:** Consistency errors are the #1 reason for desk rejection.
- **Output:** A consistency audit report.

### Step 6: Run a Plagiarism Check
- **Action:** Use Turnitin/iThenticate. Target: **similarity index < 15%** (excluding references and quotes).
- **Why:** Most journals desk-reject papers with > 20% similarity.
- **Output:** Plagiarism report + revision of flagged passages.

### Step 7: Peer Feedback Round
- **Action:** Send to 2–3 trusted reviewers (supervisor, co-author, colleague). Provide a **revision focus sheet** (what kind of feedback you want).
- **Why:** Fresh eyes catch blind spots; author knows the text *too well*.
- **Output:** Consolidated peer feedback document.

### Step 8: Synthesize Feedback and Triage
- **Action:** Merge peer feedback + self-revision notes. Categorize each item as:
  - **Must-fix** (factual error, missing citation, methodological flaw)
  - **Should-fix** (clarity, structure)
  - **Nice-to-fix** (style preference)
- **Output:** Triage table (see Section 3.2).

### Step 9: Implement Revisions (v2, v3, …, vN)
- **Action:** Apply changes systematically. Maintain a **change log** documenting what was changed, where, and why.
- **Why:** Provides transparency and supports the response letter later.
- **Output:** Revised manuscript with change log.

### Step 10: Final Proofread + Format Check
- **Action:** Verify journal-specific formatting (font, line spacing, heading style, reference style). Do a final grammar pass.
- **Why:** Surface errors undermine credibility even when content is strong.
- **Output:** Submission-ready manuscript.

## 3.2 The Revision Triage Matrix (Analytical Tool)

| Feedback Item | Category | Priority | Action | Time Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| *"Sample size is too small"* | Must-fix | High | Add power analysis, acknowledge limitation | 3 hours |
| *"Paragraph 3 is unclear"* | Should-fix | Medium | Rewrite for clarity | 1 hour |
| *"Use active voice in conclusion"* | Nice-to-fix | Low | Apply throughout | 30 minutes |
| *"Reference [12] is missing DOI"* | Must-fix | High | Add DOI | 5 minutes |
| *"Consider adding a flow diagram"* | Should-fix | Medium | Design and insert figure | 4 hours |

## 3.3 The "Three Lenses" Self-Revision Matrix

| Lens | Perspective | Key Question | Tools/Techniques |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reviewer Lens** | "I am an expert who has never seen this paper" | Is the method sound? Are conclusions justified? | Use reviewer report template |
| **Reader Lens** | "I am a non-specialist intelligent reader" | Is the writing clear? Is the jargon explained? | Read aloud; ask a non-expert friend |
| **Editor Lens** | "I am the journal editor with 50 papers this month" | Does this paper fit the journal? Is it well-formatted? | Check journal aims/scope; verify template |

## 3.4 The Response Letter Template Structure

```
RESPONSE TO REVIEWER COMMENTS
Manuscript ID: [ID]
Manuscript Title: [Title]

Dear Editor and Reviewers,

We thank the editor and reviewers for their constructive feedback.
We have carefully addressed all comments. Below we provide a
point-by-point response. Reviewer comments are in [bold],
our responses in [regular] text, and revised text in [italics].

────────────────────────────
REVIEWER 1
────────────────────────────

Comment 1.1: [Reviewer's verbatim comment]
Response: Our response explaining the change, citing page/line numbers
in the revised manuscript.

Comment 1.2: ...

────────────────────────────
REVIEWER 2
────────────────────────────

Comment 2.1: ...

Sincerely,
[Author name, affiliation, contact]
```

## 3.5 Quantitative Self-Assessment: Revision Quality Score

A simple self-scoring formula for revision completeness (out of 100):

$$
R_{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{15} w_i \cdot c_i
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight of checklist item $i$ (sum of all $w_i = 100$)
- $c_i \in \{0, 1\}$ = completion status (1 = done, 0 = not done)

| Item | Weight $w_i$ | Completed $c_i$ | Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structure follows IMRaD | 10 | 1 | 10 |
| Abstract is structured | 8 | 1 | 8 |
| Research gap is explicit | 10 | 0 | 0 |
| Methods are reproducible | 10 | 1 | 10 |
| Results answer RQ | 8 | 1 | 8 |
| Discussion interprets | 10 | 1 | 10 |
| Limitations acknowledged | 6 | 1 | 6 |
| All figures/tables cited | 5 | 1 | 5 |
| References consistent | 6 | 1 | 6 |
| Plagiarism < 15% | 8 | 1 | 8 |
| Language proofread | 5 | 1 | 5 |
| Co-authors approved | 5 | 0 | 0 |
| Journal format applied | 5 | 1 | 5 |
| Response letter drafted | 4 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | **100** | — | **$R_{score} = 81$** |

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A score of $R_{score} \geq 90$ indicates the manuscript is submission-ready. A score of 70–89 indicates 1–2 more revision rounds needed. Below 70 suggests major revision is required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **"editing"** (surface-level) with **"revising"** (comprehensive). Marks are awarded for demonstrating understanding that revision is a *multi-level, iterative process*.
> - When asked to "describe the revision process," students must mention **all three levels** (substantive, structural, surface) to score full marks.
> - In response-letter questions, students often **forget to provide page/line numbers** for revisions. Always cite the revised location.
> - A common error: treating the **abstract as an afterthought**. In KTU valuation, the abstract carries ~5–7 marks and must follow structured format (Background/Aim/Methods/Results/Conclusion).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define "revising a paper" and distinguish it from "editing."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Revision** is the comprehensive, multi-level process of re-examining and improving a research manuscript's content, structure, and presentation to enhance its clarity, logical coherence, and scholarly impact.

**Editing**, in contrast, is the *surface-level* subset of revision focused only on grammar, spelling, punctuation, and word-level corrections.

| Aspect | Revising | Editing |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Whole document | Sentences/words |
| Focus | Ideas, structure, flow | Mechanics, syntax |
| Timing | Multiple passes | Final pass |
| KTU Weightage | 85% | 15% |

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: distinction table; 1 mark: scope/timing example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any THREE hierarchical levels of revision with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Substantive/Global Revision (1 mark):** E.g., Rewriting the introduction to clearly articulate the research gap.
2. **Structural/Organizational Revision (1 mark):** E.g., Reordering paragraphs to follow logical cause-effect sequence.
3. **Surface/Local Revision (1 mark):** E.g., Correcting subject-verb agreement and ensuring consistent verb tense.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Option**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **three hierarchical levels of revision** with suitable examples. Discuss why each level is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **post-review revision workflow** that an author must follow after receiving peer reviewer comments. Construct a sample response letter structure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

The three hierarchical levels of revision, as recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEFTT755, Module 3), are:

**1. Substantive / Global Revision (Big Picture) — 2.5 marks**

This is the *most critical* level, addressing the **intellectual content** of the paper. It evaluates:

- Is the research question clearly defined and significant?
- Is the literature review comprehensive and recent?
- Is the methodology appropriate and rigorous?
- Are the conclusions supported by the data?
- Does the paper make an original contribution?

*Example:* A researcher discovers during revision that the sample size in their survey study was too small for the regression analysis they performed. They must redesign the study with a larger sample, redo the analysis, and rewrite the results section.

*Why necessary:* Without substantive revision, the paper's intellectual foundation is weak, leading to rejection regardless of perfect grammar.

**2. Structural / Organizational Revision (Mid Picture) — 2.5 marks**

This level addresses the **architecture** of the paper — how ideas are sequenced and connected.

- Does the paper follow IMRaD structure?
- Are paragraphs ordered logically?
- Are transitions between sections smooth?
- Are figures/tables placed at appropriate points?

*Example:* Moving the "Limitations" subsection from the Conclusion to a separate paragraph in the Discussion to give it appropriate emphasis.

*Why necessary:* Even strong ideas fail if presented in a confusing order. Structural revision ensures the **reader can follow the argument effortlessly**.

**3. Surface / Local Revision (Micro Picture) — 2 marks**

This is the **final polishing** layer addressing language mechanics:

- Grammar, spelling, punctuation
- Word choice and sentence structure
- Citation format consistency
- Numerical accuracy and units

*Example:* Standardizing all in-text citations from (Smith, 2020) to (Smith 2020) per the journal's APA-style requirement.

*Why necessary:* Surface errors undermine the author's credibility and may distract reviewers from the paper's merits.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**The Post-Review Revision Workflow:**

**Step 1: Triage Comments (1.5 marks)**

Categorize each reviewer comment as:
- *Accept and implement* (factual errors, methodological gaps)
- *Clarify misunderstanding* (reviewer may have misread)
- *Respectfully disagree* (with strong evidence-based justification)

**Step 2: Construct Response Table (1.5 marks)**

Use a two-column format:

| Reviewer Comment | Author Response + Revision Location |
|---|---|
| "Sample size seems inadequate" | We agree. We have added a power analysis (Section 3.2, p. 8) and increased n from 50 to 120. Revised text: "..." |

**Step 3: Implement Revisions BEFORE Writing Response Letter (1.5 marks)**

Make all changes in the manuscript first. This ensures accurate page/line citations in the response letter.

**Step 4: Highlight Changes (1 mark)**

Use track changes, colored text, or a "marked" PDF version to help reviewers locate modifications quickly.

**Step 5: Write Cover Letter to Editor (1 mark)**

- Thank the editor and reviewers
- Summarize major changes
- Reaffirm the paper's suitability for the journal
- Confirm no new co-authors or major content added

**Step 6: Resubmit with Response Letter (0.5 marks)**

Upload the revised manuscript, marked-up version, and response letter through the journal portal.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip:** A well-crafted response letter can turn a "reject" into "accept with major revision." Treat reviewers as **collaborative critics**, not adversaries.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Discuss the **SPACE framework** for self-revision. Apply it to a hypothetical research paper titled *"Impact of IoT-Based Smart Irrigation on Water Conservation in Kerala."* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the **"Response Letter"** in detail. What are the key components and common pitfalls to avoid? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**The SPACE Framework (1 mark for introduction):**

SPACE is a mnemonic for five critical self-revision dimensions:

**S — Structure (1.5 marks)**

For the IoT irrigation paper, the author must verify that:
- The paper follows IMRaD.
- Sections are in logical order (Intro → Methods → Results → Discussion → Conclusion).
- The methodology section describes the IoT sensor setup, data collection, and analytical methods.

**P — Purpose (1.5 marks)**

- The research aim ("to evaluate water savings from IoT-based smart irrigation") must be stated explicitly in the abstract and introduction.
- The research gap (limited Kerala-specific studies) must be clear.

**A — Audience (1.5 marks)**

- Pitch the language for a multidisciplinary journal audience (agriculture + engineering).
- Define technical terms like "MQTT protocol" and "soil moisture sensors" for non-specialist readers.

**C — Clarity (1.5 marks)**

- Ensure sentences are concise and jargon-free.
- Use active voice where possible ("We measured soil moisture daily" rather than "Soil moisture was measured daily").

**E — Evidence (1.5 marks)**

- Every claim (e.g., "30% water savings") must be supported by data and statistical analysis.
- Cite Kerala-specific government water-use statistics.
- Acknowledge limitations (e.g., single-season study, limited geographic scope).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**The Response Letter: Definition and Purpose (1 mark)**

A **response letter** (also called a *rebuttal letter* or *response to reviewers*) is a formal document in which authors systematically address every comment raised by peer reviewers. It accompanies the revised manuscript upon resubmission.

**Key Components (4 marks):**

1. **Header Information**
   - Manuscript ID, title, journal name
   - Date of original submission and current resubmission
   - Author contact details

2. **Opening Paragraph**
   - Thank the editor and reviewers
   - State that all comments have been addressed
   - Summarize major changes

3. **Point-by-Point Responses**
   - Numbered responses matching reviewer comments
   - For each: restate comment → explain action → cite revised location (page/line)

4. **Closing Paragraph**
   - Reaffirm the paper's contribution
   - Express willingness for further revision
   - Provide contact information

5. **Attachments**
   - Revised manuscript (clean version)
   - Marked-up version (showing changes)
   - Updated supplementary materials (if any)

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid (2 marks):**

| Pitfall | Why It's Bad | Better Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Ignoring a reviewer comment | Signals disrespect | Address *every* comment, even if to disagree politely |
| Aggressive/defensive tone | Alienates reviewers | Use phrases like "We thank the reviewer for highlighting..." |
| No page/line references | Makes verification hard | Always cite revised location (e.g., "p. 7, lines 142–148") |
| Vague responses | Reviewer may reject again | Be specific: show *exactly* what changed and why |
| Adding new content not asked for | Confuses the revision scope | Only address what reviewers raised |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Revision = re-seeing the paper from a critical distance; NOT just grammar correction.
- ✅ **Three Levels:** Substantive (60%) → Structural (25%) → Surface (15%) — remember the weightage!
- ✅ **SPACE Framework:** Structure, Purpose, Audience, Clarity, Evidence — apply to every self-revision pass.
- ✅ **Cold Storage Rule:** Wait minimum 48 hours between drafting and revising for cognitive clarity.
- ✅ **Read Aloud Technique:** The ear catches what the eye misses.
- ✅ **Peer Feedback:** Minimum 2–3 trusted reviewers; provide a "focus sheet" of what feedback you want.
- ✅ **Plagiarism Threshold:** Target < 15% similarity (excluding references and direct quotes).
- ✅ **Response Letter Protocol:** Address EVERY comment; cite page/line; be respectful; use a table format.
- ✅ **Triage Categories:** Must-fix → Should-fix → Nice-to-fix — prioritize accordingly.
- ✅ **Quantitative Self-Assessment:** $R_{score} \geq 90$ = submission-ready.
- ✅ **The "So What?" Test:** Apply to every claim to ensure justification.
- ✅ **Internal Consistency:** Abstract numbers = Results numbers; cited figures must exist; reference list = in-text citations.
- ✅ **Common Confusion:** Editing ≠ Revising. Editing is *one level* (surface) of revision.
- ✅ **Murray's Three-Eye Principle:** Reader's eye → Editor's eye → Proofreader's eye.
- ✅ **Response Letter Tone:** Collaborative, not combative. Reviewers are allies, not enemies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked "Discuss the process of revising a paper," always structure your answer as: (1) Definition, (2) Three levels with examples, (3) Self-revision process, (4) Peer review response process, (5) Conclusion emphasizing iterative nature.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Responding to Peer Reviews** is the formal, structured, and scholarly process by which an author systematically addresses the comments, criticisms, suggestions, and queries raised by anonymous expert reviewers (and sometimes the handling editor) on a submitted research manuscript, conference paper, thesis chapter, or research proposal. The response is typically submitted as a *point-by-point rebuttal document* alongside a revised manuscript, demonstrating academic humility, scientific rigor, and intellectual accountability to the scholarly community.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** and as prescribed in the syllabus of *PEFTT755 – Research Methodology and Statistics*, this topic falls under the broader umbrella of **scientific communication ethics** and the *post-submission phase of the research publication cycle*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Master Chef and the Food Critic" Model**

Imagine you are a **master chef** who has just opened a new restaurant. You submit a signature dish to a panel of **food critics** before officially adding it to the menu. Three critics taste it and give you detailed feedback:

- **Critic A** (Substantive): "The flavors don't balance — the lime overwhelms the coconut."
- **Critic B** (Technical): "The plating violates classical gastronomy rules."
- **Critic C** (Editorial): "The dish name is confusing; consider renaming."

You now have a choice: get defensive and ignore them, or **carefully respond to each critic, accept valid points, defend the ones you can justify with evidence, and revise your dish accordingly**.

**Responding to peer reviews is exactly this process** — but for a research paper instead of a dish. The "dish" is your **manuscript**, the "critics" are **peer reviewers**, and the "rebuttal" is your **response letter**. Your goal is to convince the editor (the head critic) that you have taken the feedback seriously, acted on legitimate concerns, and respectfully defended any point where the reviewer's criticism is based on misunderstanding or incomplete information.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> The Research Methodology course (PEFTT755) explicitly trains B.Tech students in the **complete research lifecycle**. Since most final-year B.Tech projects culminate in **conference/journal publications** and **M.Tech/Ph.D. aspirations**, the ability to respond to peer reviews is a **mandatory graduate attribute** under NBA and NEP 2020 outcomes. The skill maps directly to:
> - **CO3**: Communicate effectively the research findings in written and oral formats.
> - **CO4**: Demonstrate ethical practices in research and publication.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning in the Peer-Review Context |
|---|---|
| **Reviewer** | Anonymized subject-matter expert who evaluates manuscript quality. |
| **Editor / Handling Editor** | The gatekeeper who synthesizes reviewer reports and decides the manuscript's fate. |
| **Rebuttal Letter** | The formal document where authors respond to each reviewer comment. |
| **Major Revision** | A decision requiring substantial changes (typically 30–60 days to revise). |
| **Minor Revision** | A decision requiring small clarifications or fixes (typically 14–21 days). |
| **Accept with Revisions** | A near-acceptance, conditional on addressing minor concerns. |
| **Reject & Resubmit** | A rejection with an invitation to substantially rework and resubmit. |
| **Rejection without Review (Desk Reject)** | Editor rejects without external review, often on scope/quality grounds. |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Backbone: The 5-Phase Response Model

Academic peer-review response is best understood through a **5-phase cognitive model**. Each phase has a clear purpose, inputs, and outputs.

### Phase 1 — Ingestion & Emotional Decoupling
- **What happens**: The author opens the reviewer comments.
- **The "Why"**: Reviewer comments are often *blunt, harsh, or even rude*. The author must emotionally detach before processing the content, because anger leads to defensive rebuttals that hurt acceptance chances.
- **Best Practice**: Wait **24–48 hours** after receiving reviews before drafting a response. Discuss with co-authors and supervisor.

### Phase 2 — Deconstruction & Classification
- **What happens**: Each comment is broken into a *discrete actionable unit* and classified.
- **The "Why"**: A single reviewer paragraph may contain 5 distinct points. If you respond to the paragraph as a whole, you will miss nuances.
- **Best Practice**: Number every comment. Build a spreadsheet/table.

### Phase 3 — Strategic Decision
- **What happens**: For each comment, the author decides ONE of three actions:
  1. **Accept fully** — Implement the suggestion.
  2. **Accept partially** — Implement a modified version.
  3. **Rebut politely** — Decline with evidence-based justification.
- **The "Why"**: Not all reviewer comments are correct. Reviewers are human and can be mistaken. However, **roughly 70–80% of comments are valid and should be accepted**.

### Phase 4 — Implementation in Manuscript
- **What happens**: Accepted changes are integrated into the manuscript with **track-changes** enabled.
- **The "Why"**: The editor must verify the changes against the response. Clear traceability is critical.

### Phase 5 — Documentation in Response Letter
- **What happens**: A formal letter is drafted with **red color for added text** and **blue for the reviewer's original quoted comment**.
- **The "Why"**: Visual contrast makes it effortless for the editor to verify.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Decision Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "3-R" Decision Rule**
> For every reviewer comment, ask: *Is the comment **R**ight, **R**epairable, or **R**ebuttable?*

| Comment Type | Author's Correct Action | Tone in Response | Mark in Manuscript |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Typographical / Grammatical** | Accept fully | "Thank you. We have corrected this." | Red strikethrough → new text |
| **Missing recent reference** | Accept fully | "We have added [Citation, Year]." | New reference in list |
| **Clarity / Wording issue** | Accept fully | "We have rephrased for clarity." | Tracked insertion |
| **Methodological concern** | Accept partially or rebut with evidence | "We agree this is a valid concern. We have performed an additional analysis (see Section X, Table Y)…" | New section/figure added |
| **Theoretical disagreement** | Rebut politely with citations | "We respectfully note that [Author, Year] supports our interpretation. However, we have softened the wording to acknowledge the alternative view." | Wording softened |
| **Out-of-scope suggestion** | Rebut with scope justification | "While we agree this is an interesting direction, it falls outside the scope of the present study, which focuses on… We will pursue this in future work." | No change |
| **Factually wrong comment** | Rebut firmly but politely with evidence | "We respectfully disagree. As shown in Equation (3) and Figure 4, the value is X, not Y. We suspect a typographical error in the reviewer's calculation." | No change, but add clarification |
| **Contradictory comment** (Reviewer contradicts own previous statement) | Politely point out the contradiction | "We note that the reviewer earlier suggested X, and the current comment appears to suggest Y. We have followed the more recent guidance (Y) for consistency." | Implementation of latest guidance |

## 2.3 The Anatomy of a Perfect Response Letter

A well-structured response letter follows this template:

1. **Opening Paragraph** — Thanks to the editor and reviewers, declares the manuscript is revised.
2. **Summary of Major Changes** — 3–5 bullets highlighting the most important revisions.
3. **Point-by-Point Response** — For each reviewer, repeat their numbered comments in **blue/italic** and respond in **black/regular**. Cite page/line numbers in the revised manuscript.
4. **Closing Paragraph** — Reaffirms the manuscript's contribution, requests reconsideration.
5. **Appendices** (optional) — Supplementary documents, datasets, or extended proofs.

## 2.4 The Six Cardinal Sins of Author Response

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid These — They Almost Guarantee Rejection**

1. **Being Disrespectful or Sarcastic** — "The reviewer clearly did not read Section 3."
2. **Ignoring Comments** — Not addressing even one reviewer comment is fatal.
3. **Vague Responses** — "We have revised the manuscript accordingly" (without specifying where).
4. **Blaming the Reviewer** — Shifting responsibility to the reviewer.
5. **Adding New Material Without Disclosure** — Introducing new claims/figures that weren't in the original.
6. **Exceeding the Deadline** — Late submissions often lead to administrative rejection.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **B.Tech final-year project** and subsequent **M.Tech/Ph.D. research**, peer-review response skills are used in:

- **IEEE / Springer / Elsevier journal submissions** for engineering research.
- **Conference paper rebuttals** at IEEE ICCCNT, ICASSP, ICRA, etc.
- **Thesis chapter evaluations** by doctoral committees.
- **Grant proposal revisions** for DST-SERB, AICTE, or KTU seed-money grants.
- **Industry R&D technical reports** that undergo internal peer review.

The **principles are universal** across disciplines — only the technical content differs.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The Comment Processing Pipeline

Since this topic is qualitative, the "derivation" is the **logical pipeline for processing a reviewer comment** from raw receipt to final manuscript integration. Below is the exhaustive analytical matrix.

## 3.1 The Comment Processing Algorithm (Verbal Derivation)

**Input:** A reviewer comment $C_i$ (where $i$ indexes the $i$-th discrete comment in the report).

**Output:** A revised manuscript $M'$ and a response letter entry $R_i$.

### Step 1 — Comment Extraction

The author reads the full reviewer report and decomposes it into discrete actionable units:

$$
C_{\text{raw}} \rightarrow \{C_1, C_2, C_3, \ldots, C_n\}
$$

Each $C_i$ should ideally address **one** scientific, technical, or editorial issue. If a paragraph contains multiple concerns, split it.

**Why this matters:** Bundled responses lose marks with editors because it is impossible to verify whether *each* concern was addressed.

### Step 2 — Comment Classification

Each $C_i$ is classified into one of the four canonical categories:

$$
\text{Type}(C_i) \in \{\text{Editorial}, \text{Clarification}, \text{Methodological}, \text{Theoretical}\}
$$

- **Editorial:** Grammar, typo, reference format.
- **Clarification:** "Please explain X in more detail."
- **Methodological:** "Why did you use Method A instead of Method B?"
- **Theoretical:** "Your conclusion contradicts Theory Z."

**Why classify?** Different types require different response strategies.

### Step 3 — Validity Assessment

The author evaluates the validity of $C_i$ on a 3-point scale:

$$
\text{Validity}(C_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if comment is fully correct} \\ 0.5 & \text{if comment is partially correct} \\ 0 & \text{if comment is factually incorrect} \end{cases}
$$

This scoring is *internal* to the author team; it is **not** shared with the editor. It helps decide action.

### Step 4 — Action Selection

Using the validity score, the action is selected:

$$
\text{Action}(C_i) = \begin{cases} \text{Accept} & \text{if } \text{Validity}(C_i) = 1 \\ \text{Accept-Partial} & \text{if } \text{Validity}(C_i) = 0.5 \\ \text{Rebut} & \text{if } \text{Validity}(C_i) = 0 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Manuscript Modification

If $\text{Action}(C_i) \in \{\text{Accept}, \text{Accept-Partial}\}$, the modification $M_i$ is generated and inserted:

$$
M_{i-1} \rightarrow M_i = M_{i-1} \oplus M_i
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes the tracked-changes insertion operation.

### Step 6 — Response Letter Generation

The response entry $R_i$ is drafted following the **Quote-Respond-Cite** micro-template:

$$
R_i = \underbrace{Q_i}_{\text{quoted comment in blue}} + \underbrace{A_i}_{\text{author response in black}} + \underbrace{L_i}_{\text{location citation in revised MS}}
$$

For example:

> **Reviewer 1, Comment 3 (blue):** *"The authors do not justify why they used ReLU activation over Swish."*
>
> **Response (black):** We thank the reviewer for this valuable observation. We have added a justification paragraph in Section 3.2 (lines 145–152) discussing the trade-offs between ReLU and Swish, and clarifying that our choice was driven by the limited training-data regime, where Swish offered no measurable benefit but increased computational cost by 18%.
>
> **Location:** Revised MS, Section 3.2, Lines 145–152.

### Step 7 — Cross-Check and Submission

The final check verifies:

1. Every $C_i$ for $i = 1 \ldots n$ has a corresponding $R_i$.
2. Every $R_i$ cites a verifiable location in $M'$ (page, line, equation, figure).
3. The response PDF and the tracked-changes PDF are internally consistent.

$$
\text{Submit iff } \forall i, \; R_i \neq \emptyset \;\wedge\; L_i \in M'
$$

If the condition fails, the author must re-draft the missing response before submission.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Processing Three Real-Style Comments

### Comment 1 (from Reviewer A)
> *"The literature review is too short. Add more recent references on transformer-based models."*

**Step 1 — Extraction:** $C_1$ = "Add recent references on transformers."
**Step 2 — Type:** Editorial / Coverage.
**Step 3 — Validity:** 1 (fully valid).
**Step 4 — Action:** Accept.
**Step 5 — Modification:** Added 6 references (2022–2025) and a new paragraph in Section 2.
**Step 6 — Response:** Quote the comment, state the number of references added, cite Section 2 line range.

### Comment 2 (from Reviewer B)
> *"Equation 7 is dimensionally inconsistent. The LHS has units of m/s, but the RHS has units of m/s²."*

**Step 1 — Extraction:** $C_2$ = "Eq. 7 dimensional inconsistency."
**Step 2 — Type:** Methodological / Technical.
**Step 3 — Validity:** 1 (mathematically verifiable).
**Step 4 — Action:** Accept.
**Step 5 — Modification:** Re-derived Eq. 7; corrected the unit mismatch; added a footnote.
**Step 6 — Response:** Thank the reviewer for catching the error, describe the correction, and provide the corrected equation in the response letter for clarity.

### Comment 3 (from Reviewer C)
> *"The authors should use a larger dataset, like ImageNet, to validate their findings."*

**Step 1 — Extraction:** $C_3$ = "Validate on ImageNet."
**Step 2 — Type:** Methodological.
**Step 3 — Validity:** 0.25 (suggestion is out of scope; our method targets embedded-device benchmarks where ImageNet is irrelevant).
**Step 4 — Action:** Rebut politely.
**Step 5 — Modification:** No manuscript change; added a brief scope-justification sentence in Section 5.1 to preempt similar concerns.
**Step 6 — Response:** Respectfully explain that the target deployment is edge devices (citing memory and latency constraints), so ImageNet is not representative. Offer to include ImageNet results in a follow-up paper if the editor/reviewer insists.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Complete Peer-Review Response Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Reviewer Report] --> B[Pause 24-48 Hours]
    B --> C[Emotional Decoupling and Team Discussion]
    C --> D[Deconstruct Report into Discrete Comments]
    D --> E[Build Comment Master Table]
    E --> F{Classify Each Comment}
    F -->|Editorial or Typo| G1[Category: Editorial]
    F -->|Clarification| G2[Category: Clarification]
    F -->|Methodological| G3[Category: Methodological]
    F -->|Theoretical| G4[Category: Theoretical]
    G1 --> H{Assess Validity}
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H -->|Fully Valid| I1[Action: Accept Fully]
    H -->|Partially Valid| I2[Action: Accept Partial]
    H -->|Invalid| I3[Action: Rebut with Evidence]
    I1 --> J1[Implement Change in Manuscript]
    I2 --> J2[Implement Modified Change]
    I3 --> J3[Draft Polite Rebuttal with Citations]
    J1 --> K[Update Tracked-Changes Manuscript]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> L{Scope Justification Needed?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Add Scope-Clarification Sentence]
    L -->|No| N[Proceed]
    M --> N
    K --> O[Draft Response Letter with Quote-Respond-Cite Format]
    N --> O
    O --> P{All Comments Addressed?}
    P -->|No| Q[Re-Draft Missing Responses]
    Q --> O
    P -->|Yes| R[Cross-Check Locations in Manuscript]
    R --> S[Final Proofreading]
    S --> T[Submit Revised Package to Editor]
    T --> U[Wait for Editor Decision]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The Three-Tone Language Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reviewer Comment Received] --> B{Comment Type?}
    B -->|Acceptable| C[Use Appreciation Tone]
    B -->|Defensible| D[Use Respectful-Defense Tone]
    B -->|Out-of-Scope| E[Use Scope-Limitation Tone]
    C --> F[Phrases: We thank the reviewer, We have corrected, We have added]
    D --> G[Phrases: We respectfully note, However our analysis shows, We have softened the wording]
    E --> H[Phrases: This falls outside the scope, We will explore this in future work, Our focus is on]
    F --> I[Editor Perception: Cooperative and Receptive]
    G --> J[Editor Perception: Scholarly and Evidence-Based]
    H --> K[Editor Perception: Focused and Self-Aware]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Section 1: Cover Greeting and Thank You] --> R2[Section 2: Summary of Major Revisions as Bulleted List]

    R2 --> R5[Section 3: Reviewer 3 Point-by-Point Response]

    R5 --> R6
```
