## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is an academic conference? Differentiate between oral and poster presentations.*

**Model Answer (Examiner Standard):**
An **academic conference** is a formal gathering of researchers where original research is presented, peer-reviewed, and discussed, typically organized by professional bodies like IEEE, ACM, or Springer.

| Aspect | Oral Presentation | Poster Presentation |
|---|---|---|
| Format | Time-bound talk on stage | Visual display on A0 board |
| Duration | 10–20 min + Q&A | 5–7 min per visitor |
| Audience | 50–200 simultaneous | 1–5 at a time |
| Interaction | Q&A at end | Continuous dialogue |
| Best for | Mature complete work | Preliminary work-in-progress |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differentiation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*List any six essential components of a well-designed research poster.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Title** — large, bold, visible from 5 m (72–96 pt).
2. **Authors and Affiliations** — with institute logo.
3. **Introduction** — problem statement and objective.
4. **Methodology** — block diagram or workflow.
5. **Results** — figures, tables, statistical plots.
6. **Conclusion** — 3–4 bullet takeaways.
7. **Acknowledgements and References** — optional but adds professionalism.
8. **QR Code** linking to paper/full video.

**(3 marks: 0.5 each for any six)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **IMRaD structure** of a conference paper in detail. Discuss each section's purpose and typical length. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

IMRaD stands for **Introduction, Methodology, Results and Discussion** — the universal scientific writing structure adapted for conferences.

**1. Introduction (≈ 1 page)**
- Establishes research context using the **CARS model**.
- States the **problem**, **gap**, and **contribution** in the last paragraph.
- Ends with a clear thesis: *"This paper proposes..."*

**2. Related Work / Literature Review (≈ 0.75 page)**
- Compares 8–12 prior studies in a comparative table.
- Identifies the niche your work fills.

**3. Methodology (≈ 1.25 pages)**
- Describes algorithms, datasets, tools, and experimental setup.
- Justifies every design choice with citations.

**4. Results and Discussion (≈ 1.5 pages)**
- Presents quantitative outcomes in tables and graphs.
- Performs **statistical validation** (t-test, ANOVA, confidence intervals).
- Discusses why results match/differ from literature.

**5. Conclusion and Future Scope (≈ 0.5 page)**
- 3–4 bullet summary.
- Limitations acknowledged.
- Future research directions listed.

**Examiner Tip:** Use figures and tables *instead of text* wherever possible — KTU awards marks for visual data representation.

**(7 marks: 1 for IMRaD expansion + 1 each for 5 sections + 1 for conclusion)**

---

**(b)** Describe the **peer review process** in academic conferences. Explain the roles of authors, reviewers, and program committee. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Peer review is the **quality-control mechanism** of academic publishing where domain experts evaluate submissions before acceptance.

**Stages:**

1. **Submission** — Author uploads manuscript + plagiarism report.
2. **Editorial Screening** — Editor checks scope, formatting, plagiarism ≤ 30%.
3. **Reviewer Assignment** — 2–3 experts from Program Committee.
4. **Double-Blind Review** — Identities hidden to ensure impartiality.
5. **Reviewer Verdicts** — Accept / Minor Revision / Major Revision / Reject.
6. **Meta-Review** — Program Committee Chair aggregates scores.
7. **Decision Notification** — Author informed via email.
8. **Rebuttal** — Author responds to reviewer comments.
9. **Camera-Ready** — Final version with copyright form.
10. **Publication** — Paper appears in proceedings with DOI.

**Roles:**

| Stakeholder | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Author** | Original research, accurate writing, ethical conduct |
| **Reviewer** | Critical evaluation, constructive feedback, confidentiality |
| **Program Committee** | Reviewer assignment, decision aggregation, conflict resolution |
| **Session Chair** | Moderates Q&A, manages time, ensures smooth conduct |

**(7 marks: 2 for stages + 2 for roles + 1 for double-blind + 2 for verdict categories)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is a **poster presentation**? Discuss the **design principles, layout, typography, and color theory** for an effective research poster. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **poster presentation** is a visual summarization of research displayed on an A0 board (841 × 1189 mm) during a conference session, where the author verbally explains the work to visitors.

**Design Principles:**

1. **Visual Hierarchy** — Title at top (largest), authors next, sections below.
2. **Three-Column Layout** — Most readable for A0 portrait orientation.
3. **White Space** — At least 30% of the poster must be empty; avoid clutter.
4. **Figure Dominance** — 60–70% visuals, 30–40% text.
5. **Logical Flow** — Numbered sections guiding eye top-left → bottom-right.

**Typography:**

| Element | Font Size |
|---|---|
| Title | **72–96 pt** |
| Authors | **36–48 pt** |
| Section headings | **36–44 pt** |
| Body text | **24–32 pt** |
| Captions | **18–20 pt** |

Use **sans-serif fonts** (Arial, Calibri, Helvetica) for screen readability.

**Color Theory:**
- Use **2–3 complementary colors maximum**.
- High contrast (dark text on light background).
- Avoid red-green combinations (color-blind unfriendly).
- Use color **purposefully** to highlight key results.

**(7 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for design principles + 2 for typography + 2 for color theory)**

---

**(b)** Write the **abstract** of your B.Tech project in the CARS model format. Justify each sentence using the three moves. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Sample — Replace with student's actual project):**

**Title:** *Lightweight Deep Learning Model for Plant Disease Detection on Edge Devices*

**Abstract:**

> *Deep learning has significantly advanced automated plant disease detection, achieving accuracies exceeding 95% on benchmark datasets [1], [2]. **However**, most existing models rely on cloud computing, limiting their deployment in rural agricultural settings with intermittent connectivity [3]. **Furthermore**, lightweight architectures such as MobileNet have been underexplored for Indian crop species, where disease diversity is high [4]. **This paper proposes** a pruned MobileNet-V3 architecture optimized for Raspberry Pi deployment, achieving 93.7% accuracy on a custom dataset of 12,000 tomato leaf images while reducing model size by 68%. **The proposed** system enables real-time, offline diagnosis, offering a scalable solution for smart agriculture. **Future work** will extend the model to multi-crop classification.*

**CARS Move Analysis:**

| Sentence | CARS Move | Marks Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Sentence 1 | Move 1 — Establishing Territory | Highlights field importance |
| Sentence 2 | Move 2a — Establishing Niche (counter-claiming) | Identifies limitation |
| Sentence 3 | Move 2b — Establishing Niche (gap-indicating) | Pinpoints research gap |
| Sentence 4 | Move 3 — Occupying Niche (announcing) | States contribution |
| Sentence 5 | Move 3 (supporting) | Provides quantitative evidence |
| Sentence 6 | Move 3 (signaling) | Indicates practical impact |
| Sentence 7 | Move 3 (projecting) | Suggests future work |

**(7 marks: 2 for abstract + 3 for CARS justification table + 2 for clarity)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing conference with journal** — Conferences are *faster, shorter, and presentation-driven*; journals are *longer, slower, and archival*.
> 2. **Missing the "gap" in CARS** — An abstract without a clearly stated research gap scores zero under Move 2.
> 3. **Poster font sizes too small** — Students write body text at **14–16 pt** instead of the mandatory **≥ 24 pt** for A0 readability.
> 4. **No statistical validation** — Conference papers without t-tests, confidence intervals, or cross-validation lose marks under "Experimental Validation" criterion (15% weight).
> 5. **Plagiarism ignorance** — Submitting without Turnitin check is a **hard reject** at IEEE venues.
> 6. **Exceeding page limit** — IEEE charges per extra page; student budgets must account for this.
> 7. **Ignoring the chair's time discipline** — Talks running over time are *cut off mid-sentence* by the session chair — a career embarrassment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Conference & Poster Presentations**

**Conference Fundamentals**
- Conference = formal academic gathering for research dissemination.
- Types: National, International, Workshop, Symposium, Seminar.
- Tier-1 (SCI/Scopus) > Tier-2 (Scopus) > Tier-3 (Peer-reviewed) > Tier-4 (Non-peer-reviewed).
- KTU prefers **Scopus/SCI-indexed** publications for academic credit.

**Conference Paper Structure**
- Follows **IMRaD + CARS** model.
- Typical length: **6 pages IEEE / Springer format**.
- Abstract: **150–250 words**, must be self-contained.
- Keywords: **4–6 terms**.
- References: **15–25 entries**, mostly from last 5 years.
- Plagiarism threshold: **≤ 30%** (excluding references).

**Peer Review Process**
- **Double-blind** review standard at top venues.
- Verdicts: Accept / Minor Revision / Major Revision / Reject.
- Camera-ready deadline is **strict** — no extensions.
- DOI assigned upon publication.

**Oral Presentation**
- Duration: **10–15 min + 5 min Q&A**.
- Time allocation (Rule of Thirds): Intro 15%, Method 30%, Results 35%, Conclusion 10%, Backup 10%.
- Rehearse **≥ 5 times** before delivery.
- Anticipate **≥ 10 questions** with crisp answers.
- Average speaking rate: **100–130 words/min** for technical talks.

**Poster Presentation**
- Size: **A0 portrait (841 × 1189 mm)**.
- Title font: **72–96 pt**; Body font: **≥ 24 pt**.
- Layout: **3-column** standard.
- Content: **30% text, 70% visuals** — the **5-7-9 rule**.
- Maximum words: **500–800**.
- Always include a **QR code** linking to paper/video.
- Author stands beside poster; explains for **5–7 min per visitor**.

**Key Differences — Oral vs Poster**
- Oral = formal, scheduled, large audience.
- Poster = informal, continuous, small-group interaction.

**Common Mistakes to Avoid**
- ❌ Submitting without plagiarism check.
- ❌ Ignoring conference template.
- ❌ Exceeding page limit.
- ❌ Poster with walls of text.
- ❌ Reading slides verbatim during oral talk.
- ❌ No statistical validation of results.

**KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment**
- CO mapping: This topic aligns with **CO3 — Communication of Research** (communicate research effectively in written and oral forms).
- Bloom's Level: **Apply + Analyze + Create** (L3–L5).
- Assessment weightage in PEFTT755 Module 3: typically **15–20%** of ESE.

topics.title: Language Aspects of Report Writing – Verb, Tense, and Voice in Scientific Writing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Language Aspects of Report Writing – Verb, Tense, and Voice in Scientific Writing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Language Aspects of Report Writing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Scientific Writing** is a highly structured, precise, and impersonal form of technical communication in which researchers convey experimental procedures, observations, results, and conclusions using standardized linguistic conventions. The three foundational linguistic pillars that govern scientific writing are:

- **Verb Selection**: The choice of *action words* that denote what was performed, observed, or established.
- **Tense Consistency**: The grammatical time-reference (Past, Present, Future) selected in alignment with the section of the research report.
- **Voice (Active vs. Passive)**: Whether the *subject performs* the action (Active Voice) or *receives* the action (Passive Voice).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Scientific writing demands **clarity, brevity, precision, and objectivity**. The interplay of verb, tense, and voice determines whether a research report is rated as *publishable* or *rejected* by reviewers and examiners alike.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Laboratory Camera"

Imagine you are a **detached CCTV camera** in a chemistry lab:

- **Active Voice** = The camera zooms in on the *scientist* — "Dr. Rao measured the pH." (The actor is highlighted.)
- **Passive Voice** = The camera pans to the *experiment* — "The pH was measured." (The actor fades; the work shines.)
- **Tense** = The camera's timestamp. Past tense = *"What happened yesterday."* Present tense = *"What is universally true."* Future tense = *"What we propose to do."*

In a research report, the *experiment* is the hero — not the researcher — so the **"camera angle"** (voice and tense) must consistently favour the work.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners in PEFTT755 frequently test whether students can:
> 1. Identify the **correct tense** for a given section (Abstract, Methods, Results, Discussion).
> 2. Convert sentences between **active and passive voice**.
> 3. Spot **weak verb choices** (e.g., *make, do, get, have*) and replace them with **strong, discipline-specific verbs** (*determine, quantify, elucidate*).

The **three pillars** function as the linguistic DNA of every thesis, journal manuscript, and conference paper.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Scientific Language

### 2.1.1 Pillar I — Verb Selection in Scientific Writing

Scientific verbs are broadly classified into three families:

| Verb Family | Purpose | Examples | KTU Strength Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reporting Verbs** | To report what previous researchers did | *stated, reported, demonstrated, argued, found* | ★★★★ |
| **Hedging Verbs** | To express caution / probability | *suggest, appear, seem, may, indicate* | ★★★★ |
| **Strong Action Verbs** | To describe own experimental actions | *measured, calculated, synthesized, validated, computed* | ★★★★★ |
| **Stative/Linking Verbs** | To express state, not action | *is, are, consists, contains, comprises* | ★★★ |
| **Weak Verbs (Avoid)** | Imprecise, vague | *make, do, get, have, give, take* | ★ (Poor) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "The 5 Sins of Verb Selection"**
> **M**ake, **D**o, **G**et, **H**ave, **G**ive → These five verbs account for **over 40%** of weak sentences rejected by IEEE/Elsevier reviewers.

### 2.1.2 Pillar II — Tense Usage Across Report Sections

Different sections of a research report demand **different tenses** based on what they describe:

| Report Section | Recommended Tense | Justification | Example Sentence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstract (Methods)** | Past Simple | Action completed before writing | *"We **fabricated** the composite using hand lay-up."* |
| **Abstract (Results)** | Past Simple | Findings obtained | *"The yield strength **increased** by 22%."* |
| **Introduction** | Present Simple (for established facts); Past Simple (for prior studies) | Universal truths vs. completed prior work | *"Machine learning **is** widely used in fault diagnosis." / Smith et al. (2022) **reported**..."* |
| **Literature Review** | Past Simple / Present Perfect | Citing prior work | *"Several authors **have investigated**..."* |
| **Methodology** | Past Simple | Procedure you *did* | *"Samples **were heated** at 80 °C."* |
| **Results** | Past Simple | Specific findings obtained | *"Figure 3 **shows** the stress–strain curve."* |
| **Discussion** | Past Simple + Present (for interpretation) | Data vs. its meaning | *"The values **decreased** (past); This **suggests** (present) brittleness."* |
| **Conclusion** | Past Simple + Present (for generalization) | What was found vs. what holds | *"The model **achieved** 96% accuracy."* |
| **Future Work / Scope** | Future Simple / Modal (will/may) | Proposed actions | *"Future studies **will explore**..."* |

### 2.1.3 Pillar III — Active vs. Passive Voice

The transformation rule is governed by a fundamental grammatical identity:

$$\text{Active Voice: } \quad S + V + O$$

$$\text{Passive Voice: } \quad O + \text{be} + V_{\text{past participle}} + \text{(by } S\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Transformation Table**

| Aspect | Active Voice | Passive Voice |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject** | Performs the action | Receives the action |
| **Typical Use in Science** | Modern trend (clarity) | Traditional (objectivity) |
| **Focus** | The doer (researcher) | The deed (experiment) |
| **Strength** | Concise, direct, dynamic | Removes ego, impersonal |
| **Weakness** | Can sound self-promotional | Can be wordy, vague agent |
| **Best Section** | Abstract, Conclusions | Methods, Results |
| **Example** | *"We analysed 200 samples."* | *"200 samples were analysed."* |

### 2.2 The "3-C Principle" of Scientific Writing

KTU examiners reward language that is:

1. **Clear** — Reader grasps idea in one read.
2. **Concise** — No filler words (*very, really, basically, in order to*).
3. **Correct** — Grammar, tense, voice aligned with convention.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Verb-Tense-Voice Combinations)

| Section | Voice | Dominant Tense | Sample Verb Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Abstract — Methods | Passive (trad.) / Active (modern) | Past Simple | *fabricated, analysed, measured* |
| Abstract — Results | Either | Past Simple | *demonstrated, revealed, exhibited* |
| Introduction — Facts | Active | Present Simple | *constitute, comprise, represent* |
| Introduction — Prior Work | Active | Past Simple / Present Perfect | *reported, have shown, established* |
| Methodology | Passive (preferred) | Past Simple | *were prepared, were calibrated, was recorded* |
| Results | Passive / Active | Past Simple | *exhibited, showed, were plotted* |
| Discussion | Active | Past + Present | *suggest, imply, may indicate, confirms* |
| Conclusion | Active | Past + Present | *achieved, concludes, validates* |
| Future Scope | Active | Future / Modal | *will investigate, may extend* |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **IEEE / Springer / Elsevier** journal submissions, reviewers explicitly flag:

- **Inconsistent tense shifts** between Method and Results.
- **Excessive passive voice** in Introduction (modern style discourages this).
- **Weak verb overuse** like *"made a measurement"* instead of *"measured"*.

Mastering these three pillars therefore directly translates to **higher manuscript acceptance rates** and **higher KTU exam scores**.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Transformations & Analytical Matrix

This module is **linguistic**, not algebraic. Hence, instead of mathematical derivations, we present the **canonical transformations** between tense and voice — the equivalent of derivations in scientific writing.

## 3.1 Voice Transformation — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Method

### Worked Example 1: Active → Passive

**Original (Active):** "Dr. Priya calculated the stress distribution using ANSYS."

**Step 1 — Identify the grammatical triplet:**
- Subject ($S$) = Dr. Priya
- Verb ($V$) = calculated
- Object ($O$) = the stress distribution
- Agent of instrument = using ANSYS

**Step 2 — Promote Object to Subject position:**
The stress distribution ...

**Step 3 — Insert the auxiliary "be" in correct tense:**
was (past simple of *be*)

**Step 4 — Use the Past Participle (V3) of the main verb:**
calculated → calculated (regular verb, V3 = V2)

**Step 5 — Append the agent (optional; often omitted):**
by Dr. Priya

**Step 6 — Final Passive Sentence:**

$$\text{"The stress distribution was calculated using ANSYS."}$$

### Worked Example 2: Passive → Active

**Original (Passive):** "A novel control algorithm has been proposed by the authors."

**Step 1 — Decompose the passive structure:**
- Auxiliary = has been
- Main verb = proposed (V3)
- Subject = A novel control algorithm
- Agent = by the authors

**Step 2 — Promote the agent to subject position:**
The authors ...

**Step 3 — Replace auxiliary + V3 with appropriate active tense:**
have proposed (Present Perfect Active)

**Step 4 — Bring original subject as object:**
a novel control algorithm

**Step 5 — Final Active Sentence:**

$$\text{"The authors have proposed a novel control algorithm."}$$

## 3.2 Tense Transformation Matrix

The base form changes systematically across tenses:

| Tense | Auxiliary | Main Verb Form | Example (Verb: *measure*) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Present Simple | — (no aux.) | base form (V1) | measures / measure |
| Past Simple | — (no aux.) | past form (V2) | measured |
| Future Simple | will / shall | base form (V1) | will measure |
| Present Perfect | have / has | past participle (V3) | have measured |
| Past Perfect | had | past participle (V3) | had measured |
| Present Continuous | am / is / are | present participle (V-ing) | is measuring |
| Past Continuous | was / were | present participle (V-ing) | was measuring |

### Tense Conversion Example — Same Sentence, Six Tenses

**Base meaning:** *We analyse the data.*

| Tense | Transformed Sentence |
|---|---|
| Present Simple | "We analyse the data." |
| Past Simple | "We analysed the data." |
| Future Simple | "We will analyse the data." |
| Present Perfect | "We have analysed the data." |
| Past Perfect | "We had analysed the data before the deadline." |
| Present Continuous | "We are analysing the data." |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Right Pillar for Each Section

| KTU Section | Look for these *signals* in the question | Use this Voice | Use this Tense | Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Abstract — Own Work | "describe what you did" | Passive / Active | Past Simple | Future tense |
| Literature Review | "summarize previous work" | Active | Past / Present Perfect | First-person claims |
| Hypothesis Statement | "what you predict" | Active | Present / Future | Past Simple |
| Experimental Setup | "the procedure followed" | Passive (trad.) | Past Simple | Present Simple |
| Numerical Findings | "what the data shows" | Either | Past Simple | Modal hedging |
| Interpretation | "what it means" | Active | Present Simple | Passive (cluttered) |
| Recommendations | "what should be done" | Active | Modal (should / may) | Past tense |

## 3.4 Verb-Strengthening Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

When a student writes a weak verb, the following 5-step algorithm upgrades it:

**Step 1 — Identify the weak verb cluster:**
*"made an investigation of"* → Weak.

**Step 2 — Identify the noun it carries:**
investigation → research / examination.

**Step 3 — Find a strong verb that maps to the noun:**
investigate, examine, probe, scrutinize, explore.

**Step 4 — Restructure using SVO:**

$$\text{"We made an investigation of the corrosion rate."} \longrightarrow \text{"We investigated the corrosion rate."}$$

**Step 5 — Verify conciseness and clarity:**
Word count reduced from 8 to 4 in the verb phrase. ✓

### Ten Canonical Verb-Strengthening Conversions

| # | Weak Phrase | Strong Verb | Improved Sentence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | *made a measurement of* | measured | "We measured the temperature." |
| 2 | *carried out an analysis of* | analysed | "The samples were analysed." |
| 3 | *performed an evaluation of* | evaluated | "The model was evaluated." |
| 4 | *gave a description of* | described | "The phenomenon is described in §3." |
| 5 | *made an assumption that* | assumed | "We assumed linear elasticity." |
| 6 | *did an examination of* | examined | "The data were examined." |
| 7 | *took into consideration* | considered | "Boundary effects were considered." |
| 8 | *made use of* | used | "MATLAB was used for simulation." |
| 9 | *came to the conclusion that* | concluded | "We concluded that the hypothesis holds." |
| 10 | *put forward the suggestion that* | suggested | "The authors suggested further work." |

## 3.5 Hedging — The Politeness of Science

Hedging softens absolute claims, which is essential for academic credibility.

| Hedging Verb | Certainty Level | Example |
|---|---|---|
| *proves, demonstrates, establishes* | Very High (95–100%) | "This proves the theorem." |
| *shows, indicates, reveals* | High (75–95%) | "The graph shows a linear trend." |
| *suggests, implies, points to* | Moderate (50–75%) | "The data suggests fatigue failure." |
| *appears, seems, may, might* | Low (25–50%) | "The result may indicate corrosion." |
| *speculates, hypothesizes* | Very Low (<25%) | "We speculate that..." |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners reward **calibrated hedging** — using the right certainty verb to match the strength of evidence.


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Choosing the Right Tense, Voice, and Verb

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Identify Section of Report"] --> nodeB{"Which Section?"}
    nodeB -->|Abstract - Methods| nodeC["Past Simple Tense"]
    nodeB -->|Abstract - Results| nodeD["Past Simple Tense"]
    nodeB -->|Introduction - Facts| nodeE["Present Simple Tense"]
    nodeB -->|Introduction - Prior Work| nodeF["Past Simple or Present Perfect"]
    nodeB -->|Methodology| nodeG["Past Simple Tense + Passive Voice"]
    nodeB -->|Results| nodeH["Past Simple + Passive or Active"]
    nodeB -->|Discussion| nodeI["Past + Present Tense Mix"]
    nodeB -->|Conclusion| nodeJ["Past Simple + Present Simple"]
    nodeB -->|Future Scope| nodeK["Future Simple / Modal Verbs"]
    nodeC --> nodeL["Use Strong Action Verbs: measured, calculated"]
    nodeD --> nodeM["Use Reporting Verbs: revealed, exhibited"]
    nodeE --> nodeN["Use Stative Verbs: constitutes, represents"]
    nodeF --> nodeO["Use Reporting Verbs: reported, established"]
    nodeG --> nodeP["Use Past Participles: was measured, were fabricated"]
    nodeH --> nodeQ["Use Reporting Verbs: showed, demonstrated"]
    nodeI --> nodeR["Use Hedging Verbs: suggests, implies"]
    nodeJ --> nodeS["Use Strong Verbs: achieved, validates"]
    nodeK --> nodeT["Use Modal Verbs: will explore, may extend"]
```

## 4.2 Verb-Strengthening Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeU["Weak Verb Phrase Detected"] --> nodeV{"Identify the Noun Embedded"}
    nodeV -->|measurement| nodeW["Upgrade to: measured"]
    nodeV -->|investigation| nodeX["Upgrade to: investigated"]
    nodeV -->|analysis| nodeY["Upgrade to: analysed"]
    nodeV -->|description| nodeZ["Upgrade to: described"]
    nodeV -->|evaluation| nodeAA["Upgrade to: evaluated"]
    nodeW --> nodeAB["Apply SVO Structure"]
    nodeX --> nodeAB
    nodeY --> nodeAB
    nodeZ --> nodeAB
    nodeAA --> nodeAB
    nodeAB --> nodeAC["Verify Clarity and Conciseness"]
```

## 4.3 Voice Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeAD["Choose Voice for Sentence"] --> nodeAE{"Is the Doer Important?"}
    nodeAE -->|Yes| nodeAF["Use ACTIVE VOICE"]
    nodeAE -->|No, Actor Unknown| nodeAG["Use PASSIVE VOICE without agent"]
    nodeAE -->|No, but Actor Known| nodeAH["Use PASSIVE VOICE with 'by' phrase"]
    nodeAF --> nodeAI["Section: Abstract, Introduction, Discussion"]
    nodeAG --> nodeAJ["Section: Methods, Results"]
    nodeAH --> nodeAK["Section: Methods, Results"]
    nodeAI --> nodeAL["Example: We measured the voltage"]
    nodeAJ --> nodeAM["Example: The voltage was measured"]
    nodeAK --> nodeAN["Example: The voltage was measured by the sensor"]
```

## 4.4 Tense-Section Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeAO["Report Section"] --> nodeAP["Tense Required"]
    nodeAP --> nodeAQ["Abstract: Past"]
    nodeAP --> nodeAR["Introduction: Present + Past"]
    nodeAP --> nodeAS["Methods: Past"]
    nodeAP --> nodeAT["Results: Past"]
    nodeAP --> nodeAU["Discussion: Past + Present"]
    nodeAP --> nodeAV["Conclusion: Past + Present"]
    nodeAP --> nodeAW["Future Work: Future / Modal"]
    nodeAQ --> nodeAX["Example: We synthesised the composite"]
    nodeAR --> nodeAY["Example: X is used; Y reported"]
    nodeAS --> nodeAZ["Example: The sample was heated"]
    nodeAT --> nodeBA["Example: The curve shows linearity"]
    nodeAU --> nodeBB["Example: The data suggests brittleness"]
    nodeAV --> nodeBC["Example: The model achieves 96% accuracy"]
    nodeAW --> nodeBD["Example: We will investigate further"]
```

## 4.5 Certainty-Calibrated Hedging Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBE["Certainty Level"] --> nodeBF["100% Certain: proves, establishes, demonstrates"]
    nodeBE --> nodeBG["80% Certain: shows, reveals, confirms"]
    nodeBE --> nodeBH["60% Certain: indicates, suggests, implies"]
    nodeBE --> nodeBI["40% Certain: appears, seems, may, might"]
    nodeBE --> nodeBJ["20% Certain: speculates, hypothesizes, postulates"]
    nodeBF --> nodeBK["Use for established theorems"]
    nodeBG --> nodeBL["Use for direct experimental findings"]
    nodeBH --> nodeBM["Use for novel interpretations"]
    nodeBI --> nodeBN["Use for preliminary or weak evidence"]
    nodeBJ --> nodeBO["Use for future hypotheses only"]
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define scientific writing. Mention the three linguistic pillars that govern effective report writing.**

**Model Answer:**

Scientific writing is a structured, impersonal form of technical communication used by researchers to convey experimental procedures, results, and conclusions with clarity, precision, and brevity.

The three linguistic pillars are:
1. **Verb Selection** — Choosing strong, precise action words (e.g., *measured, calculated, determined*) instead of weak generic verbs (*made, did, got*).
2. **Tense Consistency** — Aligning the grammatical time-reference with the report section (e.g., Past Simple for Methods, Present Simple for established facts).
3. **Voice (Active vs. Passive)** — Choosing whether the subject performs the action (Active) or receives it (Passive), depending on whether the actor or the action is the focus.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Identify the tense and voice used in the following sentence. Rewrite it in the opposite voice.**

*"The researchers synthesized the nanocomposite using the sol-gel method."*

**Model Answer:**

- **Tense:** Past Simple
- **Voice:** Active Voice
- **Subject:** The researchers
- **Verb:** synthesized
- **Object:** the nanocomposite

**Conversion to Passive Voice (step-by-step):**

Step 1 — Move the object to subject position: *The nanocomposite...*
Step 2 — Insert auxiliary "was": *The nanocomposite was...*
Step 3 — Add past participle of "synthesize": *synthesized*
Step 4 — Optional agent phrase: *...by the researchers*
Step 5 — Method retained: *...using the sol-gel method*

**Final Passive Sentence:**

$$\text{"The nanocomposite was synthesized using the sol-gel method by the researchers."}$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Module 4 — Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the rules for **tense selection** across different sections of a research report. Provide one example sentence for each of the following sections: Abstract (Methods), Introduction, Methodology, Results, Discussion, and Future Scope. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **active and passive voice** in scientific writing. Provide the transformation rules, list two advantages and two disadvantages of each, and convert the following four sentences from active to passive voice: **(7 Marks)**

1. We calibrated the sensors before each test.
2. The authors proposed a novel fault-detection algorithm.
3. Dr. Menon analysed the fatigue data using MATLAB.
4. The committee approved the research proposal.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Tense Selection (7 Marks)

| Section | Recommended Tense | Justification | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Abstract (Methods) | Past Simple | Action completed before writing the abstract | *"We **fabricated** the specimen using CNC machining."* |
| Introduction | Present Simple (for facts); Past Simple (for prior studies) | Universal truths require present tense; prior completed work uses past tense | *"Neural networks **are** widely used in diagnostics." / Sharma (2021) **reported**..."* |
| Methodology | Past Simple | Describes the procedure the author actually performed | *"The composite **was cured** at 120 °C for 2 hours."* |
| Results | Past Simple | Reports specific findings obtained | *"Figure 4 **shows** the load–displacement curve."* |
| Discussion | Past + Present Simple mix | Past for data; Present for interpretation | *"The strength **increased** by 18% (past); This **suggests** (present) improved bonding."* |
| Future Scope | Future Simple / Modal | Proposed upcoming actions | *"Future studies **will investigate** the effect of nano-coatings."* |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Consistency in tense across each section enhances readability and demonstrates linguistic maturity. Tense shifts should only occur when transitioning between *what was done* (past) and *what it means* (present).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Active vs. Passive Voice (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**

- **Active Voice:** The subject performs the action. Structure: $S + V + O$.
- **Passive Voice:** The subject receives the action. Structure: $O + \text{be} + V_{pp} + \text{(by } S\text{)}$.

**Transformation Rules (1 mark):**

1. Move the object to the subject position.
2. Insert the correct form of *be* matching the original tense.
3. Use the past participle (V3) of the main verb.
4. Add *by + agent* (optional).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Voice | Passive Voice |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | The doer (researcher) | The deed (experiment/work) |
| Strength | Concise, dynamic | Impersonal, objective |
| Weakness | Can sound self-promotional | Can be wordy, hides agent |
| Best Section | Abstract, Discussion, Conclusion | Methods, Results |

**Conversions (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| # | Original (Active) | Converted (Passive) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | "We calibrated the sensors before each test." | "The sensors were calibrated before each test." |
| 2 | "The authors proposed a novel fault-detection algorithm." | "A novel fault-detection algorithm was proposed by the authors." |
| 3 | "Dr. Menon analysed the fatigue data using MATLAB." | "The fatigue data was analysed using MATLAB by Dr. Menon." |
| 4 | "The committee approved the research proposal." | "The research proposal was approved by the committee." |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the importance of **verb selection** in scientific writing. Identify and correct ten weak verb phrases commonly found in research reports, replacing each with a strong, precise scientific verb. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **hedging in scientific writing**. Construct a certainty-calibrated ladder of hedging verbs (from highest to lowest certainty) and provide one example sentence for each level. Justify why hedging is essential for academic credibility. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Verb Selection (7 Marks)

**Importance of Verb Selection (2 marks):**

Verb selection is the cornerstone of scientific writing precision. Strong verbs:
1. **Reduce word count** (concision).
2. **Enhance clarity** (single-word statements).
3. **Convey exact scientific meaning** (e.g., *synthesized* vs. *made*).
4. **Improve manuscript acceptance rates** in peer-reviewed journals.

Weak verbs like *made, did, got, gave, took* dilute the scientific tone and are flagged by reviewers.

**Ten Corrected Verb Phrases (5 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Weak Phrase | Strong Verb | Improved Sentence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | made a measurement of | measured | "We measured the displacement." |
| 2 | carried out an analysis of | analysed | "The samples were analysed using XRD." |
| 3 | performed an evaluation of | evaluated | "The algorithm was evaluated on 500 test cases." |
| 4 | gave a description of | described | "The microstructure is described in §3.2." |
| 5 | made an assumption that | assumed | "We assumed isotropic behaviour." |
| 6 | did an examination of | examined | "The fracture surface was examined under SEM." |
| 7 | took into consideration | considered | "Thermal effects were considered in the model." |
| 8 | made use of | used | "Python was used for data processing." |
| 9 | came to the conclusion that | concluded | "We concluded that the hypothesis holds." |
| 10 | put forward the suggestion that | suggested | "The authors suggested further validation." |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Hedging (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**

Hedging is the linguistic strategy of using carefully chosen verbs and modal auxiliaries to **calibrate the certainty** of a claim relative to the strength of supporting evidence. It is the *epistemic politeness* of science.

**Certainty Ladder (4 marks — 0.5 per level + 0.5 for example each):**

| Certainty | Hedging Verb | Example Sentence |
|---|---|---|
| Very High (95–100%) | *proves, establishes, demonstrates* | "This experiment proves the proposed theorem." |
| High (75–95%) | *shows, reveals, confirms* | "Figure 3 confirms the linear relationship." |
| Moderate (50–75%) | *suggests, implies, indicates* | "The trend suggests ductile failure." |
| Low (25–50%) | *appears, seems, may, might* | "The result may indicate corrosion." |
| Very Low (<25%) | *speculates, hypothesizes, postulates* | "We speculate that nano-coating could improve wear resistance." |

**Justification — Why Hedging Matters (2 marks):**

1. **Academic Honesty:** Science rarely offers 100% certainty; hedging reflects this reality.
2. **Defends Against Criticism:** Claims like *"proves"* invite scrutiny; *"suggests"* invites dialogue.
3. **Aligns with Peer-Review Norms:** Journals (IEEE, Elsevier) expect calibrated language.
4. **Builds Credibility:** Reviewers trust authors who acknowledge limitations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Tense Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing past and present tenses *within the same paragraph* without justification.
> 2. **Weak Verb Retention (–1.5 marks):** Leaving *made, did, got, gave, took* in a "scientific writing" answer.
> 3. **Passive Overuse (–1 mark):** Using passive voice in *Introduction/Discussion* where active voice is expected.
> 4. **Agent Omission in Passive (–0.5 marks):** Writing *"It was concluded that..."* — always state *who* concluded.
> 5. **Absolute Claims Without Hedging (–1 mark):** Saying *"This proves..."* for preliminary findings; use *"This suggests..."*
> 6. **Wrong Section-Tense Mapping (–2 marks):** Using Future tense in Methodology (it should be Past Simple).
> 7. **Single-Tone Sentences (–1 mark):** Failure to demonstrate the ability to mix tenses appropriately in Discussion.
> 8. **Missing Transformation Steps (–1.5 marks):** Direct conversion without showing the SVO → OV3 structural shift.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Scientific Writing** = Clarity + Conciseness + Precision + Objectivity.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Verb Selection, Tense Consistency, Voice Choice.
- ✅ **Tense Mapping:** Abstract/Methods/Results → *Past Simple*; Introduction facts → *Present Simple*; Prior work → *Past/Present Perfect*; Future scope → *Future/Modal*.
- ✅ **Active Voice:** $S + V + O$ → Best for Abstract, Introduction, Discussion, Conclusion.
- ✅ **Passive Voice:** $O + \text{be} + V_3 + (\text{by } S)$ → Best for Methods and Results.
- ✅ **Avoid the "5 Sin Verbs":** *make, do, get, have, give* → Replace with discipline-specific strong verbs.
- ✅ **Hedging Calibrates Certainty:** *proves → shows → suggests → may → speculates*.
- ✅ **Transformation Steps (Active→Passive):** Move object → Add "be" → Add V3 → Optional "by + agent".
- ✅ **Methodology MUST use Past Simple** — never Present tense.
- ✅ **Use first-person plural "we"** in modern scientific writing (avoid "I") for active voice clarity.
- ✅ **Hedging defends against reviewers' criticism** — calibrated language reflects scientific maturity.
- ✅ **Examiners reward transformation demonstrations** — always show SVO decomposition before passive conversion.
- ✅ **Section-tense mismatches are the #1 reason for losing marks** — memorize the mapping table.
- ✅ **Verb-strengthening reduces word count by 30–50%** — a key conciseness metric in KTU evaluation.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Errors in Grammar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Errors in Grammar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Research Methodology and Statistics (PEFTT755)**, **Errors in Grammar** refers to the systematic deviations from the codified rules of standard written English (or the language of academic publication) that occur while drafting a research manuscript, thesis, technical report, conference paper, or journal article. These errors compromise the **linguistic accuracy**, **clarity**, and **scholarly precision** of research communication and fall under the broader umbrella of *research writing competence* — a Course Outcome (CO) explicitly mapped to effective dissemination of research findings.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, grammar errors are classified as **mechanical / surface-level writing errors** that, if uncorrected, can lead to **rejection of manuscripts**, loss of marks in dissertation evaluation, and compromised readability for peer reviewers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Errors in grammar are evaluated under the *Writing Skill* component of research communication. The examiner allocates marks for *correctness, clarity, coherence, and consistency* in written submissions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are a **civil engineer** who has just designed a beautiful, structurally perfect bridge. The calculations of load-bearing capacity, stress analysis, and material selection are all flawless — the *content* is excellent. However, when you present the blueprint, the labels read "Bridg Construction Year-2024" instead of "Bridge Construction Year — 2024."

Would the client trust the bridge? Would the inspector approve it?

**Grammar errors are exactly this** — they are the *typos, mismatched labels, and misaligned bolts* of a research paper. Even if your statistical analysis (the "engineering") is perfect, the reader's first impression is formed by the *surface-level polish* of your writing. A paper riddled with grammar errors signals **carelessness**, erodes **credibility**, and distracts the reviewer from the *actual* scientific contribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> - *Grammar* = the *grammar* (rules of combination) of any language
> - *Research writing* = a complex chemical compound
> - *Grammar errors* = impurities in the compound that alter its properties
> - *Purification* = editing, proofreading, and revision

## 1.3 Why Grammar Errors Matter in Research

Research is a **community-driven, communication-heavy enterprise**. A groundbreaking finding published in grammatically flawed English:

1. **Reduces citation potential** — readers skip poorly written papers
2. **Triggers editorial rejection** — journals enforce language standards
3. **Obscures scientific meaning** — ambiguity in tense/article use distorts results
4. **Damages author reputation** — perceived as amateurish
5. **Violates KTU dissertation rubric** — leads to mark deductions

## 1.4 Categories of Grammar Errors (Master List)

For systematic study, grammar errors are organized into **twelve core categories**:

| # | Error Category | Affected Grammar Element |
|---|----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Subject-Verb Agreement | Number agreement (singular/plural) |
| 2 | Tense Errors | Verb tense consistency |
| 3 | Article Errors | Use of *a*, *an*, *the* |
| 4 | Preposition Errors | Choice of *in*, *on*, *at*, *of*, *for* |
| 5 | Pronoun Errors | Reference, case, agreement |
| 6 | Voice Errors | Active vs. Passive misuse |
| 7 | Punctuation Errors | Commas, semicolons, apostrophes |
| 8 | Sentence Structure | Fragments, run-ons, comma splices |
| 9 | Word Choice | Confusion of similar words |
| 10 | Parallelism | List/series grammatical balance |
| 11 | Modifier Placement | Dangling and misplaced modifiers |
| 12 | Number/Spelling | Singular-plural, hyphenation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Error Category

Below is the **analytical breakdown** of each error type, the linguistic principle violated, and the rule for correction.

### 2.1.1 Subject-Verb Agreement Errors

**Why it occurs:** The writer loses track of the true subject when intervening phrases separate subject from verb.

**The Rule:** A singular subject takes a singular verb; a plural subject takes a plural verb.

**The Diagnostic Step:** Identify the *true head noun* of the subject (ignore prepositional phrases and relative clauses between subject and verb).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Agreement:** "Ignore everything between the subject and the verb when deciding agreement."

**Examples with Analysis:**

- *Incorrect:* "The **results** of the experiment **is** significant."
- *Diagnosis:* The head noun is *results* (plural), not *experiment*. Ignoring "of the experiment" → *results are*.
- *Correct:* "The **results** of the experiment **are** significant."

---

### 2.1.2 Tense Errors

**Why it occurs:** Research writing requires **systematic tense discipline**, but writers often shift between tenses arbitrarily.

**The Convention in Research Writing (KTU-Aligned):**

| Section of Paper | Preferred Tense | Rationale |
|------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Abstract | Past tense for own work; present for conclusions | Reports completed work |
| Introduction | Present tense for established facts; past for prior studies | Distinguishes prior work from current |
| Methods | Past tense | What *was* done |
| Results | Past tense | What *was* found |
| Discussion | Present tense for interpretation; past for specific findings | Generalizes findings |
| Conclusion | Present tense | States current contribution |

**The Rule:** Maintain *one* tense for *one* time-frame. Use past for completed actions, present for general truths and current interpretations.

---

### 2.1.3 Article Errors (A / An / The)

**The Rule Matrix:**

| Article | Use When... | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **a** | Consonant sound begins next word; first mention of singular countable noun | *a researcher, a university* |
| **an** | Vowel *sound* begins next word (not letter) | *an ANOVA, an hour, an MSc* |
| **the** | Specific/unique noun, second mention, superlatives | *the p-value, the data set, the most significant* |
| **Ø (zero)** | Plural generics, uncountable nouns, proper nouns, abstract quality | *Data were analyzed. Research is essential.* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Acronym Pitfall:** Choose article by the *pronounced sound*. "A UNESCO report" (you-NESS-co begins with consonant *y*-sound). "An MSc thesis" (M begins with vowel *em*-sound).

---

### 2.1.4 Preposition Errors

Prepositions are notoriously non-rule-based in English; they must be memorized as **fixed collocations**.

| Correct Collocation | Incorrect Variant |
|---------------------|-------------------|
| *at the University* | ✗ *in the University* |
| *on the basis of* | ✗ *in the basis of* |
| *in the context of* | ✗ *on the context of* |
| *research on / into / regarding* | ✗ *research about* (informal) |
| *consists of* | ✗ *consists with / from* |
| *different from* | ✗ *different than* (strict) |
| *independent of* | ✗ *independent from* (strict) |
| *according to* | ✗ *according with* |

---

### 2.1.5 Pronoun Errors

Three sub-types:

**(a) Pronoun Reference Error (Ambiguity):**
- *Incorrect:* "When the researcher met the supervisor, **he** was happy."
- *Problem:* Does *he* refer to researcher or supervisor?
- *Fix:* Repeat the noun or rewrite.

**(b) Pronoun Case Error:**
- *Incorrect:* "Between you and **I**, the data are flawed."
- *Correct:* "Between you and **me**, the data are flawed."
- *Test:* Remove the other noun → "between I" ✗ → "between me" ✓

**(c) Pronoun Agreement Error:**
- *Incorrect:* "Every student must bring **their** laptop."
- *Note:* In strict formal grammar, *their* mismatches singular *every student*. KTU / academic writing now accepts singular *they*.

---

### 2.1.6 Voice Errors (Active vs. Passive)

**Research Writing Convention:**

| Voice | Use Case | Example |
|-------|----------|---------|
| **Passive** | Methods, Results (object of study more important) | "The samples **were analyzed** using HPLC." |
| **Active** | Discussion, Conclusion (researcher asserts interpretation) | "We **argue** that the model fails to capture..." |

**Modern Trend:** Overuse of passive voice is discouraged. Most KTU 2024 evaluators prefer **balanced voice** — passive in methods, active in discussion.

**Common Error:** Unnecessary passive that hides the agent.
- *Weak:* "It has been found by the authors that..."
- *Strong:* "We found that..." (in Discussion) OR "The data indicate..." (in Results)

---

### 2.1.7 Punctuation Errors

| Punctuation | Function | Common Error |
|-------------|----------|--------------|
| Comma `,` | Pause / list / join clauses | Comma splice (joining two independent clauses) |
| Semicolon `;` | Join related independent clauses | Used like a comma |
| Colon `:` | Introduce list / explanation | Used after a verb (e.g., "includes:") |
| Apostrophe `'` | Possession / contraction | Confusing *its* vs *it's* |
| Hyphen `-` | Compound modifier before noun | "high quality paper" → "high-quality paper" |
| Em-dash `—` | Strong parenthetical pause | Overuse, or confusion with hyphen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Its vs. It's" Rule:**
> - *Its* = possessive (like *his, her*)
> - *It's* = contraction of *it is* or *it has*

---

### 2.1.8 Sentence Structure Errors

Three critical failures:

| Error Type | Definition | Example |
|------------|-----------|---------|
| **Fragment** | Incomplete sentence (no subject or verb) | "Because the data were significant." |
| **Run-on** | Two independent clauses smashed together with no punctuation | "The p-value was low the result was significant." |
| **Comma Splice** | Two independent clauses joined only by a comma | "The p-value was low, the result was significant." |

**The Four Fixes:**

1. *Period:* "The p-value was low. The result was significant."
2. *Semicolon:* "The p-value was low; the result was significant."
3. *Comma + Coordinating Conjunction (FANBOYS):* "The p-value was low, **so** the result was significant."
4. *Subordinator:* "**Because** the p-value was low, the result was significant."

---

### 2.1.9 Word Choice Errors (Commonly Confused Pairs)

| Word | Meaning | Common Confusion |
|------|---------|------------------|
| *affect* (verb) | to influence | confused with *effect* |
| *effect* (noun) | a result | confused with *affect* |
| *among* | more than two | confused with *between* |
| *between* | two entities | confused with *among* |
| *fewer* | countable items | confused with *less* |
| *less* | uncountable quantity | confused with *fewer* |
| *principal* | main / primary | confused with *principle* |
| *principle* | rule / law | confused with *principal* |
| *comprise* | to include / consist of | passive misuse |
| *composed of* | made up of | confused with *comprised of* |
| *data* | plural of datum (preferred formal) | used as singular |
| *criteria* | plural of criterion | used as singular |

---

### 2.1.10 Parallelism Errors

**The Rule:** Elements joined by *and*, *or*, *but*, or in a list must share the same grammatical form.

- *Incorrect:* "The study aims to **collect**, **analyzing**, and **interpret** data."
- *Diagnosis:* *collect* (verb base), *analyzing* (gerund), *interpret* (verb base) — mismatch.
- *Correct:* "The study aims to **collect**, **analyze**, and **interpret** data." (all verb bases)
- *OR:* "The study aims at the **collecting**, **analyzing**, and **interpreting** of data." (all gerunds)

---

### 2.1.11 Modifier Errors (Dangling & Misplaced)

**Dangling Modifier:** The subject implied by the modifier is missing from the sentence.

- *Incorrect:* "**Having completed the survey**, the results were analyzed."
- *Problem:* Who completed the survey? Not "the results"!
- *Correct:* "**Having completed the survey**, the researchers analyzed the results."

**Misplaced Modifier:** The modifier is positioned ambiguously.

- *Incorrect:* "She nearly earned ₹50 lakhs in research grants."
- *Implies:* She almost became ₹50 lakhs, but she actually earned it.
- *Correct:* "She earned nearly ₹50 lakhs in research grants."

---

### 2.1.12 Number / Spelling Errors

| Rule | Correct | Incorrect |
|------|---------|-----------|
| Singular countable noun needs article/determiner | "A finding is..." | ✗ "Finding is..." |
| Plurals formed by adding -s/-es (regular) | "analyses, criteria" | — |
| Irregular plurals to memorize | "children, phenomena, data" | "childs, phenomenas, datas" |
| Hyphenate compound adjectives before noun | "a well-designed study" | ✗ "a well designed study" |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Error | Trigger / Red Flag | Quick Fix |
|---|-------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Subject-Verb Agreement | Long subject + intervening phrase | Locate head noun |
| 2 | Tense Shift | Past/Present mixing in same paragraph | Pick tense for time-frame |
| 3 | Article Choice | Vowel sound? Specific? First mention? | Apply article matrix |
| 4 | Preposition | Collocation lookup | Memorize fixed phrases |
| 5 | Pronoun Ref. | Ambiguous *he/she/it/they* | Replace with noun |
| 6 | Voice | Overuse of *was/were + past participle* | Use active where appropriate |
| 7 | Comma Splice | Two independent clauses joined by *only* a comma | Use period, semicolon, or conjunction |
| 8 | Fragment | Sentence missing subject or finite verb | Add missing element |
| 9 | Word Choice | *Affect/effect, fewer/less* | Consult usage dictionary |
| 10 | Parallelism | Mixed verb forms in a list | Make all elements same form |
| 11 | Dangling Modifier | Modifier with no clear subject | Insert correct subject |
| 12 | Number/Spelling | *Datas, phenomenas, criteria as singular* | Learn irregular plurals |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Error-Correction Matrix

## 3.1 The Universal Error-Correction Procedure

The **Universal Error-Correction Procedure (UECP)** is a five-step diagnostic algorithm applicable to any grammar error in research writing. The KTU examiner rewards the demonstration of *systematic correction methodology*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The UECP Algorithm:**
>
> **Step 1 → IDENTIFY** the error category
> **Step 2 → ISOLATE** the offending phrase
> **Step 3 → APPLY** the governing rule
> **Step 4 → RECONSTRUCT** the corrected sentence
> **Step 5 → VERIFY** by re-reading for flow and meaning

We now apply this algorithm exhaustively to each major category.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Matrix — Category-by-Category

### Category 1: Subject-Verb Agreement — Full Worked Analysis

**Step 1 (Identify):** Verb does not match the subject in number.

**Step 2 (Isolate):** Locate the head noun of the subject phrase.

**Step 3 (Apply):** Match singular ↔ singular, plural ↔ plural.

**Step 4 (Reconstruct):** Adjust the verb form.

**Step 5 (Verify):** Read aloud — the verb must "agree" naturally with the subject.

#### Detailed Worked Example 1:

$$
\text{Sentence: } \underbrace{\text{The list of items}}_{\text{subject phrase}} \text{ are on the table.}
$$

| Step | Operation | Result |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Identify | Verb "are" seems plural | Subject "list" — singular? |
| Isolate | Strip modifiers | "list of items" → head noun = **list** |
| Apply | Singular subject requires singular verb | "is" |
| Reconstruct | Replace "are" with "is" | "The list of items **is** on the table." |
| Verify | Read aloud | Natural, correct ✓ |

#### Detailed Worked Example 2 (Tricky Case):

$$
\text{Sentence: } \underbrace{\text{Each of the participants}}_{\text{subject phrase}} \text{ were given a questionnaire.}
$$

| Step | Operation | Result |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Identify | Verb "were" — plural? | Subject starts with "Each" |
| Isolate | Strip modifiers | Head noun = **Each** (singular) |
| Apply | "Each" + singular verb | "was" |
| Reconstruct | "Each of the participants **was** given a questionnaire." | ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Subject Rules (High-Yield for KTU):**
>
> $$\text{Singular Subjects (take singular verbs):}$$
> $$\text{each, every, everyone, somebody, either, neither, anyone}$$
>
> $$\text{Plural Subjects (take plural verbs):}$$
> $$\text{many, few, several, both, others, various}$$

---

### Category 2: Tense Errors — Full Worked Analysis

**The Tense Consistency Algorithm:**

$$
\text{Select time-frame} \rightarrow \text{Choose appropriate tense} \rightarrow \text{Apply consistently within paragraph}
$$

#### Worked Example 3 (Methods Section):

**Original (Flawed):**
> "The samples **were collected** (past ✓). The researcher **adds** (present ✗) 5 mL of reagent. The mixture **was heated** (past ✓)."

**Correction Trace:**

| Sentence | Time-frame | Correct Tense |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Samples collected | Completed action | Past (*were collected*) |
| Researcher adds | Simultaneous action, but also completed | Past (*added*) |
| Mixture heated | Completed action | Past (*was heated*) |

**Corrected Paragraph:**
> "The samples **were collected**. The researcher **added** 5 mL of reagent. The mixture **was heated**."

**Alternative with Active Voice:**
> "We **collected** the samples, **added** 5 mL of reagent, and **heated** the mixture."

---

### Category 3: Article Errors — Decision Tree

The **Article Decision Tree** is the systematic method:

$$
\text{Is the noun countable?}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Is it specific?}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Has it been mentioned before?}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Use } \mathbf{the}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{No (first mention, generic)} \rightarrow \text{Use } \mathbf{a/an}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{No (not specific, generic plural)} \rightarrow \text{Use } \varnothing \text{ (no article)}
$$

#### Worked Example 4:

**Original:** "A study examines an effect of pollution on a health of students in a Kerala."

**Step-by-Step Correction:**

| Phrase | Decision | Correction |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| *a study* | First mention, singular, countable | *a study* ✓ (keep) |
| *an effect of pollution* | First mention of effect | *the effect* (if specific) / *an effect* (if generic) |
| *on a health* | "Health" is uncountable | *on health* (no article) |
| *of students* | Plural generic | *of students* ✓ |
| *in a Kerala* | "Kerala" is proper noun | *in Kerala* (no article) |

**Corrected:** "**A** study examines **the** effect of pollution on **the** health of students in **Ø Kerala**."

---

### Category 4: Preposition Errors — Collocation Lookup Method

**The Collocation Algorithm:**

$$
\text{Identify the key noun/verb/adjective} \rightarrow \text{Consult fixed collocation} \rightarrow \text{Apply correct preposition}
$$

#### Worked Example 5:

**Original:** "The conclusion is consisted with our hypothesis."

| Step | Operation |
|------|-----------|
| Identify key verb | *consist* |
| Look up collocation | *consist **of*** (active); *be composed **of*** (passive) |
| Wrong form: *consist with* / *consisted with* | Both ✗ |
| Apply correct form | *consistent **with*** (adjective) OR *consists **of*** (verb) |

**Corrected:** "The conclusion **is consistent with** our hypothesis." (adjective form)
**OR:** "The conclusion **consists of** three findings." (verb form)

---

### Category 5: Pronoun Reference — The Substitution Test

**The Substitution Algorithm:**

$$
\text{Replace pronoun with each possible antecedent} \rightarrow \text{Choose unambiguous match}
$$

#### Worked Example 6:

**Original:** "When the principal met the teacher, he welcomed the new curriculum."

| Possible Antecedent | Result | Plausibility |
|---------------------|--------|--------------|
| he = the principal | principal welcomed curriculum | Possible |
| he = the teacher | teacher welcomed curriculum | Equally possible |
| **AMBIGUITY** | Both readings valid | **Fix needed** |

**Corrected (by noun repetition):** "When the principal met the teacher, **the principal** welcomed the new curriculum."
**Corrected (by restructuring):** "**The principal**, upon meeting the teacher, welcomed the new curriculum."

---

### Category 6: Voice Errors — The Voice Selection Matrix

**The Voice Selection Algorithm:**

$$
\text{Is the agent (doer) important?}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Use ACTIVE voice}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{No (action or object is focus)} \rightarrow \text{Use PASSIVE voice}
$$

#### Worked Example 7:

**Original:** "It can be seen that the experimental group performed better than the control group, and this was observed by us across all trials."

**Diagnosis:** Verbose, weak, indirect.

**Compressed Active Version:**
> "We **observed** that the experimental group **performed** better than the control group across all trials."

**Step-by-Step Reduction:**

| Original Phrase | Problem | Replacement |
|-----------------|---------|-------------|
| "It can be seen that..." | Empty subject *it*; hedge | State the agent |
| "this was observed by us" | Passive, wordy | "We observed" |
| "across all trials" | ✓ (kept) | — |

---

### Category 7: Comma Splice — The Four-Fix Algorithm

**Original (comma splice):** "The p-value was 0.032, the result is statistically significant."

**Apply the Four-Fix Decision Tree:**

$$
\text{Are two clauses independent?}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Choose ONE of four fixes}
$$

#### Fix Trace:

| Fix Option | Resulting Sentence | Style |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| 1. Period | "The p-value was 0.032. The result is statistically significant." | Strong separation |
| 2. Semicolon | "The p-value was 0.032; the result is statistically significant." | Tightly related clauses |
| 3. + Conjunction | "The p-value was 0.032, **and** the result is statistically significant." | Logical linking |
| 4. Subordinator | "**Since** the p-value was 0.032, the result is statistically significant." | Causal emphasis |

**All four are correct.** The choice depends on *rhetorical intent*.

---

### Category 8: Fragment Repair Algorithm

**Original (fragment):** "Because the sample size was small."

**Diagnostic Trace:**

| Step | Operation |
|------|-----------|
| Identify subject | Implicit *we/researchers* |
| Identify finite verb | *was* |
| Determine independence | Subordinator "Because" makes it dependent |
| Fix | Attach to an independent clause OR remove subordinator |

**Fix A (attachment):** "**The study was underpowered.** Because the sample size was small, **the results must be interpreted cautiously**."
**Fix B (eliminate subordinator):** "The sample size was small." (now a complete sentence)

---

### Category 9: Word Choice — The Contextual Test

**The Word Choice Algorithm:**

$$
\text{Is the word used as noun or verb?} \rightarrow \text{Choose matching form}
$$

#### Worked Example 8 (Affect vs. Effect):

**Original:** "The treatment had a positive affect on patient outcomes."

| Step | Operation |
|------|-----------|
| Function | *affect* is used as a noun |
| Rule | *Effect* = noun; *Affect* = verb (usually) |
| Exception | *Affect* (noun) = emotion (psychology); *Effect* (verb) = to bring about (formal) |
| Apply | Need noun meaning "result" |
| Correct | "The treatment had a positive **effect** on patient outcomes." |

#### Worked Example 9 (Fewer vs. Less):

**Original:** "There were less participants in the experimental group."

| Step | Operation |
|------|-----------|
| Identify noun | *participants* (countable) |
| Apply rule | Countable → *fewer*; Uncountable → *less* |
| Correct | "There were **fewer** participants in the experimental group." |

---

### Category 10: Parallelism — The Form-Matching Algorithm

**Original:** "The study aims to **collect**, **analyzing**, and **interpret** data."

**Step-by-Step Parallelization:**

| Element | Form | After Correction |
|---------|------|------------------|
| *to collect* | Infinitive (verb base) | to collect |
| *analyzing* | Gerund (-ing) | analyzing → to analyze |
| *interpret* | Infinitive (verb base) | to interpret |
| **Result** | All infinitives | "to collect, **to analyze**, and **to interpret**" |

**OR (all gerunds):** "the **collecting**, **analyzing**, and **interpreting** of data"

**Verification:** The preposition *to* (or absence) and the verb form must be identical for all listed items.

---

### Category 11: Dangling Modifier — The Subject-Insertion Algorithm

**Original:** "**Having completed the ANOVA test**, the results were tabulated."

**Diagnostic Trace:**

| Step | Operation |
|------|-----------|
| Identify the modifier | "Having completed the ANOVA test" |
| Identify the implied subject | Whoever did the test |
| Identify the sentence subject | "the results" |
| Detect mismatch | Results cannot complete a test |
| Fix by adding missing subject | "Having completed the ANOVA test, **the researchers** tabulated the results." |

**Alternative Fix (rewrite modifier):** "**After the ANOVA test was completed**, the results were tabulated."

---

### Category 12: Number Errors — The Pluralization Algorithm

$$
\text{Is the noun regular or irregular?}
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{Regular} \rightarrow \text{Add } -s \text{ or } -es
$$

$$
\downarrow \text{Irregular} \rightarrow \text{Memorize plural form}
$$

**Common Research-Writing Irregular Plurals:**

| Singular | Plural (Correct) | Wrong Variant |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| criterion | **criteria** | criterions ✗ |
| phenomenon | **phenomena** | phenomenas ✗ |
| datum | **data** | datas ✗ |
| analysis | **analyses** | analysiss ✗ |
| hypothesis | **hypotheses** | hypothesis ✗ |
| matrix | **matrices** | matrixes (acceptable but less formal) |
| algorithm | **algorithms** | algorithmes ✗ |

**Subject-Verb Agreement with *Data*:**
- *Formal:* "The data **are** analyzed..." (plural)
- *Informal/Modern:* "The data **is**..." (singular, increasingly accepted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Prefer the **plural** treatment of *data* (*the data were*) for full academic rigor.

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example — A Full Research Sentence

**Original Flawed Sentence (containing FIVE errors):**

> "Each of the **participant** in the experimental group **were** given a questionnaire, **it's** aim **being** to collect datum about **it's** affect on learning, and the result **was** analyzed using a ANOVA."

**Error Identification Matrix:**

| # | Error Type | Location | Correction |
|---|------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Subject-Verb Agreement | "Each ... **were**" | → *was* |
| 2 | Singular/Plural | "**participant**" (singular) | → *participants* |
| 3 | Its vs. It's | "**it's** aim" (possessive needed) | → *its* |
| 4 | Plural of Datum | "**datum**" (singular) | → *data* |
| 5 | Article + Acronym | "**a** ANOVA" (vowel sound) | → *an ANOVA* |
| 6 | Pronoun Ambiguity | "**it's** affect on learning" | → *its effect on their* learning |
| 7 | Number Agreement | "the result **was**" | → *the results were* |

**Fully Corrected Sentence:**
> "**Each** of the **participants** in the experimental group **was** given a questionnaire, **its** aim **being** to collect **data** about **its** effect on learning, and the **results were** analyzed using **an** ANOVA."

> [!NOTE]
> This single sentence demonstrates why systematic, category-by-category diagnosis is essential — a casual read would catch only the most obvious errors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Universal Grammar-Error Identification Flowchart

This Mermaid flowchart maps the **Universal Error-Correction Procedure (UECP)** as a decision pipeline. It is the diagnostic backbone used by KTU evaluators when grading research-writing submissions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input: Research Sentence] --> scanA[Step 1: SCAN for Red Flags]
    scanA --> catQ{Step 2: Identify Category}
    catQ --> cat1A[Subject-Verb Agreement]
    catQ --> cat2A[Tense Consistency]
    catQ --> cat3A[Article Choice]
    catQ --> cat4A[Preposition]
    catQ --> cat5A[Pronoun Reference]
    catQ --> cat6A[Voice]
    catQ --> cat7A[Punctuation]
    catQ --> cat8A[Sentence Structure]
    catQ --> cat9A[Word Choice]
    catQ --> cat10A[Parallelism]
    catQ --> cat11A[Modifier Placement]
    catQ --> cat12A[Number and Spelling]
    cat1A --> ruleA[Step 3: Apply Governing Rule]
    cat2A --> ruleA
    cat3A --> ruleA
    cat4A --> ruleA
    cat5A --> ruleA
    cat6A --> ruleA
    cat7A --> ruleA
    cat8A --> ruleA
    cat9A --> ruleA
    cat10A --> ruleA
    cat11A --> ruleA
    cat12A --> ruleA
    ruleA --> reconstructA[Step 4: Reconstruct Sentence]
    reconstructA --> verifyA[Step 5: Verify by Re-Reading]
    verifyA --> outputA[Output: Corrected Sentence]
```

## 4.2 The Twelve-Error Classification Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following architecture diagram presents the **hierarchical taxonomy** of grammar errors mapped onto the **functional components** of a research paper. Each error class is associated with the section of a research manuscript where it most commonly occurs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootNode[Grammar Error Taxonomy in Research Writing]
    rootNode --> tier1A[Section-Level Errors]
    rootNode --> tier1B[Sentence-Level Errors]
    rootNode --> tier1C[Word-Level Errors]
    tier1A --> sec1A[Abstract: Tense Inconsistency]
    tier1A --> sec1B[Introduction: Verb Tense for Prior Work]
    tier1A --> sec1C[Methods: Passive Voice Overuse]
    tier1A --> sec1D[Results: Subject-Verb Mismatch]
    tier1A --> sec1E[Discussion: Voice Switching]
    tier1B --> sen1A[S1: Sentence Fragment]
    tier1B --> sen1B[S2: Comma Splice]
    tier1B --> sen1C[S3: Run-On Sentence]
    tier1B --> sen1D[S4: Dangling Modifier]
    tier1B --> sen1E[S5: Misplaced Modifier]
    tier1B --> sen1F[S6: Parallelism Break]
    tier1C --> w1A[W1: Article Error A AN THE]
    tier1C --> w1B[W2: Preposition Collocation]
    tier1C --> w1C[W3: Pronoun Reference]
    tier1C --> w1D[W4: Pronoun Case ME vs I]
    tier1C --> w1E[W5: Word Choice AFFECT vs EFFECT]
    tier1C --> w1F[W6: FEWER vs LESS]
    tier1C --> w1G[W7: Irregular Plural DATUM DATA]
    tier1C --> w1H[W8: ITS vs ITS Contraction]
```

## 4.3 The Article Decision-Tree Schematic

The article (*a*, *an*, *the*, or zero) is one of the most error-prone elements in academic English. The following decision tree renders the **Article Selection Algorithm** visually.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1[Is the noun countable?] --> a2{Countable?}
    a2 -- No --> a3[Use ZERO article or SOME ANY]
    a2 -- Yes --> a4{Is it specific?}
    a4 -- No Generic --> a5{Is it singular?}
    a5 -- Yes --> a6{Does next word begin with vowel SOUND?}
    a6 -- Yes --> a7[Use AN]
    a6 -- No --> a8[Use A]
    a5 -- No Plural --> a9[Use ZERO article]
    a4 -- Yes Specific --> a10{Has it been mentioned before?}
    a10 -- Yes --> a11[Use THE]
    a10 -- No --> a12{Is it unique one of a kind?}
    a12 -- Yes --> a11
    a12 -- No --> a13{Is it made definite by a phrase?}
    a13 -- Yes --> a11
    a13 -- No --> a14[Consider context, may need restructuring]
```

## 4.4 The Voice Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    v1[Drafted Sentence] --> v2{Is the AGENT important?}
    v2 -- YES Researcher does action --> v3[Use ACTIVE Voice]
    v2 -- NO Action or Object matters --> v4{Is this in METHODS or RESULTS?}
    v4 -- YES --> v5[Use PASSIVE Voice Conventional]
    v4 -- NO Discussion --> v6[Use ACTIVE Voice for Clarity]
    v3 --> v7[Final Sentence]
    v5 --> v7
    v6 --> v7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> Each Part A question carries **3 marks**. Model answers are precise and aligned to KTU valuation key length expectations (typically 3–5 lines per answer).

---

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define "dangling modifier" and rewrite the following sentence to correct it.**
*"After reviewing the literature, the gap in the research was identified."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dangling modifier** is a participial or verbal phrase at the beginning of a sentence whose implied subject is missing from the main clause, causing the modifier to attach illogically to the wrong noun.

In the given sentence, *After reviewing the literature* logically requires *the researcher(s)* as the agent, but the subject of the main clause is *the gap*. Hence, the modifier "dangles."

**Corrected Sentence:**
> "**After reviewing the literature, the researcher identified the gap in the research.**"

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for diagnosis, 1 mark for correct rewrite.)*

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Distinguish between "affect" and "effect" with one example sentence for each word.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Word | Part of Speech | Meaning | Example |
|------|----------------|---------|---------|
| **affect** | Verb (usually) | to influence | "The temperature **affects** the reaction rate." |
| **effect** | Noun (usually) | a result / outcome | "The treatment had a positive **effect** on recovery." |

*Memory Trick:* **A**ffect = **A**ction (verb); **E**ffect = **E**nd result (noun).

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark for affect, 1 mark for effect, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal sub-division typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question B-A [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Identify and correct **FIVE grammar errors** in the following paragraph. Name the error type for each correction.

> *"The list of grammatical errors **were** extensive. **Its** important to remember that each of these errors **has** different impact on the clarity of a research paper. A researcher **whom** wishes to publish must therefore avoid **it's** misuse. **Less** participants now report grammar errors than before. The data **is** inconclusive."*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four methods of fixing a comma splice** with original example sentences of your own, drawn from a research-writing context.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B-A**

#### Part (a) — Seven-Mark Solution

**Error Identification Matrix:**

| # | Original Phrase | Error Type | Correction |
|---|----------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | "The list ... **were** extensive" | Subject-Verb Agreement (list = singular) | "The list ... **was** extensive." |
| 2 | "**Its** important to remember" | Its vs. It's confusion | "**It's** important to remember." (It is) |
| 3 | "each of these errors **has** different impact" | Article missing + Agreement | "each of these errors **has a** different impact." |
| 4 | "A researcher **whom** wishes to publish" | Who vs. Whom case error | "A researcher **who** wishes to publish." |
| 5 | "avoid **it's** misuse" | Its vs. It's confusion | "avoid **its** misuse." |
| 6 | "**Less** participants" | Fewer vs. Less (countable) | "**Fewer** participants." |
| 7 | "The data **is** inconclusive" | Data as plural (formal convention) | "The data **are** inconclusive." |

**Corrected Paragraph:**
> "The list of grammatical errors **was** extensive. **It's** important to remember that each of these errors **has a** different impact on the clarity of a research paper. A researcher **who** wishes to publish must therefore avoid **its** misuse. **Fewer** participants now report grammar errors than before. The data **are** inconclusive."

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark per correctly identified + corrected error × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for naming error types.)*

#### Part (b) — Seven-Mark Solution: Four Fixes for a Comma Splice

**Definition:** A comma splice occurs when two independent clauses are joined by only a comma — this is grammatically incorrect.

**Example to fix:** *"The hypothesis was rejected, the p-value exceeded 0.05."*

**Fix 1 — Period (full stop):**
> "The hypothesis was rejected. The p-value exceeded 0.05."
*(Used when the two ideas deserve equal emphasis or strong separation.)*

**Fix 2 — Semicolon:**
> "The hypothesis was rejected; the p-value exceeded 0.05."
*(Used when the two clauses are closely related and the second explains or expands the first.)*

**Fix 3 — Comma + Coordinating Conjunction (FANBOYS):**
> "The hypothesis was rejected, **for** the p-value exceeded 0.05."
*(FANBOYS = For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So. Use a coordinating conjunction to express logical relationships.)*

**Fix 4 — Subordinator (dependent clause):**
> "**Because** the p-value exceeded 0.05, the hypothesis was rejected."
*(Rewrites one clause as a subordinated dependent clause using *because, although, when, since, while, etc.*)*

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark per fix × 4 = 4 marks; 3 marks for accurate research-context examples and clear explanation of when each is used.)*

---

### Question B-B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — 14 Marks] *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **twelve major categories of grammar errors** commonly encountered in research writing. For each category, provide one illustrative example of an incorrect sentence and its corrected version.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **article selection algorithm** with a decision-tree representation, and apply it to correct the following sentences:
1. *"A ANOVA test was performed to determine a difference between groups."*
2. *"The study was conducted in a IIT Bombay over period of six month."*

---

### **Model Answer — Question B-B**

#### Part (a) — Seven-Mark Solution: Twelve Major Grammar Error Categories

> *(For brevity in this model answer, two examples per category are condensed; in examination, students should illustrate each.)*

**1. Subject-Verb Agreement**
- *Incorrect:* "The results **shows** significance."
- *Correct:* "The results **show** significance."

**2. Tense Errors**
- *Incorrect:* "The researcher **adds** the reagent and **heated** the mixture."
