where $n$ = number of years since the author's first publication. It is a career-length-normalized h-index that allows comparison between a young researcher and a senior professor.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A researcher's 8 papers have the following citation counts: 30, 22, 15, 10, 6, 4, 2, 1. Calculate the h-index, g-index, and i10-index.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1: Sort in descending order** — already sorted: 30, 22, 15, 10, 6, 4, 2, 1.

**Step 2: Calculate h-index** — find the largest $h$ where paper at rank $h$ has $\geq h$ citations:

| Rank | Citations | $\geq$ Rank? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | $\checkmark$ |
| 2 | 22 | $\checkmark$ |
| 3 | 15 | $\checkmark$ |
| 4 | 10 | $\checkmark$ |
| 5 | 6 | $\checkmark$ |
| 6 | 4 | $\times$ (4 < 6) |

$\Rightarrow h = 5$

**Step 3: Calculate g-index** — find the largest $g$ where top $g$ papers have sum $\geq g^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g=5: 30+22+15+10+6 &= 83, \text{ need } 25 \quad \checkmark \\
g=6: 83+4 &= 87, \text{ need } 36 \quad \checkmark \\
g=7: 87+2 &= 89, \text{ need } 49 \quad \checkmark \\
g=8: 89+1 &= 90, \text{ need } 64 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All conditions pass up to $g=8$ (the total sum is 90, which is $\geq 64$).

$\Rightarrow g = 8$

**Step 4: Calculate i10-index** — count of papers with $\geq 10$ citations:
Papers with $\geq 10$ citations: 30, 22, 15, 10 → **4 papers**.

$\Rightarrow i10 = 4$

**Final Answer**: h-index = 5, g-index = 8, i10-index = 4.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Differentiate between traditional citation-based metrics and Altmetrics. Explain the PlumX framework with its five categories.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Model Solution**:

**Difference between Traditional Metrics and Altmetrics**:

| Aspect | Traditional Metrics | Altmetrics |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Journal citation databases (WOS, Scopus) | Social media, news, blogs, policy docs |
| **Time Window** | Long-term (years) | Real-time (within days) |
| **Measured Entity** | Researchers, journals | Individual articles |
| **Impact Type** | Academic impact only | Societal + academic impact |
| **Speed** | Slow (citations take years) | Fast (Twitter mentions in hours) |
| **Manipulation** | Self-citation, coercive citation | Bot accounts, paid engagement |
| **Coverage** | Limited to indexed journals | All scholarly outputs (datasets, code) |

**PlumX Framework** — PlumX is one of the most comprehensive altmetric tools. It categorizes research engagement into **5 broad categories**:

1. **USAGE** — counts of downloads, views, clicks, library holdings. Indicates how often the work is being **read or accessed**.
2. **CAPTURES** — bookmarks, favorites, exports to reference managers (Mendeley, Zotero). Indicates **saving for later use**.
3. **MENTIONS** — comments, news stories, blog posts, Wikipedia citations. Indicates **discussion and reference** outside academia.
4. **SOCIAL MEDIA** — shares, likes, retweets on Twitter, Facebook, Reddit. Indicates **public attention and virality**.
5. **CITATIONS** — traditional citation counts from Web of Science, Scopus. Maintains **academic legitimacy**.

PlumX aggregates signals from these five categories to produce a composite **PlumX Print** badge that gives a holistic view of an article's impact.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A journal published 400 citable items in 2021 and 500 in 2022. In 2023, the journal's 2021 papers received 1200 citations and 2022 papers received 1800 citations. Compute the 2023 Impact Factor and CiteScore (assume CiteScore for 2023 is based on citations in 2020–2023 to documents in 2019–2022, and you are given that 2020–2023 citations to 2019–2022 documents = 5000, documents in 2019–2022 = 1800).** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1: Calculate the 2023 Impact Factor**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Citations in 2023} &= 1200 + 1800 = 3000 \\
\text{Total Citable Items in 2021+2022} &= 400 + 500 = 900 \\
\text{IF}_{2023} &= \frac{3000}{900} = 3.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the 2023 CiteScore** (4-year window: citations 2020–2023 to documents 2019–2022).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CiteScore} &= \frac{\text{Citations in 2020-2023 to documents of 2019-2022}}{\text{All documents published in 2019-2022}} \\
&= \frac{5000}{1800} \\
&\approx 2.78
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compare and interpret**.

The IF (3.33) is higher than the CiteScore (2.78) for the same journal because:
- IF uses a 2-year window with **only citable items** (articles + reviews).
- CiteScore uses a 4-year window with **all documents** (including editorials, letters).

A single metric should not be used in isolation; both IF and CiteScore should be reported together for a balanced evaluation.

$\Rightarrow$ **2023 IF = 3.33**, **2023 CiteScore = 2.78**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing the citation window**: Students often swap $Y-1, Y-2$ in IF. The 2023 IF counts citations in **2023** to papers of **2021 and 2022** (not 2022 and 2023).
> 2. **Forgetting the formula for h-index**: It is NOT the most-cited paper's citation count, NOR the average. It is the **largest $h$ where the $h^{th}$ paper has $\geq h$ citations**.
> 3. **Mixing databases**: Don't compare h-index from Google Scholar with h-index from Web of Science — they are different numbers.
> 4. **Ignoring self-citation**: Many metrics don't filter self-citation. Mention this as a **limitation** in your answers.
> 5. **Not specifying the time window**: IF is a 2-year metric, but JIF also publishes a 5-year IF. Always mention which one you are calculating.
> 6. **Field normalization forgotten**: When comparing authors across fields (Biomedical vs. CS), use RCR or FWCI, not raw h-index.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concepts to Remember Before KTU Exam**

- **Impact Factor (IF)** uses a **2-year citation window** with only citable items.
- **CiteScore** uses a **4-year window** with all documents.
- **h-index** = largest $h$ where $h$ papers have $\geq h$ citations each.
- **g-index** = largest $g$ where top $g$ papers have $\geq g^2$ citations; always $g \geq h$.
- **i10-index** = number of papers with $\geq 10$ citations (Google Scholar).
- **m-quotient** = $h \div$ (years since first paper) → career-length normalized.
- **RCR** = actual citations $\div$ expected citations (field-normalized).
- **FWCI** = world-relative impact; 1.0 = global average.
- **Altmetrics** = social media, news, blogs, downloads → measure societal impact.
- **PlumX** has **5 categories**: Usage, Captures, Mentions, Social Media, Citations.
- **SJR** = prestige-weighted, like Google's PageRank; uses citing journal quality.
- **SNIP** = field-normalized at citation level to account for discipline differences.
- **Eigenfactor** = 5-year SJR but **excludes self-citations**.
- **Hirsch (2005)** proposed the h-index; **Egghe (2006)** proposed g-index.
- **Garfield (1955)** invented the Impact Factor at the Institute for Scientific Information (ISI).
- **Limitations to mention**: field bias, self-citation, database dependence, English-language bias, career-length bias.
- **Always specify the database** (WoS / Scopus / Google Scholar) when reporting any metric.
- **Real-world use**: NAAC, NIRF, UGC-CAS, AICTE PhD supervisor eligibility all use these metrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Referencing Management Software (Mendeley, Zotero, etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Referencing Management Software (Mendeley, Zotero, etc.) # MODULE 4: REFERENCING MANAGEMENT SOFTWARE ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reference Management Software (RMS)**, also known as **bibliographic management software** or **citation management software**, is a dedicated application designed to collect, store, organize, retrieve, format, and export bibliographic references (citations) for academic research, scholarly writing, and publications. It acts as a personal digital library that automates the tedious process of manually formatting references in various citation styles (APA, MLA, Chicago, IEEE, Vancouver, Harvard, etc.). In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these tools are classified as **Research Support Tools** that fall under the umbrella of **Information Management Systems** for academic and scientific work. They integrate with word processors (Microsoft Word, LibreOffice Writer, Google Docs) via plugins to enable **in-text citation** and **automated bibliography generation**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic belongs to Module 4, which covers *"Tools for Research"* — specifically the section on *Literature Management and Bibliography Tools*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Think of Reference Management Software as a "Smart Library Card System for the Digital Age."** Imagine a librarian who: - 📚 **Remembers every book** you have ever read (stores bibliographic data). - 🏷️ **Knows where you put the PDF** of every paper (PDF annotation and storage). - ✍️ **Can instantly rewrite the same book citation** in APA, MLA, IEEE, or Chicago style — without you typing it again (citation style switching). - 🔄 **Automatically inserts citations** as you type in Word (MS Word plugin). - 🤝 **Lets you share your library** with co-authors for collaborative research (cloud sync and shared libraries). That "librarian" is **Mendeley, Zotero, EndNote**, or any of the other tools we are about to study. Without such a tool, a researcher writing a 50-reference thesis would have to manually retype or reformat each citation — a process that is error-prone, time-consuming, and academically risky. ### 1.3 Why Do KTU Students Need This? | Pain Point (Manual Method) | Solution via RMS | |---|---| | Spending 30+ minutes formatting a single bibliography page | One-click style switching (under 5 seconds) | | Risk of plagiarism due to missing citations | Insert-as-you-write workflow + duplicate detection | | PDFs scattered across Downloads, Desktop, Email | Centralized PDF library with auto-renaming | | Co-authors using different reference styles | Shared libraries with unified style application | | Losing track of "which paper said what" | Annotation, tagging, and note-taking features | > [!TIP] > For your **B.Tech Mini Project (MP1)**, **Main Project (MP2)**, and **M.Tech/PG dissertations**, KTU examiners actively check reference quality. Using a reference manager makes your bibliography consistent, professional, and examiner-friendly. ### 1.4 Classification of Reference Management Software RMS tools are classified along three KTU-recognized axes: **Axis 1: Deployment Model** - **Desktop-based:** EndNote, JabRef, BibTeX - **Web-based (Cloud-native):** Paperpile, RefWorks - **Hybrid (Desktop + Web Sync):** Mendeley, Zotero **Axis 2: License Model** - **Free / Open Source:** Zotero, JabRef, Mendeley (basic free tier) - **Subscription-based (Paid):** EndNote, RefWorks, Paperpile - **Freemium:** Mendeley (Elsevier-owned, free with optional premium) **Axis 3: Output Format** - **CSL-based (Citation Style Language):** Zotero, Mendeley, Paperpile - **Proprietary Style Files (.ens):** EndNote - **BibTeX Native:** JabRef, BibLaTeX ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Core Functional Architecture of Reference Management Software Every reference management tool, regardless of brand, follows a **five-stage operational pipeline**: **Stage 1 — Capture (Ingestion)** - Manual entry of bibliographic data - Import from databases (Scopus, Web of Science, PubMed, Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore) - Browser extension one-click capture - DOI-based auto-fetch (Digital Object Identifier resolver) - PDF metadata extraction (DOI, title, author, journal) **Stage 2 — Store (Persistence Layer)** - Local database (SQLite for Zotero, proprietary for EndNote) - PDF file storage with hash-based deduplication - Cloud synchronization (Mendeley Desktop, Zotero Web Library, iCloud for Papers) **Stage 3 — Organize (Curation)** - Folders / Collections - Tags (user-defined keywords) - Smart search queries - Notes and annotations - Linked files (PDFs, supplementary materials) **Stage 4 — Cite (Insertion Engine)** - Word/LibreOffice plugin - In-text citation insertion (author-year or numeric, depending on style) - Bibliography regeneration - Citation style switching **Stage 5 — Share (Collaboration)** - Shared libraries (group libraries in Zotero, shared groups in Mendeley) - Export formats (BibTeX, RIS, EndNote XML, plain text) - Public group libraries for collaborative projects ### 2.2 Major Tools — Theoretical Profile #### 2.2.1 Mendeley > [!NOTE] > **Mendeley** was founded in 2007 by three PhD students (Victor Henning, Paul Foeckler, and Jan Reichelt) and acquired by **Elsevier** in 2013. It is the most widely-used reference manager globally in engineering and life sciences. - **Developer:** Elsevier (RELX Group) - **License Model:** Freemium (Free tier = 2 GB cloud storage) - **Storage Backend:** Local Mendeley Desktop + Mendeley Web (cloud sync) - **Word Plugin:** "Mendeley Cite" (for MS Word and LibreOffice) - **Citation Styles:** 10,000+ CSL styles via the official style repository - **PDF Reader:** Built-in PDF reader with highlight, underline, sticky notes - **Social Features:** Public groups, profile pages, "Mendeley Suggest" recommendations - **Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Web, iOS, Android #### 2.2.2 Zotero > [!IMPORTANT] > **Zotero** is developed by the **Corporation for Digital Scholarship** (Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media, George Mason University, USA). It is **free, open-source**, and governed by a non-profit — making it the academic gold standard for unrestricted research. - **Developer:** Corporation for Digital Scholarship (non-profit) - **License Model:** Fully Free and Open Source (AGPLv3) - **Storage Backend:** Local SQLite database + optional Zotero File Storage (300 MB free) - **Word Plugin:** "Zotero Word Processor Plugin" (built-in) - **Citation Styles:** 10,000+ CSL styles (community-maintained) - **PDF Reader:** Built-in PDF reader with annotations - **Extensibility:** Plugin architecture (Better BibTeX, Zotero PDF Translate, ZotFile) - **Browser Connector:** Mozilla Firefox native + Chrome/Edge/Safari extension - **Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS (read-only) #### 2.2.3 EndNote - **Developer:** Clarivate Analytics (formerly Thomson Reuters) - **License Model:** Paid (Institutional license common in universities) - **Storage Backend:** Local `.enl` library + EndNote Web (paid sync) - **Word Plugin:** "EndNote Cite-While-You-Write (CWYW)" - **Citation Styles:** 7,000+ proprietary `.ens` styles - **Special Strength:** Deep integration with Web of Science database - **Platforms:** Windows, macOS (iPad app available) #### 2.2.4 Other Notable Tools | Tool | Key Differentiator | Cost | |---|---|---| | **RefWorks** | Web-native, popular in academic libraries | Institutional subscription | | **Paperpile** | Google Docs native integration | Paid (individual) | | **JabRef** | BibTeX-native, open-source, Java-based | Free (open source) | | **Papers (Read by Papers)** | macOS-iOS ecosystem | Paid | | **Citavi** | Knowledge management + task planning | Free (academic) | ### 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Feature Comparison Matrix (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **high-yield comparison** examiners expect students to memorize. This is the most-asked topic in KTU University Exams for Module 4. | Feature | Mendeley | Zotero | EndNote | JabRef | RefWorks | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Cost** | Freemium | Free (Open Source) | Paid | Free (Open Source) | Subscription | | **Developer** | Elsevier | Corporation for Digital Scholarship | Clarivate | Open-source community | ProQuest | | **Storage Capacity** | 2 GB free | 300 MB free | Unlimited local | Unlimited local | 500 MB+ | | **Cloud Sync** | Yes (Mendeley Web) | Yes (Zotero account) | Yes (EndNote Web) | No (local only) | Yes (web-native) | | **Word Plugin** | Mendeley Cite | Zotero Plugin | CWYW | BibTeX4Word | Write-N-Cite | | **Citation Styles** | 10,000+ | 10,000+ | 7,000+ | BibTeX styles | 4,000+ | | **PDF Reader** | Yes (built-in) | Yes (built-in) | Yes (tabbed) | No (external) | No (external) | | **Browser Extension** | Yes | Yes (native) | Yes | No | Yes | | **Collaboration** | Public/Private Groups | Group Libraries | Shared Library | Single user | Shared folders | | **Platforms** | Win/Mac/Linux | Win/Mac/Linux | Win/Mac | Win/Mac/Linux | Web-based | | **Mobile App** | iOS/Android | iOS (read-only) | iPad only | No | Limited | | **Data Format** | CSL JSON | Zotero RDF | EndNote XML | BibTeX | RefWorks format | | **Open Standard** | CSL | CSL + Zotero RDF | Proprietary (.ens) | BibTeX | Proprietary | ### 2.4 Key Terminology for KTU 2024 Exam > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-define terms** (commonly asked as 3-mark questions): - **Citation Style:** A standardized format for presenting in-text citations and bibliographies (e.g., APA 7th, IEEE, Vancouver, MLA, Chicago). Defined using **CSL (Citation Style Language)** — an open XML-based declarative language. - **CSL (Citation Style Language):** An open-source XML schema for describing the formatting of citations and bibliographies. Maintained by the **Citation Style Language** project and used by Zotero, Mendeley, Papers, and Pandoc. - **DOI (Digital Object Identifier):** A persistent identifier for academic articles, books, and datasets. Format: `10.xxxx/yyyyy`. Enables one-click import into any RMS. - **BibTeX:** A reference management format native to LaTeX. Files have `.bib` extension. Each entry has a unique **citation key** (e.g., `@article{smith2024,...}`). - **Citation Key:** A unique alphanumeric identifier assigned to each reference in a library (e.g., `Smith2024`, `IEEE_2023_ML`). Used for LaTeX `\cite{key}` commands. - **In-Text Citation:** A short reference within the body of the paper (e.g., `(Smith, 2024)` or `[1]`). - **Bibliography:** The complete list of all cited works, placed at the end of the document. - **Metadata:** Structured data about a publication (title, authors, year, journal, volume, pages, DOI, abstract). Stored in a structured format for programmatic processing. - **RIS File:** A standardized tag-based format for citations (`.ris` extension). Universally supported across RMS tools. - **Word Processor Plugin:** A software add-in that integrates the RMS with MS Word/LibreOffice for in-text citation insertion and bibliography generation. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Typical Use Case | |---|---| | **Computer Science (ML/AI)** | Managing 100+ arXiv preprints for a literature review on deep learning architectures | | **Civil Engineering** | Tracking ACI, IS 456, and ASTM standards for thesis on structural concrete | | **Mechanical Engineering** | Citing ASME journal papers on CFD or finite element analysis | | **Electrical Engineering** | Managing IEEE conference proceedings for VLSI design research | | **Biomedical Engineering** | PubMed/Mendeley integration for systematic reviews in medical device research | | **Project Reports (B.Tech MP1/MP2)** | Generating 20-30 references in IEEE format automatically | ## 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Workflow ### 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow: Adding a Reference in Zotero (Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > This procedure is examiner-favorite for **"Explain the steps to add a reference using [Tool]"** type 7-mark questions. **Step 1 — Install Zotero and the Browser Connector** - Download Zotero from the official website for your operating system. - Install the **Zotero Connector** browser extension (available for Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari). - The connector appears as a small icon in your browser toolbar. **Step 2 — Identify the Source** - Open a database such as **Google Scholar, PubMed, IEEE Xplore, Scopus, ACM Digital Library**, or a publisher's website. - Search for the paper you wish to add. **Step 3 — Capture via Browser Connector** - Navigate to the article's record page (not the PDF). - Click the **Zotero Connector icon** in the browser toolbar. The icon changes appearance based on the source type (article, book, webpage). - The metadata — including authors, title, journal, volume, issue, pages, DOI, abstract, and even the PDF file — is automatically downloaded to your Zotero library. **Step 4 — Manual Entry (Fallback)** - If the connector fails, click the **"New Item"** button (green "+" icon) in Zotero. - Select the appropriate type (e.g., *Journal Article, Book, Conference Paper*). - Fill in the metadata fields manually (Title, Author, Year, Publication, etc.). - Attach the PDF by dragging and dropping the file into the record. **Step 5 — Organize into Collections** - Right-click **"My Library"** → **"New Collection"**. - Name the collection (e.g., "Machine Learning Survey 2024"). - Drag and drop references into the collection. A single reference can belong to multiple collections. **Step 6 — Add Tags and Notes** - Select the reference → click the **Tags** tab → add keywords (e.g., "deep learning," "transformers"). - Click the **Notes** tab → write a summary or research note. **Step 7 — Cite in Word** - Open Microsoft Word with your manuscript. - Place the cursor where you want the citation. - Click the **Zotero tab** → **"Add Citation"**. - Type the author or title in the search bar. - Select the matching reference → press **Enter**. - The in-text citation is inserted (e.g., `(Smith, 2024)` for APA). - To insert the bibliography, click **"Add Bibliography"**. Zotero scans the document for all in-text citations and generates the bibliography at the cursor location. **Step 8 — Switch Citation Styles** - In the Zotero Word plugin, click **"Document Preferences"**. - Select the desired style (APA 7th, IEEE, Vancouver, MLA, Chicago, etc.). - All in-text citations and the bibliography are **reformatted instantly** — no manual edits required. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Workflow: Adding a Reference in Mendeley (Step-by-Step) **Step 1 — Install Mendeley Desktop** - Download from mendeley.com (free account required). - Sign in with your institutional or personal email. **Step 2 — Add References** - **Method A — Drag and Drop PDF:** Drag any PDF file into the Mendeley window. The built-in **"Document Reader"** extracts metadata (title, authors, year, journal) using Elsevier's **CrossRef API**. - **Method B — Web Importer:** Install the **Mendeley Web Importer** browser extension. Click the icon on an article page to import directly. - **Method C — Manual Entry:** Click **"Add"** → select document type → fill in metadata fields. **Step 3 — Organize** - Create folders via the **"Create Folder"** button. - Add tags from the right panel. - Use the **"Filter"** search bar for smart search across all fields. **Step 4 — Cite in Word** - Mendeley installs the **"Mendeley Cite"** add-in automatically. - Open Word → look for the **"References"** tab → click **"Mendeley Cite"**. - Sign in → search for reference → insert citation. - Click **"Insert Bibliography"** to generate the reference list. **Step 5 — Collaboration** - Click **"Create Group"** → choose public or private → invite co-authors via email. - Group members can add, annotate, and read PDFs in a shared library. ### 3.3 Citation Style Mathematical Analogy Although citation styles are not numerical, we can model the **bibliography generation process** as a transformation function: $$\text{Bibliography} = f(\text{References} \times \text{CSL Style})$$ Where: - $f$ = the CSL rendering engine - $\text{References}$ = the set of structured metadata entries - $\text{CSL Style}$ = the chosen style file (e.g., `apa-7th-edition.csl`) For a paper with $n$ references, switching from style $S_1$ to style $S_2$ yields: $$B_{S_2} = f(\{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n\}, S_2)$$ This is why **switching styles is a one-click operation** — the engine re-runs $f$ with the new style file but uses the same underlying reference set. ### 3.4 Comparative Cost-Benefit Analysis (Decision Framework) > [!TIP] > Use this analytical framework to decide **which RMS to recommend** in exam answers or for your own project. For a researcher with $n$ references and $s$ citation style changes required, the **time savings** $T$ versus manual formatting can be modeled as: $$T_{\text{saved}} = (n \times t_{\text{manual}}) - (n \times t_{\text{RMS}}) - T_{\text{setup}}$$ Where: - $t_{\text{manual}}$ ≈ 5 minutes per reference (manual typing + formatting) - $t_{\text{RMS}}$ ≈ 0.5 minutes per reference (insertion via plugin) - $T_{\text{setup}}$ ≈ 60 minutes (one-time learning + installation) **Break-even point** (where RMS becomes worthwhile): $$n \times (5 - 0.5) = 60$$ $$n \approx 13.3 \text{ references}$$ > Therefore, **for any project with more than 14 references, using an RMS saves time.** All KTU B.Tech projects (MP1, MP2) easily exceed this threshold, making RMS use **mandatory** from a time-economics standpoint. ### 3.5 Zotero vs. Mendeley Decision Matrix | Selection Criterion | Choose Zotero if… | Choose Mendeley if… | |---|---|---| | **Budget constraint** | You need a fully free tool with no limits | You accept Elsevier's freemium model | | **Privacy / Open-source ethos** | You want AGPLv3 open-source code | You are fine with proprietary software | | **LaTeX / BibTeX workflow** | You need Better BibTeX plugin for citation key control | Mendeley has limited BibTeX export | | **PDF storage needs** | You have < 300 MB of PDFs OR use WebDAV sync | You want 2 GB free cloud storage | | **Browser integration** | You use Firefox heavily (Zotero was Mozilla-born) | You use Chrome/Edge | | **Institutional affiliation** | You are at an academic institution with no Elsevier license | Your university has a Mendeley institutional edition | | **Mobile reading** | You need iPad annotation sync | You need full iOS/Android apps | ### 3.6 Citation Style Selection Algorithm When selecting a citation style for your KTU report, follow this decision tree: $$ \text{Style} = \begin{cases} \text{IEEE} & \text{if field} \in \{\text{CS, EE, ECE, EEE, Mechanical}\} \\ \text{APA 7th} & \text{if field} \in \{\text{Psychology, Education, Management, Civil (some)}\} \\ \text{Vancouver} & \text{if field} = \text{Biomedical / Medical} \\ \text{ACM} & \text{if field} = \text{Computer Science (ACM publications)} \\ \text{ASCE} & \text{if field} = \text{Civil / Structural Engineering (ASCE journals)} \\ \text{ASME} & \text{if field} = \text{Mechanical Engineering (ASME journals)} \\ \text{Harvard} & \text{if field} = \text{Management / Economics} \\ \text{Chicago} & \text{if field} = \text{Humanities / Architecture} \end{cases} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Reference Management System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Databases
Scopus, IEEE, PubMed, Google Scholar] -->|One-Click Import| B[Browser Connector
Zotero Connector / Mendeley Web Importer] A2[PDF Files
Local / Downloaded] -->|Drag and Drop| C[Ingestion Engine
DOI Resolver, CrossRef API] B --> C D[Manual Entry
New Item Button] --> C C --> E[Local Database
SQLite / Proprietary] E --> F[Organize Module
Collections, Tags, Notes] F --> G[PDF Storage
File System + Hash Dedup] F --> H[Search Index
Full-Text + Metadata] I[Cloud Sync
Zotero Web / Mendeley Web] <--> E J[Word Processor Plugin
Mendeley Cite / Zotero Plugin] -->|Insert Citation| K[MS Word / LibreOffice Document] E --> J L[CSL Style Engine
APA, IEEE, Vancouver, MLA] -->|Render| K E --> L M[Group Library
Collaboration] <--> E N[BibTeX / RIS Export
LaTeX / Pandoc] --> E ``` ### 4.2 Workflow: Adding a Reference and Generating Bibliography ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Open Database
Google Scholar / IEEE] --> S2[Step 2: Click Connector Icon] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Metadata Auto-Extracted] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Verify in Library] S4 --> S5{Step 5: Organize?} S5 -->|Yes| S6[Add to Collection + Tags] S5 -->|No| S7[Keep in My Library] S6 --> S8[Step 6: Open MS Word] S7 --> S8 S8 --> S9[Step 7: Click Zotero/Mendeley Tab] S9 --> S10[Step 8: Insert In-Text Citation] S10 --> S11[Step 9: Click Insert Bibliography] S11 --> S12{Step 10: Change Style?} S12 -->|Yes| S13[Select New Style from Dropdown] S13 --> S14[Bibliography Auto-Refreshes] S12 -->|No| S15[Final Document Ready] S14 --> S15 ``` ### 4.3 Mendeley vs. Zotero — Side-by-Side Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Mendeley[Mendeley Architecture] M1[Mendeley Desktop App] --> M2[Local Library .xml] M1 --> M3[PDF Folder] M1 <--> M4[Mendeley Web Cloud] M4 <--> M5[Group Libraries] M1 --> M6[Mendeley Cite Plugin] M6 --> M7[MS Word] end subgraph Zotero[Zotero Architecture] Z1[Zotero Desktop App] --> Z2[SQLite Database] Z1 --> Z3[Storage Folder PDFs] Z1 <--> Z4[Zotero Web Library] Z4 <--> Z5[Group Libraries] Z1 --> Z6[Zotero Word Plugin] Z6 --> Z7[MS Word / LibreOffice] Z1 --> Z8[Zotero Connectors Browser] Z8 --> Z9[Chrome / Firefox / Edge] Z1 --> Z10[Extensions
Better BibTeX, ZotFile] end ``` ### 4.4 Citation Style Switching Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Reference Database
Structured Metadata] --> CSL{CSL Style Engine} A1[apa-7th-edition.csl] --> CSL A2[ieee.csl] --> CSL A3[vancouver.csl] --> CSL A4[mla-9th-edition.csl] --> CSL CSL --> O1[APA Formatted Output] CSL --> O2[IEEE Formatted Output] CSL --> O3[Vancouver Formatted Output] CSL --> O4[MLA Formatted Output] O1 --> DOC[MS Word Document] O2 --> DOC O3 --> DOC O4 --> DOC ``` ### 4.5 Decision Tree — Which RMS Should a KTU Student Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Need Free Tool?} -->|Yes| Q2{Use LaTeX?} Q1 -->|No - Institution Pays| Q3[Choose EndNote] Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Choose Zotero + Better BibTeX] Q2 -->|No MS Word Only| Q5{Need > 2 GB Cloud?} Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Zotero + WebDAV / Paid Storage] Q5 -->|No| Q7[Choose Mendeley or Zotero] Q4 --> Q8[Final: Zotero] Q6 --> Q8 Q7 --> Q9{Firefox Heavy User?} Q9 -->|Yes| Q8 Q9 -->|No| Q10[Choose Mendeley] Q3 --> Q11[Final: EndNote] Q8 --> Q12[Selected] Q10 --> Q12 Q11 --> Q12 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Define Reference Management Software. List any three popular reference management tools.** **Model Answer:** Reference Management Software (RMS) is a computer application designed to collect, organize, store, retrieve, format, and export bibliographic references (citations) for academic research and scholarly writing. It automates the creation of in-text citations and bibliographies in various standardized citation styles (APA, IEEE, Vancouver, MLA, etc.) and integrates with word processors like Microsoft Word and LibreOffice. Three popular reference management tools are: 1. **Mendeley** — Free reference manager with PDF reader, social features, and cloud sync (developed by Elsevier). 2. **Zotero** — Free and open-source reference manager with browser connector, plugin architecture, and 10,000+ citation styles (developed by Corporation for Digital Scholarship). 3. **EndNote** — Commercial reference manager widely used in academic and corporate research environments (developed by Clarivate Analytics). *[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for three tools]* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** **What is a citation style? Name the citation style commonly used in IEEE conference papers.** **Model Answer:** A **citation style** is a standardized set of rules that defines the format for presenting in-text citations and bibliographic entries in a research document. Different academic disciplines use different citation styles to maintain consistency and readability across publications. The citation style commonly used in **IEEE conference papers and journals** is the **IEEE Editorial Style Manual** (often simply called the "IEEE style"). It uses a **numeric citation system** in square brackets — for example, `[1]`, `[2]`, `[3]` — and references are listed in the order of first appearance in the text. The corresponding bibliography entry includes author initials, surname, paper title in quotes, conference name in italics, and year. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "IEEE format" with "APA format." IEEE uses **numbered citations in square brackets**, while APA uses **author-date format** like `(Smith, 2024)`. --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question]** **Differentiate between Zotero and Mendeley in terms of licensing and storage.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Zotero | Mendeley | |---|---|---| | **Licensing** | Free and open-source (AGPLv3 license), developed by a non-profit organization (Corporation for Digital Scholarship) | Freemium — free for basic use, owned by Elsevier (commercial company) | | **Storage** | 300 MB free cloud storage via Zotero account; unlimited local storage | 2 GB free cloud storage via Mendeley Web; unlimited local storage | Zotero is fully free forever, while Mendeley's free tier has limits and some advanced features require an institutional or paid subscription. --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam Pattern] **Q.A. (a)** Explain the key features of Mendeley. How is Mendeley Cite plugin used to insert citations in a Word document? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare Mendeley, Zotero, and EndNote based on any four features. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Key Features of Mendeley (7 Marks)** Mendeley is a reference management software developed by **Elsevier** in 2007 and acquired by the company in 2013. Its key features are: 1. **PDF Library Management:** Mendeley automatically extracts metadata from imported PDF files (title, authors, journal, year, DOI) using Elsevier's **CrossRef API** integration. It stores PDFs in a centralized local folder with hash-based deduplication. 2. **Built-in PDF Reader:** The integrated document reader allows highlighting, underlining, and adding sticky notes to PDFs. All annotations are stored in the local library and synchronized to the cloud. 3. **Mendeley Cite Plugin:** A dedicated add-in for Microsoft Word and LibreOffice Writer that enables "cite while you write" functionality. Insert in-text citations, generate bibliographies, and switch styles with a single click. 4. **Mendeley Web Importer:** A browser extension (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari) that allows one-click capture of references from databases like Scopus, Web of Science, IEEE Xplore, PubMed, and Google Scholar. 5. **Group Libraries:** Researchers can create public or private groups (up to 100 members in the free tier), share PDFs, and collaboratively annotate references — ideal for joint research projects and literature reviews. 6. **Citation Style Support:** Over 10,000 citation styles via the official CSL style repository, including APA 7th, IEEE, Vancouver, MLA, Chicago, Harvard, and ACM. 7. **Cross-Platform Availability:** Available on Windows, macOS, Linux, Web, iOS, and Android — with cloud synchronization of libraries across all devices. 8. **Social Network:** Mendeley has a built-in academic social network ("Mendeley Suggest") that recommends papers based on your library's content. **Using the Mendeley Cite Plugin (Step-by-Step):** - **Step 1:** Ensure Mendeley Desktop is installed and you are signed in. - **Step 2:** Open your manuscript in Microsoft Word. The "Mendeley Cite" add-in appears under the **"References"** tab. - **Step 3:** Click **"Mendeley Cite"** → sign in with your Mendeley credentials. - **Step 4:** Place the cursor in the text where the citation should appear. - **Step 5:** In the Mendeley Cite panel, type the author name or title in the search bar. - **Step 6:** Select the matching reference from the dropdown list → click **"Insert"**. The in-text citation appears in the document. - **Step 7:** To insert the bibliography, place the cursor at the document's end and click **"Insert Bibliography"** in the Mendeley Cite panel. The full reference list is auto-generated. - **Step 8:** To change the citation style, click **"Citation Style"** in the panel and select the desired style (e.g., switch from APA to IEEE). All in-text citations and the bibliography reformat automatically. --- **(b) Comparison of Mendeley, Zotero, and EndNote (7 Marks)** | Feature | Mendeley | Zotero | EndNote | |---|---|---|---| | **Licensing** | Freemium (Elsevier-owned) | Free and open-source (AGPLv3) | Paid (Clarivate-owned) | | **Storage** | 2 GB free cloud; unlimited local | 300 MB free cloud; unlimited local | Unlimited local + paid cloud | | **Word Plugin** | Mendeley Cite | Zotero Plugin (built-in) | Cite-While-You-Write (CWYW) | | **Citation Styles** | 10,000+ (CSL) | 10,000+ (CSL) | 7,000+ (proprietary .ens) | | **PDF Reader** | Yes (built-in with annotations) | Yes (built-in with annotations) | Yes (tabbed reader) | | **Mobile App** | Full iOS/Android apps | iOS read-only | iPad only | | **Open Source** | No (proprietary) | Yes (community-developed) | No (proprietary) | | **Best For** | Biomedical/Engineering research | Humanities/Open-source advocates | Corporate/Institutional research | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **Q.B. (a)** What is Zotero? Explain the step-by-step procedure to add a reference from Google Scholar using Zotero Connector. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is a citation style? Explain the IEEE citation style with an example. Mention any two other popular citation styles. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Zotero — Definition and Procedure (7 Marks)** **Zotero** is a free, open-source reference management software developed by the **Corporation for Digital Scholarship** (originally the Roy Rosenzweig Center for History and New Media at George Mason University, USA). It enables researchers to collect, organize, cite, and share bibliographic references. It uses an open-source license (AGPLv3), supports 10,000+ citation styles, and has a strong plugin ecosystem (e.g., Better BibTeX, ZotFile, Zotero PDF Translate). **Step-by-Step Procedure to Add a Reference from Google Scholar Using Zotero Connector:** 1. **Step 1 — Install Zotero Desktop** from the official website (zotero.org) for your operating system (Windows/macOS/Linux). 2. **Step 2 — Install Zotero Connector** browser extension for your browser (Firefox, Chrome, Edge, or Safari). The icon appears in the browser toolbar. 3. **Step 3 — Open Google Scholar** (scholar.google.com) in your browser. Ensure Zotero Desktop is running in the background. 4. **Step 4 — Search for the desired article** using keywords. For example: *"deep learning transformer architecture"*. 5. **Step 5 — Click on the article title** in the search results to open the article's record page (which may include the publisher's website, PDF link, or the metadata view on Google Scholar). 6. **Step 6 — Click the Zotero Connector icon** in the browser toolbar. The icon shape changes based on the type of source detected (e.g., a document icon for articles). The metadata is automatically downloaded to your Zotero library, including title, authors, journal/conference, year, volume, issue, pages, DOI, and abstract. 7. **Step 7 — Verify the reference** in Zotero Desktop. Open the **Zotero Desktop** application → check the **"My Library"** collection → confirm the newly added reference. Edit any incorrect fields manually by clicking on them. 8. **Step 8 — Attach the PDF** (if not auto-downloaded): Drag and drop the PDF file onto the reference entry in Zotero. Zotero automatically renames the PDF based on the citation metadata. 9. **Step 9 — Organize** the reference into a collection: Right-click "My Library" → "New Collection" → name it (e.g., "Deep Learning 2024") → drag the reference into the new collection. 10. **Step 10 — Add tags and notes**: Select the reference → add tags in the right panel (e.g., "transformer," "NLP") → add a note summarizing the paper's key findings. --- **(b) Citation Style — IEEE and Others (7 Marks)** A **citation style** is a standardized set of formatting rules that dictate how in-text citations and bibliographic entries are presented in academic documents. Citation styles ensure consistency, clarity, and credibility in scholarly writing. They are defined using the **Citation Style Language (CSL)**, an open XML-based format. **IEEE Citation Style:** The **IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers) citation style** is widely used in engineering, computer science, and information technology publications. Its key features are: - **In-text citations** are presented as **numbers in square brackets**, in the order of first appearance in the text — e.g., `[1]`, `[2]`, `[3]`. Multiple citations are grouped as `[1], [3]` or `[1]–[3]`. - **Reference list** is numbered in the same order as the in-text citations. - Each reference includes: author initials and surname, paper title in quotation marks, journal/conference name in italics, volume, issue, page numbers, and year. **Example of IEEE-style in-text citation:** > Recent advances in deep learning have revolutionized image classification [1], [3], [5]. **Example of IEEE-style bibliography entry:** > [1] A. Krizhevsky, I. Sutskever, and G. E. Hinton, "ImageNet classification with deep convolutional neural networks," *Communications of the ACM*, vol. 60, no. 6, pp. 84–90, May 2017. **Two Other Popular Citation Styles:** 1. **APA 7th Edition (American Psychological Association):** Used in social sciences, education, and management. Uses **author-date in-text citations** — e.g., `(Smith, 2024)` — and a reference list sorted alphabetically by author's surname. Example entry: `Smith, J. (2024). Deep learning fundamentals. Springer.` 2. **Vancouver Style:** Used in biomedical and life sciences. Uses **numbered in-text citations** (similar to IEEE) but with a different bibliographic format. The reference list is in citation-number order, and journal titles are abbreviated in italics (following PubMed/MEDLINE conventions). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 4:** > > 1. **Confusing citation styles:** Many students mix APA and IEEE in the same bibliography. APA uses `(Author, Year)` and alphabetical bibliography. IEEE uses `[1]`, `[2]` and numbered bibliography. **Mixing them = -2 to -3 marks.** > > 2. **Omitting developer/organization names:** When listing RMS tools, students often write *"Mendeley — Elsevier"* correctly but forget that Zotero is from the **Corporation for Digital Scholarship** (not "Mozilla" — Mozilla only hosts the browser extension). Examiners deduct marks for incomplete ownership info. > > 3. **No workflow steps:** A 7-mark question like "Explain how to add a reference in Zotero" expects **numbered procedural steps** with at least 7-8 steps. Writing a paragraph without clear steps loses 2-3 marks. > > 4. **Forgetting citation style examples:** A question on "IEEE citation style" demands a **concrete in-text and bibliography example** — not just a description. Always include one example of each. > > 5. **Not stating cost/license:** Examiners specifically check whether you know Zotero is **free/open-source** and EndNote is **paid**. This is a 1-mark item that students frequently miss. > > 6. **Missing comparison table:** For "Compare X and Y" questions, a **tabular comparison** is mandatory. Writing the comparison in paragraph form only fetches partial marks. > > 7. **Ignoring LaTeX/BibTeX integration:** Examiners often award bonus marks for mentioning **Better BibTeX plugin** (Zotero) or **BibTeX export** (Mendeley) — which is critical for engineering thesis writing in LaTeX. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-minute revision before KTU exam — 60-second checklist:** - ✅ **Reference Management Software (RMS)** = tool to collect, organize, cite, and share bibliographic references. - ✅ **Mendeley** — owned by **Elsevier**, freemium, 2 GB free cloud, social network feature, "Mendeley Cite" Word plugin. - ✅ **Zotero** — free and open-source, by **Corporation for Digital Scholarship**, 300 MB free cloud, plugin ecosystem (Better BibTeX). - ✅ **EndNote** — paid, by **Clarivate Analytics**, deep integration with Web of Science, "Cite-While-You-Write (CWYW)" plugin. - ✅ **JabRef** — free BibTeX-native tool, popular with LaTeX users in engineering. - ✅ **RefWorks** — web-native, subscription-based, popular in academic libraries. - ✅ **Citation Style = CSL** (Citation Style Language, open XML standard). - ✅ **IEEE** = engineering/CS, numeric `[1]`, `[2]` format, ordered by first appearance. - ✅ **APA 7th** = social sciences, author-date `(Smith, 2024)`, alphabetical bibliography. - ✅ **Vancouver** = biomedical, numeric, PubMed-style abbreviated journal names. - ✅ **DOI** = Digital Object Identifier, format `10.xxxx/yyyyy`, enables one-click import. - ✅ **BibTeX** = LaTeX-native `.bib` file format with citation keys like `@article{key, ...}`. - ✅ **Five RMS stages** = Capture → Store → Organize → Cite → Share. - ✅ **Break-even analysis** = RMS saves time when n > 13 references (all KTU projects qualify). - ✅ **Word plugin** = "Mendeley Cite" or "Zotero Word Processor Plugin" — enables cite-while-you-write. - ✅ **Browser connector** = essential for one-click capture from Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, PubMed, Scopus. - ✅ **Collaboration** = Group libraries (Mendeley) / Group Libraries (Zotero) for shared research. - ✅ **PDF reader** = Mendeley and Zotero have built-in readers with annotations; EndNote has tabbed reader; JabRef uses external reader. - ✅ **Open source** = Zotero, JabRef. **Closed source** = Mendeley, EndNote, RefWorks. - ✅ **Mobile apps** = Mendeley has full iOS/Android apps; Zotero is iOS read-only. - ✅ **For KTU B.Tech projects** (MP1, MP2): use IEEE style for CS/EE/ECE/Civil/Mechanical; use APA for management/humanities. - ✅ **Always state the developer, license model, and one unique feature** for any RMS mentioned in the exam.

topics.title: Intellectual Property Rights – Copyright and Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intellectual Property Rights – Copyright and Patents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) — The Master Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** are the legally recognized exclusive rights granted to creators, inventors, and owners of original intellectual creations (inventions, literary and artistic works, designs, symbols, names, and images) for a specified period, enabling them to control the commercial use, distribution, and reproduction of their intangible creations.

These rights are intangible because they apply to **creations of the human mind** — the property does not exist as a physical object, but the law treats it as property that can be bought, sold, licensed, or inherited.

**Real-World Analogy — The "Invisible Fence" for Ideas:**
Imagine you spend three years and a crore of rupees developing a unique solar panel coating that triples efficiency. The day you publish your research, a Chinese factory could legally copy your formula and sell it cheaper, leaving you bankrupt. **IPR is the invisible legal fence** the government builds around your idea for a specific period, giving you the **monopoly right** to exclusively make, use, or sell your creation — and to sue anyone who trespasses. Without this fence, no rational person would invest in innovation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Two Pillars of IPR to Master:**
> 1. **Copyright** (protection of expressive works)
> 2. **Patents** (protection of functional inventions)
> Trademarks, Trade Secrets, Industrial Designs, and Geographical Indications are **out of syllabus scope** but are referenced for context.

---

### 1.2 Copyright — Definition & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Copyright** is a form of intellectual property protection granted to the **authors of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works**, including computer software, databases, and cinematographic films. It protects the **expression of an idea**, not the idea itself.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Author's Fingerprint":**
Think of a cookbook. The chef invents a unique recipe (the *idea*) — that idea is not copyrightable. But the moment the chef **writes the recipe in a book with specific instructions, photos, and personal stories**, the **written expression** is stamped with the author's "fingerprint." Copyright automatically attaches to that fingerprint the moment pen meets paper. Nobody can photocopy and resell the book without permission — but they are free to cook the same recipe in their own kitchen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Facts on Copyright:**
> - **Governing Law in India:** The **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended in 2012).
> - **Duration (India):** Lifetime of the author + **60 years**.
> - **Works Covered:** Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematograph films, sound recordings, computer programs.
> - **Automatic Protection:** No registration is mandatory; copyright arises the moment the work is created and fixed in a tangible medium.

---

### 1.3 Patent — Definition & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Patent** is an exclusive statutory right granted by the government to an **inventor** for a **novel, non-obvious, and industrially applicable invention**, conferring the right to exclude others from making, using, selling, or importing the patented invention for a limited period in exchange for full public disclosure of the invention.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Time-Limited Trade Secret":**
Imagine a medieval guild of sword-makers. The master smith discovers a secret tempering process that produces unbreakable blades. In modern times, that smith can **apply for a patent** — in exchange for **publicly publishing** the entire secret recipe in a government gazette, the government gives him a **20-year legal monopoly**: no competitor may legally use that tempering process. After 20 years, the secret becomes part of the public domain, enriching society forever. **The inventor trades secrecy for time-limited monopoly + public disclosure.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Facts on Patents:**
> - **Governing Law in India:** The **Patents Act, 1970** (latest amendment 2005, which introduced product patents in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals).
> - **Duration:** **20 years** from the date of filing the application (non-renewable, non-extendable).
> - **Territory:** Patents are **territorial** — an Indian patent gives rights only within India.
> - **Inventions NOT Patentable in India:** Atomic energy, frivolous inventions, inventions contrary to public order/morality, discoveries of scientific principles, methods of agriculture/horticulture, plants/animals (other than micro-organisms), mathematical/business methods, computer programs *per se*, literary/dramatic/musical works, schemes/mental acts, traditional knowledge, inventions requiring use of biological material from India.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Two Branches of Copyright Rights

Copyright in India is split into two distinct sub-rights under Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957:

1. **Economic Rights (Section 14):**
   - Right to reproduce the work
   - Right to issue copies to the public
   - Right to perform the work in public
   - Right to communicate the work to the public
   - Right to make cinematograph film or sound recording
   - Right to translate or adapt the work
   These rights are **transferable** through assignment, licensing, or inheritance.

2. **Moral Rights (Section 57 — Author's Special Rights):**
   - Right of **paternity** — claim authorship of the work
   - Right of **integrity** — object to any distortion, mutilation, or modification that prejudices the author's honour or reputation
   - Moral rights are **perpetual, inalienable, and not assignable** — they remain with the author even after copyright is assigned to a publisher.

### 2.2 Patentability Criteria — The "Three Pillars of Patentability"

For an invention to be granted a patent under the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (Section 2(1)(j)), it must satisfy **all three** of the following:

| # | Criterion | Definition | Common Pitfall |
|---|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Novelty** | The invention must be **new** — not known or published anywhere in the world before the priority date. | Publishing a paper or presenting at a conference **before** filing destroys novelty. |
| 2 | **Non-Obviousness (Inventive Step)** | The invention must not be obvious to a **person skilled in the art (PSITA)**. | Combining two known devices without unexpected technical effect fails. |
| 3 | **Industrial Application (Utility)** | The invention must be capable of being made or used in an **industry or agriculture**. | A pure scientific principle or abstract theory fails. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exclusions from Patentability (Section 3 & 4 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970):**
> Inventions contrary to law/public order/morality, atomic energy, discoveries, scientific principles, plants/animals (except microorganisms), methods of treatment, mathematical methods, business methods, computer programs *per se*, schemes/rules/methods of mental acts, traditional knowledge, and **pre-grant opposition by any person** is permitted.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet — Copyright vs. Patent

| Parameter | **Copyright** | **Patent** |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Governing Statute** | Copyright Act, **1957** | Patents Act, **1970** |
| **Subject Matter** | Expression of ideas (literary, artistic, musical) | Functional inventions (process, product, machine) |
| **What is Protected** | The **form of expression** | The **technical idea/function** |
| **What is NOT Protected** | The underlying idea, method, or fact | The mere expression of the invention in a document |
| **Rights Granted** | Right to reproduce, distribute, perform, communicate, adapt | Right to **exclude others** from making, using, selling, importing |
| **Registration Required?** | **No** — automatic upon creation | **Yes** — mandatory application to Patent Office |
| **Term of Protection** | **Life of author + 60 years** | **20 years** from filing date |
| **Authority (India)** | Copyright Office, Department of Higher Education | Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM) |
| **Applicability to Software** | **Yes** (as literary work) | **Generally No** (as computer program *per se*) |
| **Transferability** | Assignable + licensable (economic rights only) | Assignable + licensable (with registration at Patent Office) |
| **International Mechanism** | **Berne Convention** (1886) | **Paris Convention** (1883) + **PCT** (Patent Cooperation Treaty, 1970) |
| **Time Limit for Filing** | Must be within lifetime + 60 years; registration optional | Must be filed within **12 months** of first publication for priority claim |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing that copyright protects "ideas." This is **WRONG** and costs 2 marks. Always state that copyright protects the **"expression of ideas," not the ideas themselves.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of IPR

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Patents drive R&D investment in new drug molecules (e.g., Pfizer's COVID-19 vaccine patent).
- **Software Industry:** Copyright protects source code; open-source licenses (GPL, MIT) are contractual IPR frameworks.
- **Biotechnology:** Patents on CRISPR gene-editing, Bt-cotton seeds, etc.
- **Academic Research:** Researchers must file patents **before** publishing, because publication destroys novelty (a KTU viva favourite).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> Since this module is **non-mathematical** (law-based conceptual content), Section 3 provides **exhaustive step-by-step procedural flows** for both Patent Granting and Copyright Registration procedures, which are the most commonly asked KTU 14-mark questions.

---

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Patent Application Procedure (India)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Authority:** Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM) under the Patents Act, 1970, with Patent Offices at Kolkata (Head Office), Delhi, Mumbai, and Chennai.

**Step 1 — Determination of Patentability**
The inventor conducts a **prior-art search** through public databases (Indian Patent Database: https://ipindiaservices.gov.in, WIPO PATENTSCOPE, Google Patents, Espacenet) to ensure the invention is novel, non-obvious, and industrially applicable. A weak search guarantees rejection.

**Step 2 — Drafting the Patent Specification**
The applicant prepares a **Patent Specification** (the technical + legal document) which contains:
- **Title of Invention**
- **Field of Invention**
- **Background / Prior Art**
- **Summary of Invention**
- **Detailed Description** with drawings
- **Claims** — the **most legally critical** part defining the scope of protection
- **Abstract**

**Step 3 — Filing the Application**
The application is filed in the **prescribed Form 1** (Application for Grant of Patent) along with **Form 2** (Provisional or Complete Specification), **Form 3** (Statement and Undertaking regarding foreign applications), **Form 5** (Declaration of Inventorship), and **Form 26** (Power of Attorney, if filed through a patent agent). The filing date becomes the **priority date** — critical for novelty assessment.

**Step 4 — Publication (Form 18)**
After **18 months** from the priority date (or earlier on request in Form 18), the application is published in the Patent Office Journal. Upon publication, the **provisional rights** under Section 11-A crystallize, and any infringer becomes liable for damages and account of profits from publication onwards.

**Step 5 — Request for Examination (Form 18)**
The applicant must file a request for examination within **48 months** of the priority date, otherwise the application is treated as withdrawn. The application is then examined by a **Patent Examiner** in the relevant technology domain.

**Step 6 — First Examination Report (FER) & Response**
The examiner issues a **First Examination Report (FER)** under Section 12, citing prior art and objecting to claims. The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable to a maximum of 12 months), overcoming each objection by **amendment of claims, arguments, or distinguishing the prior art**.

**Step 7 — Pre-Grant Opposition (Section 25(1))**
After publication, **any interested party** may file a pre-grant opposition by submitting a representation + evidence. This is a KTU viva favourite — India uniquely allows this.

**Step 8 — Grant of Patent (Section 43)**
If all objections are overcome, the patent is granted and published in the Patent Office Journal. A **Certificate of Patent** is issued, and the **Seal of the Patent Office** is affixed. From this date, the **20-year statutory term** begins (the term is counted from the priority date, not the grant date).

**Step 9 — Renewal**
Patents must be renewed annually after the **2nd year** (Form 8), with a 6-month grace period + surcharge.

**Step 10 — Infringement Enforcement**
The patent holder may sue for infringement in a District Court or High Court for injunction, damages, or account of profits.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Copyright Registration Procedure (India — Optional but Advisable)

**Step 1 — Creation of Work**
Work must be original and fixed in a tangible medium. Copyright arises **automatically** — registration is **not mandatory** under Indian law, but is recommended for evidentiary value in infringement suits.

**Step 2 — Filing of Application to Copyright Office**
Application filed in **Form XIV** (for literary/dramatic/musical/artistic works) along with:
- Three copies of the work
- Statement of Particulars
- Statement of Further Particulars
- Power of Attorney (if filed through an agent)
- Prescribed fees

**Step 3 — Examination by Registrar**
The Registrar of Copyrights examines the application for compliance with the Copyright Act, 1957 and the Copyright Rules, 2013.

**Step 4 — Issuance of Extracts**
Once the application is in order, the Registrar issues **extracts** to the applicant, inviting **objections from the public within 30 days** in Form V.

**Step 5 — Hearing and Disposal**
If objections are received, a hearing is held. Otherwise, the Registrar enters the copyright in the **Register of Copyrights**.

**Step 6 — Certificate of Registration**
A **Certificate of Registration** is issued under Section 49 of the Copyright Act, 1957. This certificate serves as **prima facie evidence** in any court proceeding.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Rights, Term, and Infringement Comparison

| Dimension | Copyright Infringement | Patent Infringement |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Statute** | Section 51, Copyright Act, 1957 | Section 104, Patents Act, 1970 |
| **Definition of Infringement** | Doing or abetting any act exclusively reserved to the copyright owner **without licence/assignment** | Any manufacture, use, sale, offer for sale, or import of the patented invention **without patent holder's consent** |
| **Defences** | Fair dealing (research, criticism, review, news reporting); statutory licences; first sale doctrine | Prior use; experimental use; Bolar exemption (generic drug trials); invalidity of patent |
| **Remedies (Civil)** | Injunction, damages, account of profits, delivery-up of infringing copies | Injunction, damages or account of profits, delivery-up |
| **Criminal Liability** | Imprisonment (6 months–3 years) + fine (₹50,000–₹2,00,000) under Section 63 | **No criminal liability** (only civil, unlike USA) |
| **Limitation Period** | 3 years from knowledge of infringement | 3 years from cause of action |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip — KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:**
> "Explain the procedure for grant of patent in India" is the **most frequently repeated 14-mark question**. Use the 10-step structure above with **statutory section numbers** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `step1`, `n1`, `appNode`) with letter prefixes. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as node IDs. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text only.

---

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Patent Granting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Inventor Discovers Invention] --> B[Step 1: Prior Art Search in Patent Databases]
    B --> C{Nov NonObs Industrial Applicable?}
    C -- NO --> Z[STOP: Reject or Modify Invention]
    C -- YES --> D[Step 2: Draft Patent Specification Form 1 Form 2 Form 3 Form 5 Form 26]
    D --> E[Step 3: File Application at Patent Office Priority Date Assigned]
    E --> F[Step 4: Publication in Patent Journal After 18 Months Section 11A]
    F --> G[Step 5: File Request for Examination Form 18 Within 48 Months]
    G --> H[Step 6: Patent Examiner Issues First Examination Report FER Section 12]
    H --> I{All Objections Overcome?}
    I -- NO --> J[Applicant Files Response to FER Within 6 Months Extendable to 12 Months]
    J --> I
    I -- YES --> K[Step 7: Pre Grant Opposition Window Section 25 Any Person]
    K --> L{Opposition Filed?}
    L -- YES --> M[Opposition Hearing Before Controller]
    M --> N{Controller Allows Patent?}
    N -- NO --> Z
    L -- NO --> O[Step 8: Grant of Patent Section 43 Certificate Issued]
    N -- YES --> O
    O --> P[Step 9: Annual Renewal From 2nd Year Form 8]
    P --> Q[Step 10: Enforcement of Rights Against Infringers Section 104]
    Q --> R[END: Patent Term 20 Years from Priority Date]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR Classification)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IPR[INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS IPR]
    IPR --> COPY[Copyright]
    IPR --> PAT[Patent]
    IPR --> TM[Trademark]
    IPR --> TS[Trade Secret]
    IPR --> ID[Industrial Design]
    IPR --> GI[Geographical Indication]
    COPY --> COPY1[Literary Works]
    COPY --> COPY2[Artistic Works]
    COPY --> COPY3[Musical Works]
    COPY --> COPY4[Dramatic Works]
    COPY --> COPY5[Cinematograph Films]
    COPY --> COPY6[Sound Recordings]
    COPY --> COPY7[Computer Software]
    PAT --> PAT1[Product Patent]
    PAT --> PAT2[Process Patent]
    PAT1 --> PAT1A[Machinery Device Substance]
    PAT2 --> PAT2A[Method of Manufacturing]
    PAT1A --> EXCL1[Non Patentable Atomic Energy Plants Animals Traditional Knowledge]
    PAT2A --> EXCL1
    COPY1 --> EXCL2[Idea Itself Not Protected Only Expression Protected]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Copyright vs Patent Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Creation of Work or Invention] --> B{Type of IP?}
    B -- EXPRESSION --> C[Copyright Path]
    B -- FUNCTION --> D[Patent Path]
    C --> C1[Automatic Protection on Creation]
    C1 --> C2[Optional Registration at Copyright Office Form XIV]
    C2 --> C3[Term: Life of Author PLUS 60 Years]
    C3 --> C4[Public Domain after Term Expiry]
    D --> D1[No Protection without Filing Application]
    D1 --> D2[Examination at Indian Patent Office]
    D2 --> D3[Grant of Patent Certificate]
    D3 --> D4[Term: 20 Years from Priority Date]
    D4 --> D5[Public Domain after Term Expiry]
    C4 --> END1[END: Free Use by Public]
    D5 --> END1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Copyright and Patent.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis of Difference | Copyright | Patent |
|---------------------|-----------|--------|
| Subject Matter | Protects the **expression** of ideas (literary, artistic, musical) | Protects a **functional invention** (product or process) |
| Registration | **Automatic** upon creation (registration optional) | **Mandatory** — must file application and obtain grant |
| Duration | **Life of author + 60 years** | **20 years** from priority date |
| Statute | Copyright Act, **1957** | Patents Act, **1970** |

*(1 mark for each correct difference — minimum 3 distinctions required for full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State any three inventions that are not patentable under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Under Section 3 & 4 of the Patents Act, 1970, the following are **not patentable** in India:
1. Inventions contrary to public order or morality (Section 3(b)).
2. **Discoveries** of any scientific principle or the formulation of a mere theory (Section 3(c)).
3. **Methods of agriculture or horticulture** (Section 3(h)).
4. Plants and animals in whole or in part thereof, other than micro-organisms, and essentially biological processes (Section 3(j)).
5. Mathematical or business methods, and computer programs *per se* (Section 3(k)).
6. Literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic works (Section 3(p)).

*(Any 3 correct points × 1 mark each = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 4]**

**(a) Explain in detail the procedure for grant of a patent in India under the Patents Act, 1970. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The procedure for grant of a patent in India is governed by the **Patents Act, 1970** and the **Patent Rules, 2003** administered by the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks (CGPDTM) under the Department of Industrial Policy and Promotion, Ministry of Commerce and Industry.

**Step 1 — Determination of Patentability (Prior-Art Search):**
Before filing, the applicant must ensure the invention satisfies the **three pillars of patentability** — novelty, inventive step (non-obviousness), and industrial application. A thorough prior-art search is conducted using the **Indian Patent Database, WIPO PATENTSCOPE, Espacenet, and Google Patents**.

**Step 2 — Drafting of Patent Specification:**
The applicant prepares a patent specification in **Form 2** (either **Provisional Specification** — to establish priority date for further development — or **Complete Specification** — final technical and legal document containing the title, field, background, summary, detailed description, drawings, and **claims** — the legally enforceable boundary of protection).

**Step 3 — Filing of Application:**
The application is filed in **Form 1** along with Form 2, Form 3 (foreign applications undertaking), Form 5 (declaration of inventorship), and Form 26 (Power of Attorney if filed through a patent agent) either in physical form or online through the e-filing portal of the Indian Patent Office. The **date of receipt** of the application becomes the **priority date** — the reference point for novelty assessment.

**Step 4 — Publication of Application:**
Under Section 11A, the application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the priority date. Early publication can be requested via Form 18. Upon publication, the applicant acquires **provisional rights** — if any infringer commences activities after publication but before grant, the applicant may claim damages or account of profits upon grant.

**Step 5 — Request for Examination:**
The applicant files a **Request for Examination (Form 18)** within **48 months** of the priority date. Failure to do so results in the application being treated as **withdrawn**.

**Step 6 — First Examination Report (FER):**
The examiner issues the FER under **Section 12** citing prior art and raising objections to novelty, inventive step, industrial applicability, clarity, or patentability. The applicant must **respond within 6 months** (extendable up to 12 months), overcoming objections by **amending claims or providing arguments distinguishing the prior art**.

**Step 7 — Pre-Grant Opposition:**
After publication, **any interested person** can file a **pre-grant opposition** under **Section 25(1)** by submitting a written representation and evidence. This is a unique feature of Indian patent law.

**Step 8 — Grant of Patent:**
If no objections remain, the patent is **granted under Section 43** and recorded in the **Register of Patents**. A Certificate of Patent is issued bearing the Seal of the Patent Office. The patent is then published in the Patent Journal.

**Step 9 — Annual Renewal:**
The patent must be renewed annually from the **2nd year onwards** by paying the prescribed renewal fee (Form 8), with a 6-month grace period subject to surcharge.

---

**(b) Discuss the rights of a patent holder and the remedies available for patent infringement in India. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Rights of a Patent Holder under the Patents Act, 1970:**

A patent grant confers on the patentee the **exclusive right** under **Section 48** to:
1. **Use the invention** exclusively for the term of the patent.
2. **Exclude third parties** from making, using, offering for sale, selling, or importing the patented product or product obtained directly by the patented process.
3. **Assign** the patent (transfer ownership).
4. **License** the patent (grant permission to others under terms).
5. **Sue for infringement** in a court of competent jurisdiction.
6. **Right to claim priority** under the Paris Convention (within 12 months of first filing in a convention country).
7. **Right to surrender** or **revoke the patent** (limited grounds).

These rights are **territorial** — limited to India, and last for **20 years** from the priority date.

**Infringement of Patent (Section 104):**
A person infringes a patent if he makes, uses, exercises, sells, or imports the patented invention **without the consent of the patentee**.

**Remedies for Patent Infringement (Civil — under the Patents Act, 1970 read with the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908):**

1. **Interlocutory / Permanent Injunction (Section 108):** Court orders the infringer to stop the infringing acts.
2. **Damages or Account of Profits:** Monetary compensation, which may be compensatory or punitive in cases of flagrant infringement.
3. **Order for Delivery-Up** of infringing goods and their destruction.
4. **Declaration of Infringement.**
5. **Certificate of Contested Validity** (Section 111(5)) — if the defendant unsuccessfully contests validity, the patentee is awarded costs.
6. **Revocation (Counter-claim):** The defendant may file a counter-claim for revocation under Section 64 — on grounds of lack of novelty, inventive step, industrial application, false suggestion, non-disclosure, or that the invention was obtained in breach of contract.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** India does **not** provide criminal liability for patent infringement (unlike copyright infringement under Section 63 of the Copyright Act, 1957).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 4]**

**(a) Define Copyright. Explain the works protected and the rights conferred under the Copyright Act, 1957. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
**Copyright** is a bundle of exclusive statutory rights granted under the **Copyright Act, 1957** to the creators of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, sound recordings, cinematograph films, and computer software, to control the use of their works for a specified period. It protects the **expression of ideas**, not the ideas themselves.

**Works Protected (Section 13):**

1. **Original literary works** — books, articles, computer programs, tables, compilations, databases.
2. **Dramatic works** — plays, screenplays, choreographic works (notate or recorded).
3. **Musical works** — compositions with or without words.
4. **Artistic works** — paintings, sculptures, drawings, engravings, photographs, architectural works.
5. **Cinematograph films** — visual recordings with synchronized sound.
6. **Sound recordings** — independent of underlying composition.
7. **Government works** — government publications, official tables, laws.
8. **International works** — under Berne Convention and Universal Copyright Convention.

**Rights Conferred (Section 14 — Economic Rights):**

1. **Right to reproduce** the work in any material form.
2. **Right to issue copies** to the public.
3. **Right to perform** the work in public.
4. **Right to communicate** the work to the public (broadcast, webcast).
5. **Right to make cinematograph film or sound recording.**
6. **Right to translate or adapt** the work.
7. **Right to sell, give on hire, or commercially circulate** the work.

**Moral Rights (Section 57 — Author's Special Rights):**
1. **Right of paternity** — to claim authorship.
2. **Right of integrity** — to object to distortion, mutilation, or modification that prejudices honour or reputation.
- These rights are **perpetual, inalienable, and not assignable** — they remain with the author.

**Term of Copyright (Section 22–29):**
- **Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works:** Lifetime of the author + **60 years**.
- **Cinematograph films, sound recordings, photographs, posthumous works:** **60 years from the date of publication**.

---

**(b) Discuss the procedure for copyright registration and the remedies for copyright infringement in India. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure for Copyright Registration (Optional in India — Advisory):**

Although copyright arises **automatically** upon creation under Section 13, registration is recommended for evidentiary value. The procedure under the **Copyright Rules, 2013** is as follows:

**Step 1 — Filing of Application:**
The applicant files **Form XIV** along with **three copies of the work**, Statement of Particulars, Statement of Further Particulars, Power of Attorney (if filed through a registered copyright agent), and the **prescribed fee** at the **Copyright Office** (New Delhi).

**Step 2 — Examination:**
The **Registrar of Copyrights** examines the application for compliance with the Copyright Act and Rules, checking for completeness and originality.

**Step 3 — Publication of Extracts:**
If the application is in order, the Registrar issues **extracts** of the work and invites **public objections within 30 days** in **Form V**.

**Step 4 — Hearing and Decision:**
If objections are received, a hearing is held; otherwise, the Registrar enters the copyright in the **Register of Copyrights** and issues the **Certificate of Registration** under **Section 49** — which is **prima facie evidence** of the particulars entered therein in any court proceeding.

**Copyright Infringement and Remedies (Section 51–62):**

**Infringement (Section 51):** Doing or abetting any act exclusively reserved to the copyright owner **without licence or assignment**.

**Civil Remedies:**
1. **Injunction** (temporary, perpetual, interlocutory).
2. **Damages** (compensatory + additional damages for infringement of the right of paternity or integrity).
3. **Account of profits.**
4. **Order for delivery-up** of infringing copies for destruction.
5. **Anton Pillar Order** (search and seizure of infringing goods).

**Criminal Remedies (Section 63–70):**
- **Imprisonment:** Minimum 6 months extendable to **3 years** (and up to 7 years for second/subsequent offence).
- **Fine:** Minimum ₹50,000 extendable to **₹2,00,000** (and up to ₹10,00,000 for second/subsequent offence).
- **Search and seizure** of infringing copies.

**Defences:** Fair dealing (Section 52) for research, criticism, review, news reporting, education, judicial proceedings, and legitimate public interest.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully — KTU Examiner's Pet Penalties):**
> 1. **Writing "ideas are protected by copyright"** — **WRONG.** Always write "the **expression of ideas** is protected." Loss = **1 mark**.
> 2. **Writing that copyright is granted for "100 years"** — **WRONG** in India. The term is **Lifetime + 60 years**. Loss = **1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing that patents last "30 years"** — **WRONG** in India. The correct term is **20 years from priority date**. Loss = **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting to cite Section numbers** (e.g., "Section 48 of Patents Act 1970" or "Section 14 of Copyright Act 1957") — Section numbers are the **gold standard of statutory answers**. Loss = up to **2 marks**.
> 5. **Conflating "Patent" with "Trademark" or "Trade Secret"** — these are out-of-syllabus but examiners will deduct marks if you mix them up. Loss = **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Writing that patent grants "right to use"** — **WRONG.** A patent grants the **right to EXCLUDE others** (negative right), not the positive right to use (the patentee may still be blocked by other patents). Loss = **1 mark**.
> 7. **Failing to mention "automatic copyright"** vs. "mandatory patent registration" — examiners expect this contrast. Loss = **1 mark**.
> 8. **In a 14-mark question, skipping either pre-grant opposition or the FER response** in the patent procedure — both are mandatory KTU sub-headings.

---
