### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IPR Definition:** Legally recognized exclusive rights to creators of intangible intellectual works.
- **Copyright** protects the **expression** of ideas in literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematographic, and software works.
- **Copyright arises automatically** upon creation and fixation — **registration is optional** in India.
- **Copyright Term in India:** Life of the author + **60 years** (not 100, not 50, not lifetime only).
- **Statute:** Copyright Act, **1957** (as amended in **2012**).
- **Two branches:** Economic rights (Section 14, assignable) and Moral rights (Section 57, inalienable, perpetual).
- **Fair Dealing (Section 52):** Defends research, criticism, review, news, education use.
- **Patent** protects a **novel, non-obvious, and industrially applicable** invention — a positive, time-limited government-granted monopoly.
- **Patent Statutory Requirements:** Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application.
- **Patent Term:** **20 years** from the **priority date** (not grant date).
- **Statute:** Patents Act, **1970** (amended in **2005** to allow product patents in pharma and agrochemicals).
- **Patent Office:** Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM), Kolkata HQ.
- **Forms:** Form 1 (application), Form 2 (specification), Form 5 (inventorship), Form 18 (examination request).
- **Pre-Grant Opposition** (Section 25(1)) — any person, after publication — **unique to India**.
- **FER (First Examination Report)** under Section 12 — response within 6 months (extendable to 12 months).
- **Computer programs per se** are **NOT patentable** (Section 3(k)) but ARE protected as **literary works under Copyright**.
- **Criminal liability** exists for copyright infringement (Section 63) but **NOT for patent infringement** in India.
- **International Treaties:** Berne Convention (1886) for copyright; Paris Convention (1883) and PCT (1970) for patents.
- **Territoriality:** Both copyright and patent rights are **territorial** — Indian rights apply only in India.
- **First-to-File Principle:** Indian patent law follows the **first-to-file** rule (not first-to-invent).
- **Priority Date:** The **filing date** of the earliest application — used for novelty assessment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Codes of Ethics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Codes of Ethics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# The Codes of Ethics — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Research Methodology and Statistics** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, a **Code of Ethics** is defined as a formally documented, systematically structured, and institutionally ratified set of moral principles, professional standards, and procedural guidelines that govern the conduct of research. It prescribes acceptable practices, proscribes unethical behaviours, and provides a normative framework against which the actions of researchers, reviewers, editors, and institutions can be ethically evaluated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Pinned):**
> A *Code of Ethics* is a written, public statement of the moral standards by which members of a profession or discipline agree to conduct their research, communicate findings, and treat human/animal/environmental subjects. It functions as a *moral constitution* for the research enterprise.

The plural form — **Codes of Ethics** — refers to the cumulative, evolving body of international, national, and disciplinary codes that have been codified over the last century in response to historical atrocities, scientific frauds, and the increasing complexity of modern research.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine research without ethics as **driving without traffic rules on a highway**:
- Vehicles (researchers) would move in any direction at any speed.
- Pedestrians (participants/society) would be unsafe.
- Collisions (research harms) would be uninsurable, unpunishable, and inevitable.

A **Code of Ethics is the traffic rulebook** of research — it tells every researcher:
1. *Where* the lanes are (boundaries of conduct),
2. *When* to stop (consent checkpoints), and
3. *What penalties* apply for jumping signals (misconduct sanctions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as traffic rules do not eliminate accidents but reduce them and provide accountability after they occur, codes of ethics do not guarantee ethical behaviour — they establish a *shared normative reference* and a *mechanism for redress*.

## 1.3 Why Codes of Ethics Exist — Historical Necessity

The modern codes of ethics were not created in a vacuum. They emerged from documented tragedies:

| Historical Trigger | Year | Outcome — Code Produced |
|---|---|---|
| Nazi medical experiments on prisoners | 1945–46 | **The Nuremberg Code** |
| Tuskegee Syphilis Study (USA) | 1932–1972 | **The Belmont Report** |
| Thalidomide tragedy | 1962 | Strengthened **Declaration of Helsinki** |
| Hwang Woo-suk stem-cell fraud | 2004–05 | Reinforced **Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE)** |

## 1.4 Core Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

- **Autonomy** — Respecting the right of individuals to make informed decisions about participation.
- **Beneficence** — Obligation to do good; **Non-maleficence** — obligation to do no harm.
- **Justice** — Fair distribution of research burdens and benefits across populations.
- **Informed Consent** — A subject's voluntary agreement to participate, given after full disclosure.
- **Plagiarism** — Misappropriation of another's intellectual work, ideas, or expressions.
- **Fabrication** — Inventing data or results and recording or reporting them as if real.
- **Falsification** — Manipulating research materials, equipment, or processes to misrepresent results.
- **Conflict of Interest (COI)** — A situation in which competing interests could improperly influence professional judgment.
- **IRB / IEC / IAEC** — Institutional Review Board / Independent Ethics Committee / Institutional Animal Ethics Committee.
- **Scholarly Misconduct** — Any behaviour that violates accepted ethical standards in the academic and research community.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly expects students to differentiate between *codes of ethics* (philosophical foundations) and *ethical guidelines / regulations* (operational rules). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# The Codes of Ethics — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Major Codes of Ethics — A Conceptual Walkthrough

### 2.1.1 The Nuremberg Code (1947)

The **Nuremberg Code** is the *first* internationally recognized code of research ethics. It was articulated by the judges of the Nuremberg Military Tribunal during the trial of Nazi physicians. It contains **10 principles**, the foremost being:

- **Principle 1** — *Absolute voluntary consent* of the human subject is essential.
- **Principle 2** — The experiment must yield fruitful results *unprocurable by other methods*.
- **Principle 3** — Animal experimentation must precede human trials.
- **Principle 9** — Subjects must be free to withdraw *at any time* during the experiment.

> [!NOTE]
> The Nuremberg Code is the *moral grandparent* of all subsequent codes. Its central idea — *informed, voluntary, revocable consent* — is the cornerstone of modern biomedical ethics.

### 2.1.2 The Declaration of Helsinki (1964, last revised 2013)

Adopted by the **World Medical Association (WMA)**, this declaration expanded the Nuremberg Code to address physician-led research. It introduced:

- The principle that *the well-being of the human subject should take precedence over the interests of science and society*.
- Distinction between **therapeutic** and **non-therapeutic** research.
- Provisions for *independent ethics committee review*.
- Requirement that research with *vulnerable populations* requires special justification.

### 2.1.3 The Belmont Report (1979)

Issued by the U.S. **National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research**, it distilled three foundational principles:

1. **Respect for Persons** — Treat individuals as autonomous agents; protect those with diminished autonomy.
2. **Beneficence** — Do not harm; maximize benefits and minimize possible harms.
3. **Justice** — Fair distribution of burdens and benefits of research.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Belmont Report shifted ethical discourse from *rules* (Nuremberg) to *principles* (a more flexible, widely applicable framework). This is a **favourite KTU Part-A question**.

### 2.1.4 The APA Ethical Principles (American Psychological Association)

The APA Ethics Code (latest major revision **2017**, with amendments through 2024) contains **5 general principles**:

| Principle | Core Demand on the Researcher |
|---|---|
| **Beneficence and Non-maleficence** | Promote well-being; protect from harm |
| **Fidelity and Responsibility** | Build trustworthy relationships |
| **Integrity** | Honest, accurate communication |
| **Justice** | Fair, impartial treatment |
| **Respect for People's Rights and Dignity** | Honour autonomy, privacy, self-determination |

### 2.1.5 ICMR Code of Ethics (India — 2017)

The **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) National Guidelines for Biomedical and Health Research Involving Human Participants** is the *operative* code within India. It lays down:

- **12 general principles** including essentiality, voluntariness, non-exploitation, privacy, accountability, transparency, and totality of responsibility.
- **Procedural mandates** — ethics committee registration, SOPs, audit trails, SAE (Serious Adverse Event) reporting.
- **Special protections** for children, pregnant women, cognitively impaired persons, and economically/educationally disadvantaged groups.

### 2.1.6 Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE) Guidelines

COPE provides the de facto code for *publication-stage* ethics. Its core concerns include:

- Authorship disputes
- Duplicate submission / redundant publication
- Plagiarism detection and remediation
- Conflicts of interest disclosure
- Peer-review confidentiality
- Retraction procedures

## 2.2 Common Underlying Principles Across All Codes

Although different codes use different wording, their philosophical foundations converge on a *common ethical core*. The table below consolidates these principles — this is the **single most important synthesis** for KTU answers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — The 8 Universal Principles

| S.No. | Universal Principle | Operational Meaning in Research | Codes That Enshrine It |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Respect for Autonomy** | Voluntary, informed consent; right to withdraw | Nuremberg, Helsinki, Belmont, APA, ICMR |
| 2 | **Beneficence** | Researcher must actively promote participant welfare | Belmont, Helsinki, APA, ICMR |
| 3 | **Non-maleficence** | "Do no harm" — physical, psychological, social | Helsinki, APA, ICMR |
| 4 | **Justice / Fairness** | Equitable selection of subjects; equitable distribution of benefits | Belmont, APA, ICMR |
| 5 | **Integrity / Honesty** | Truthful reporting; no fabrication, falsification, or plagiarism | All codes + COPE |
| 6 | **Confidentiality / Privacy** | Protecting participant data and identities | All codes |
| 7 | **Accountability** | Researchers are answerable to ethics committees, institutions, and the public | Helsinki, ICMR, COPE |
| 8 | **Transparency / Disclosure** | Open declaration of methods, funding, conflicts of interest | APA, ICMR, COPE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "R-B-N-J-I-C-A-T":** *Respect, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice, Integrity, Confidentiality, Accountability, Transparency*.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Technology Research

Although codes of ethics originate in *biomedical* research, they are now central to every KTU engineering discipline:

- **Computer Science** — Ethical use of AI, data-mining consent, algorithmic fairness, open-source licensing ethics.
- **Electrical / Electronics** — IRB oversight of human-subject studies involving EEG, biometric sensors, wearables.
- **Mechanical / Civil** — Ethical responsibility for safety-critical structural, automotive, and aerospace testing.
- **Chemical** — Environmental impact assessment and responsible handling of dual-use research.

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, the *Codes of Ethics* topic directly serves **Course Outcome CO4** of PEFTT755 — *"Apply ethical principles and professional codes of ethics in research planning, conduct, and dissemination."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# The Codes of Ethics — Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework

## 3.1 Analytical Framework — How a Code of Ethics Operates in Practice

A code of ethics is not a static document; it operates as a **four-stage cycle**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step walk-through.

### Stage 1 — Inception (Triggered by a Moral Crisis)

A moral crisis — historical atrocity, fraud, or societal harm — exposes the absence of an enforceable ethical framework.

*Example:* The horrors revealed at the Doctors' Trial (1946) → Nuremberg Code.

### Stage 2 — Codification (Drafting the Code)

A body of experts (international tribunal, professional association, national council) drafts a *formal, written* document specifying:

- Scope (what research it applies to)
- Principles (foundational moral rules)
- Rules (operational standards)
- Enforcement mechanism (committees, sanctions)

### Stage 3 — Adoption & Dissemination

The code is officially adopted by the parent body and disseminated to:
- Member institutions
- Researchers (often via training modules)
- Ethics committees (as the basis for protocol review)

### Stage 4 — Operationalization & Review

The code is applied to ongoing research, and periodically revised (e.g., Helsinki has been revised **7 times** since 1964; APA Code has undergone **10 revisions**).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Decision Tree — Applying Codes of Ethics to a Proposed Study

The following analytical sequence mirrors the procedure used by an **Institutional Ethics Committee (IEC)** to evaluate any research protocol. KTU students should internalize this sequence for application-based exam questions.

**Step 1 — Identify the nature of the research.**
Determine whether the study involves *human participants*, *animal subjects*, *biological specimens*, *sensitive personal data*, or *none of the above*.

**Step 2 — Determine the applicable code.**
Match the research nature with the relevant code(s):

- Human-subjects biomedical research → Declaration of Helsinki, ICMR Guidelines
- Behavioural / psychological research → APA Ethics Code, Belmont principles
- Animal research → CPCSEA Guidelines (India), Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals
- Data-only research → COPE + Institutional data-handling policy

**Step 3 — Apply the Universal Principles Test.**
The proposed protocol must demonstrate compliance with all **8 universal principles** (R-B-N-J-I-C-A-T). Any *failing* principle = mandatory protocol revision.

**Step 4 — Verify procedural safeguards.**
Confirm existence of:

- Informed consent form (in the participant's native language)
- Risk mitigation plan
- Compensation policy for research-related injury
- Data storage, anonymization, and retention plan

**Step 5 — Submit to the IEC for review.**
The IEC evaluates:
- *Scientific validity* (must be sound to be ethical)
- *Risk-benefit ratio*
- *Vulnerability classification* of subjects
- *Independence* of researchers from sponsors

**Step 6 — Obtain approval / conditional approval / rejection.**
The IEC issues one of three outcomes, documented in writing.

**Step 7 — Ongoing monitoring and post-study obligations.**
- Continuing review reports
- Reporting of Serious Adverse Events (SAEs)
- Final study report and data archiving
- Acknowledgement of participants and funders

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis — How to Identify the *Primary* Code from a Scenario

Examiners often present a scenario and ask, *"Which code of ethics is most applicable?"* Use the following logical test:

**Test Path A:** Does the research involve *human biological / clinical intervention*?
- **Yes** → Declaration of Helsinki + ICMR Guidelines
- **No** → Proceed to Test Path B

**Test Path B:** Does the research involve *human behavioural / psychological data*?
- **Yes** → APA Ethics Code + Belmont Principles
- **No** → Proceed to Test Path C

**Test Path C:** Does the research involve *animals*?
- **Yes** → CPCSEA / NIH Guide
- **No** → Proceed to Test Path D

**Test Path D:** Does the research involve *publication / authorship disputes*?
- **Yes** → COPE Guidelines
- **No** → Apply the *institution-specific* code of ethics

## 3.4 Worked Example — Scenario Application

> **Scenario (typical KTU-style):** A postgraduate engineering student at KTU proposes a study that uses EEG readings from 30 healthy college volunteers to evaluate a new attention-monitoring headset. The data will be published in an international journal. The study is funded by the headset manufacturer.

**Applying the Test Path:**

1. Involves *human participants* with *biological data* → **Test Path A applies**.
2. **Primary Code:** Declaration of Helsinki + ICMR Guidelines (biomedical).
3. **Secondary Code:** Belmont principles (for the broader ethical framework), plus COPE for publication.
4. **Issues to flag for the IEC:**
    - *Conflict of interest* (manufacturer funding) → requires disclosure.
    - *Informed consent* must explicitly mention data sharing with the funder.
    - *Privacy / confidentiality* — EEG data are biometric identifiers → anonymization mandatory.
    - *Right to withdraw* without penalty must be communicated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This kind of *scenario analysis* is a guaranteed 7-mark sub-question in KTU 2024 ESE. Master the **Test Path A → D** logic above.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# The Codes of Ethics — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Lifecycle of a Code of Ethics

The diagram below maps the **four-stage operational lifecycle** of every major code of ethics, from its historical trigger through its periodic revision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Historical Moral Crisis] --> code1[Nuremberg Trials 1946]
    startA --> code2[Tuskegee Study 1972]
    startA --> code3[Stem Cell Fraud 2005]
    code1 --> ncode[The Nuremberg Code 1947]
    code2 --> bcode[Belmont Report 1979]
    code3 --> pcode[COPE Guidelines 2011]
    ncode --> evolve1[Revisions and Updates]
    bcode --> evolve2[Revisions and Updates]
    pcode --> evolve3[Revisions and Updates]
    evolve1 --> apply1[Applied by Ethics Committees]
    evolve2 --> apply1
    evolve3 --> apply1
    apply1 --> monitor[Ongoing Monitoring and Periodic Revision]
    monitor --> cycle1[Triggered by New Ethical Issues]
    cycle1 --> startA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Architecture of Ethical Codes

This diagram shows the *pyramidal hierarchy* of codes — from universal principles at the apex, down to institutional SOPs at the base.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    apexA[Universal Ethical Principles\nRespect, Beneficence, Justice, Integrity] --> intA[International Codes\nNuremberg, Helsinki, Belmont]
    intA --> natA[National Codes\nICMR India, NIH USA, MRC UK]
    natA --> discA[Discipline Codes\nAPA for Psychology\nCPCSEA for Animal Research]
    discA --> instA[Institutional SOPs\nIRB IEC IAEC Review Protocols]
    instA --> projA[Project Level Ethics Plan\nConsent Forms, Risk Plans, COI Declarations]
    apexA -.underpins.-> projA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Diagram — IEC Review Workflow

The diagram below traces the *decision flow* of an Institutional Ethics Committee reviewing a research protocol, mapping each gate to the relevant code of ethics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gateA[Step 1: Protocol Submission by Researcher] --> gateB[Step 2: Scientific Validity Check]
    gateB --> gateC[Step 3: Identify Applicable Code\nHelsinki or ICMR or APA or COPE]
    gateC --> gateD[Step 4: Eight Principle Compliance Test\nR-B-N-J-I-C-A-T]
    gateD --> gateE[Step 5: Risk Benefit Ratio Assessment]
    gateE --> gateF{Step 6: Decision Gate}
    gateF -- approved --> gateG[Step 7: Ongoing Monitoring\nContinuing Review Reports]
    gateF -- conditional --> gateH[Step 8: Revision and Resubmission]
    gateF -- rejected --> gateI[Step 9: Appeals Process]
    gateG --> gateJ[Step 10: Final Study Closure and Archival]
    gateH --> gateC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note:** The decision gate (Step 6) is where the IEC issues one of three outcomes — *Approved*, *Conditionally Approved (with revisions)*, or *Rejected*. In KTU answers, students often forget to mention the *appeals process* — examiners specifically deduct marks for omitting it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# The Codes of Ethics — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT755 Module 4, 3 Marks]**

*"State any three salient features of the Nuremberg Code of ethics."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Nuremberg Code (1947) was the first internationally accepted code of research ethics, formulated in response to Nazi medical atrocities. Its salient features are:

1. **Absolute voluntary consent** — The human subject must give informed, voluntary consent without coercion, and retain the right to withdraw at any time.
2. **Scientific necessity** — The experiment must be designed so that *favourable results* cannot be obtained by other means, and animal experimentation must precede human trials.
3. **Risk–Benefit proportionality** — The experiment must avoid *unnecessary physical and mental suffering and injury*, with proper facilities to protect subjects.

*(Any three valid features — 1 mark each. The above covers three distinct principles with their definitions.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT755 Module 4, 3 Marks]**

*"Distinguish between the principles of 'Beneficence' and 'Justice' as given in the Belmont Report."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Beneficence | Justice |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Demand** | Do good; maximise benefits; minimise harms | Fair, equitable distribution of research burdens and benefits |
| **Focus** | Outcomes for the *individual* participant | Outcomes for *groups and populations* |
| **Practical Application** | Risk-minimisation protocol, medical care for injured subjects | Equitable selection of subjects — no exploitation of vulnerable populations |

*(Distinction demonstrated through three parallel contrast points — full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEFTT755 Module 4, 14 Marks]**

*(a)* Explain the salient features of the **Declaration of Helsinki** in detail. How does it differ from the **Nuremberg Code** in scope and applicability? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **ICMR National Guidelines (2017)** applicable to biomedical research in India. Highlight any **four general principles** as enumerated by ICMR. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Declaration of Helsinki (7 marks)**

The Declaration of Helsinki, first adopted in 1964 by the World Medical Association and revised most recently in 2013, is a comprehensive ethical framework for *physician-led* biomedical research. Its salient features are:

1. **Patient welfare paramount** — The well-being of the human subject should take precedence over the interests of science and society (Paragraph 8).
2. **Independent ethics committee review** — Every research protocol must be submitted to an *independent ethics committee* for review, comment, and guidance (Paragraph 23).
3. **Distinction between therapeutic and non-therapeutic research** — Helsinki recognises research combined with *patient care* versus *purely scientific* research, and applies different standards.
4. **Special protection for vulnerable populations** — Research involving vulnerable groups (children, prisoners, pregnant women, persons with cognitive impairment) requires special justification.
5. **Risk–Benefit evaluation** — Risks must be *continually assessed* and must be reasonable in relation to anticipated benefits.
6. **Informed consent** — For competent subjects, voluntary informed consent is mandatory; for incompetent subjects, consent of a legal guardian is required.
7. **Post-trial access** — Participants in a clinical trial should be assured of access to beneficial interventions after the trial (Paragraph 34).

**Difference from the Nuremberg Code:**

| Dimension | Nuremberg Code (1947) | Declaration of Helsinki (1964) |
|---|---|---|
| Originator | Judicial tribunal (Nuremberg Military Tribunal) | Professional medical association (WMA) |
| Scope | Primarily non-therapeutic human experimentation | All medical research, including therapeutic research |
| Consent | Sole focus on individual consent | Consent + independent committee review |
| Vulnerable groups | Implicit | Explicit protection |
| Physician-led care | Not addressed | Required for therapeutic research |
| Periodic revision | Single, unchanged document | Revised 7 times, latest 2013 |

*(7 marks = 5 marks features + 2 marks differentiation table.)*

---

**Part (b) — ICMR National Guidelines 2017 (7 marks)**

The ICMR *National Guidelines for Biomedical and Health Research Involving Human Participants, 2017* is the *operative* code of ethics for biomedical research in India. Its structure comprises:

1. **Scope** — Applies to all biomedical, social, behavioural, and health-systems research involving human participants, their biological material, or data.
2. **Twelve General Principles** — ICMR enumerates 12 principles, including essentiality, voluntariness, non-exploitation, privacy, transparency, accountability, totality of responsibility, and public domain.

**Four selected general principles in detail:**

1. **Essentiality** — Research must be *necessary* for the advancement of knowledge; redundant or duplicative research is unethical.
2. **Voluntariness** — Participation must be *voluntary*; undue inducement, coercion, or hidden incentives are prohibited.
3. **Non-exploitation** — Vulnerable populations must be *protected* from exploitation; benefits must flow back to the study population.
4. **Privacy and Confidentiality** — Researchers must maintain the *confidentiality* of all participant information, with restricted data access.

**Procedural Mandates** — ICMR requires institutional ethics committee registration with the *Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)* for clinical trials, and registration of trials in **CTRI (Clinical Trials Registry – India)** before enrolment of the first participant.

*(7 marks = 2 marks overview + 4 marks for four principles + 1 mark for procedural mandate.)*

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT755 Module 4, 14 Marks]**

*(a)* Discuss in detail the **three core principles** of the **Belmont Report**. How do these principles serve as a moral compass for contemporary research involving human subjects? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* What is a **Conflict of Interest (COI)** in research? Discuss its various forms, the ethical concerns it raises, and the disclosure and management strategies prescribed by major codes of ethics. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The Three Principles of the Belmont Report (7 marks)**

The **Belmont Report (1979)** articulated three foundational principles that have become the *de facto* ethical framework for research involving human subjects in the social, behavioural, and biomedical sciences.

**Principle 1 — Respect for Persons**
- Treat individuals as *autonomous agents*.
- Allow them to make *informed, voluntary choices*.
- Provide *extra protection* to those with *diminished autonomy* (children, cognitively impaired individuals, prisoners).
- Operational application: *Informed consent procedure* must ensure comprehension, voluntariness, and revocability.

**Principle 2 — Beneficence**
- Obligation to *do good* and *avoid harm*.
- Researchers must systematically assess *risks and benefits* throughout the study.
- Two rules:
  - *Do not harm*.
  - *Maximise possible benefits and minimise possible harms*.

**Principle 3 — Justice**
- Fair distribution of *research burdens and benefits*.
- No group should *bear the burden* of research without sharing in its *benefits*.
- Operational application: Equitable *selection of subjects* — historically exploited groups (prisoners, minorities, economically disadvantaged) must not be over-represented in risky research.

**Role as a moral compass:**
- Provides a *principle-based* framework that is *flexible* across cultures and disciplines (unlike the rule-based Nuremberg Code).
- Guides Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) in protocol review.
- Forms the *theoretical basis* of subsequent codes such as the APA Ethics Code and the ICMR Guidelines.

*(7 marks = 6 marks for three principles in detail + 1 mark for role as moral compass.)*

---

**Part (b) — Conflict of Interest in Research (7 marks)**

**Definition:** A *Conflict of Interest (COI)* exists when a researcher's *professional judgment* concerning a primary interest (e.g., scientific validity, participant welfare) may be *improperly influenced* by a secondary interest (e.g., financial gain, personal relationships, academic prestige, ideological commitment).

**Forms of COI:**

| Type | Example |
|---|---|
| **Financial COI** | Researcher receives consulting fees from a company whose product is being studied |
| **Personal / Relational COI** | Investigator enrols close relatives or friends as participants |
| **Academic COI** | Author reviewing a competitor's manuscript or grant application |
| **Ideological COI** | Strongly held beliefs bias the design or interpretation of the study |
| **Institutional COI** | University holds equity in the company sponsoring the trial |

**Ethical Concerns Raised:**

1. **Bias in study design** — Selection of comparators, endpoints, or populations may favour the sponsor's product.
2. **Bias in data analysis and interpretation** — Selective reporting of favourable outcomes.
3. **Risk to participants** — COI may lead to under-reporting of adverse events.
4. **Erosion of public trust** — Public confidence in science is damaged when COI is uncovered.

**Disclosure and Management Strategies (per COPE, ICMR, and APA):**

- **Mandatory disclosure** — All sources of funding, equity, consulting fees, and honoraria must be declared at submission and in publications.
- **Independent monitoring** — A *Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB)* independent of the sponsor reviews interim data.
- **Recusal** — Reviewers with COI must recuse themselves from peer review.
- **Conflict-free data analysis** — Statistical analysis must be performed by statisticians *not funded by the sponsor*.
- **Public disclosure** — COI declarations are published alongside the research article.

*(7 marks = 1 mark definition + 2 marks forms table + 2 marks ethical concerns + 2 marks management strategies.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic (Dec 2023 / May 2024 Examiner Reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing codes with regulations** — Students treat the Nuremberg Code, Helsinki Declaration, and ICMR Guidelines as *interchangeable*. They are NOT. The Nuremberg Code is a *judicial pronouncement*, Helsinki is a *professional association declaration*, and ICMR is a *national regulatory document*. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for conflating them.
>
> 2. **Listing principles without explaining their operational application** — It is *not enough* to write "Beneficence" and stop. You must say *what the researcher actually does* to satisfy the principle. Always answer the *"so what?"* question.
>
> 3. **Omitting the Belmont Report's tripartite structure** — The most common 14-mark mistake is writing about Belmont but *forgetting one of the three principles*. Use the *mnemonic* **R-B-J** (*Respect, Beneficence, Justice*) and explicitly write out each.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Indian context** — KTU is a *Kerala* university. Examiners expect students to mention **ICMR Guidelines** and **CPCSEA** (for animal research). Failing to anchor your answer to the Indian regulatory context costs **1–2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Missing the publication-ethics dimension** — For Part-B questions about ethical violations, students frequently forget **COPE** and authorship fraud. Always close your answer by mentioning *post-publication* ethics.
>
> 6. **Not using a comparison table** — For Part-B sub-parts comparing two codes, examiners explicitly reward the use of a *contrasting table*. Omitting the table costs **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** A code of ethics is a *written, public statement of moral standards* governing the conduct of research.
- [ ] **Historical Anchors:** Nuremberg (1947), Helsinki (1964), Belmont (1979), APA (latest 2017), ICMR (2017), COPE (continuous revision).
- [ ] **Nuremberg Code's First Principle:** *Absolute voluntary consent* of the human subject is essential.
- [ ] **Helsinki's Core Idea:** *Patient welfare must take precedence over the interests of science and society.*
- [ ] **Belmont's Three Principles:** Respect for Persons, Beneficence, Justice — remember the *mnemonic R-B-J*.
- [ ] **APA's Five Principles:** Beneficence, Fidelity, Integrity, Justice, Respect for Rights and Dignity.
- [ ] **ICMR's Twelve Principles:** Essentiality, voluntariness, non-exploitation, privacy, confidentiality, accountability, transparency, and others.
- [ ] **COPE's Domain:** Publication and authorship ethics.
- [ ] **Eight Universal Principles Mnemonic:** **R-B-N-J-I-C-A-T** — Respect, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice, Integrity, Confidentiality, Accountability, Transparency.
- [ ] **Decision Path for Identifying Applicable Code:** Human biomedical → Helsinki/ICMR | Behavioural → APA/Belmont | Animal → CPCSEA | Publication → COPE.
- [ ] **IEC Outcome Options:** *Approved*, *Conditionally Approved*, *Rejected* — followed by *appeals process*.
- [ ] **Indian Anchors:** ICMR Guidelines, CTRI registration, CPCSEA for animal research.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Course Outcome:** This topic directly serves **CO4** of PEFTT755.
- [ ] **Bloom's Cognitive Levels Tested:** *Remember* (definitions), *Understand* (principles), *Apply* (scenario analysis), *Analyse* (compare codes).
- [ ] **Common Pitfalls:** Confusing codes with regulations; omitting Indian context; missing COPE; not using comparison tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Avoiding the Problems of Biased Survey
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Avoiding the Problems of Biased Survey

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biased Survey (KTU Definition):** A survey is said to be **biased** when its design, execution, or analysis systematically distorts the measured outcome away from the true population parameter. The bias produces an estimate that is consistently different from the actual value in a particular direction, thereby threatening the **internal validity** and **external validity** of the research.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Research Methodology terminology, survey bias refers to any systematic error (not random sampling error) that creeps into the process of data collection, sampling, measurement, or analysis. Unlike random error, which averages out with larger samples, **bias does not diminish with sample size**—it persists stubbornly regardless of how many respondents are added.

## 1.2 The Statistical Error Decomposition

The total survey error in any estimation can be expressed as:

$$\text{Total Survey Error} = \text{Bias} + \text{Variance (Random Error)}$$

A well-designed survey minimizes **both** components, but they require fundamentally different strategies. Variance shrinks with larger samples, while bias requires careful **design-based** and **model-based** interventions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Coffee Shop Poll"**
> Imagine you want to know whether Keralites prefer chai or coffee. You stand outside a single coffee shop in Kochi from 8 AM to 9 AM and ask 50 customers. You conclude "80% of Keralites prefer coffee."
>
> **What went wrong?**
> - You sampled only at one location (location bias)
> - You sampled only at one time (temporal bias)
> - You asked only people entering a coffee shop (selection bias — tea drinkers may have been excluded)
> - Customers rushing may have answered "coffee" to avoid delay (social desirability)
>
> This is a **biased survey** — your result is systematically skewed, and no matter how many coffee shop customers you ask, the answer remains wrong. To avoid bias, you would need a **probability sample** spread across households, time slots, age groups, and districts, combined with neutral question wording and trained interviewers.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic directly supports **Course Outcomes** related to designing valid research instruments and interpreting survey-based data. It is a high-weightage area in the ESE because research methodology examinations consistently test whether students can identify bias sources and propose corrections.

**Key bias magnitudes (industry standards):**
- Acceptable response rate threshold: **60%** (AAPOR benchmark)
- Acceptable non-response bias: **< 5%** on key estimates
- Acceptable margin of error: **±3% to ±5%** at 95% confidence
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Survey Biases

Survey biases are classified by the **stage of the survey process** at which they originate. The KTU 2024 framework groups them into four families.

### A. Coverage / Selection Biases (Pre-Data-Collection Stage)

| Bias Type | Operational Definition | Real-World Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Coverage Bias** | Sampling frame excludes part of target population | Using landline phone directory in a mobile-only era |
| **Selection Bias** | Non-random inclusion of respondents in sample | Self-selected online polls, snowball sampling |
| **Sampling Frame Bias** | The list used does not match the target population | Voter rolls excluding non-citizens when studying all adults |
| **Non-Response Bias** | Those who refuse differ systematically from those who respond | Busy professionals skipping lengthy questionnaires |

### B. Measurement / Response Biases (Data-Collection Stage)

| Bias Type | Operational Definition | Real-World Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Social Desirability Bias** | Respondents answer in culturally approved ways | Under-reporting alcohol use in conservative surveys |
| **Acquiescence Bias** | Tendency to agree with statements | "Yes-saying" in Likert items |
| **Extremity Bias** | Tendency to use only scale endpoints | Marking only "1" and "5" on a 5-point scale |
| **Recall Bias** | Inaccurate memory of past events | Reporting monthly income inaccurately after 1 year |
| **Order / Primacy-Recency Effect** | Earlier or later options preferred | First option chosen more often in multiple-choice |
| **Interviewer Bias** | Interviewer influences answers | Tone of voice, nodding, leading questions |
| **Question Wording Bias** | Loaded or ambiguous phrasing | "Don't you agree that…?" |
| **Mode Bias** | The medium (phone, online, face-to-face) changes answers | Sensitive topics reported less online |

### C. Processing Biases (Post-Collection Stage)

| Bias Type | Operational Definition |
|---|---|
| **Data Entry Error** | Manual coding mistakes |
| **Coding Bias** | Inconsistent categorization of open-ended responses |
| **Item Non-Response** | Missing values for specific questions |

### D. Analysis-Stage Biases

| Bias Type | Operational Definition |
|---|---|
| **Confirmation Bias** | Analyst interprets data to confirm hypothesis |
| **Aggregation Bias (Simpson's Paradox)** | Trend disappears or reverses when groups combined |
| **Publication Bias** | Significant results published, null results hidden |

## 2.2 The Six Pillars of Bias Avoidance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme - The Six Pillars of Biased Survey Avoidance**
> 1. **Proper Sampling Frame Construction**
> 2. **Probability-Based Sampling Design**
> 3. **Pre-Tested and Validated Questionnaire**
> 4. **Trained and Neutral Field Staff**
> 5. **Multi-Mode Follow-Up for Non-Response**
> 6. **Statistical Adjustment via Weighting and Post-Stratification**

### Pillar 1: Proper Sampling Frame Construction

The **sampling frame** is the actual list of units from which the sample is drawn. It must match the **target population** with minimum under-coverage and over-coverage.

**Checklist:**
- Remove duplicates
- Update frame regularly (frame mortality)
- Add new entrants (births, new firms)
- Verify frame accuracy via pilot listing

### Pillar 2: Probability-Based Sampling Design

Use methods where every unit in the population has a **known, non-zero probability** of selection:

- **Simple Random Sampling (SRS)** — every combination equally likely
- **Systematic Sampling** — every $k$-th unit
- **Stratified Sampling** — divide into homogeneous strata, then sample
- **Cluster Sampling** — sample intact groups (cost-effective)
- **Multi-Stage Sampling** — nested levels of selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid Convenience Sampling and Purposive Sampling** for inferential research — they introduce uncontrolled selection bias.

### Pillar 3: Pre-Testing the Questionnaire

Three sequential phases:

1. **Cognitive Interviewing** — verbalize thought process while answering
2. **Pilot Test** — small sample (typically 30–50) to test logistics
3. **Field Pre-Test** — full procedure on a separate small sample

### Pillar 4: Trained Field Staff

Standardized training must cover:
- Reading questions **verbatim** (no paraphrasing)
- Maintaining **neutral probes** ("Anything else?" instead of "Isn't there more?")
- Recording **paralinguistic cues** (hesitation, tone)
- Avoiding **non-verbal reinforcement**

### Pillar 5: Multi-Mode Follow-Up Protocol

The **AAPOR Standard Response Rate Protocol (RR3)** uses a multi-contact strategy:

$$\text{RR3} = \frac{I}{(I + P) + (R + NC + O) + e(UH + UO)}$$

where:
- $I$ = Complete interviews
- $P$ = Partial interviews
- $R$ = Refusals
- $NC$ = Non-contacts
- $O$ = Other eligible non-interviews
- $UH$ = Unknown if household
- $UO$ = Unknown if eligible
- $e$ = Estimated proportion of unknowns that are eligible

### Pillar 6: Statistical Adjustment

Even perfect design produces some bias, addressed via:

- **Post-stratification weights** to match known population totals
- **Propensity score weighting** to adjust for non-response
- **Calibration / Raking** to align multiple demographic margins
- **Imputation** for item non-response

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Avoiding Biased Survey Problems**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Response Rate (AAPOR RR1) | $\text{RR1} = \dfrac{I}{I + P + R + NC + O}$ | Minimum benchmark $\geq 60\%$ |
| 2 | Margin of Error | $\text{ME} = z_{\alpha/2} \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ |
| 3 | Sample Size (Proportion) | $n = \dfrac{z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | $e$ = tolerable error |
| 4 | Design Effect | $\text{DEFF} = 1 + (\bar{m} - 1)\rho$ | $\bar{m}$ = avg cluster size, $\rho$ = ICC |
| 5 | Effective Sample Size | $n_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{n}{\text{DEFF}}$ | Adjusted for clustering |
| 6 | Post-Stratification Weight | $w_{hi} = \dfrac{N_{hi}}{\hat{N}_{hi}}$ | Ratio of pop to sample count |
| 7 | Hansen-Hurwitz Estimator | $\hat{\tau} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{p_i}$ | $\pi$ estimator (HT) |
| 8 | Horvitz-Thompson Estimator | $\hat{\tau}_{HT} = \sum_{i \in s} \dfrac{y_i}{\pi_i}$ | $\pi_i$ = inclusion prob |
| 9 | Calibration Equation | $\sum_{h} w_h \cdot x_{hi} = X_i$ | Aligns weighted sample to known totals |
| 10 | Non-Response Adjustment Factor | $\text{NR-Weight} = \dfrac{1}{\hat{\phi}}$ | $\hat{\phi}$ = estimated response propensity |
| 11 | Coverage Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{frame}}}{N_{\text{pop}}}$ | $\approx 1$ indicates good coverage |
| 12 | Cooperation Rate | $\text{Coop} = \dfrac{I}{I + R + NC}$ | Reflects field quality |
| 13 | Refusal Rate | $\text{RR}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{R}{I + P + R + NC + O}$ | Lower is better |
| 14 | Contact Rate | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{I + P + R + NC}{I + P + R + NC + O + UH + UO}$ | Indicator of field effort |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Bias-free traffic volume surveys for highway design |
| **Computer Science** | User-experience surveys avoiding self-selection bias |
| **Electrical** | Customer satisfaction surveys for grid reliability |
| **Mechanical** | Quality perception surveys for product redesign |
| **Management** | Employee engagement surveys for HR policy |
| **Biostatistics** | Drug efficacy surveys with proper double-blinding |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Software Engineering** (KTU CS branch), avoiding biased survey is central to **empirical software engineering** — surveys of developer practices must correct for response bias from early adopters and academic respondents.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Sample Size to Control Bias Through Precision

We want to find the minimum sample size $n$ such that the margin of error does not exceed a tolerable level $e$ at confidence level $1 - \alpha$.

**Step 1 — Start with the confidence interval for a proportion:**

$$P\left( \left\vert \hat{p} - p \right\vert \leq z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 2 — Set the margin of error equal to the tolerable error $e$:**

$$e = z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides to remove the square root:**

$$e^2 = z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$ algebraically:**

$$n \cdot e^2 = z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot p(1-p)$$

$$n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the worst-case assumption $p = 0.5$ (maximum variance):**

$$n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot (0.5)(0.5)}{e^2} = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^{2}}{4e^2}$$

**Step 6 — For 95% confidence and $e = 0.05$:**

$$n = \frac{(1.96)^{2}}{4(0.05)^{2}} = \frac{3.8416}{0.01} = 384.16$$

**Result:** We need at least **385 respondents** to control random error within $\pm 5\%$ — but this does NOT eliminate bias. We must also apply the bias-avoidance pillars.

## 3.2 Derivation: Post-Stratification Weight

Suppose we have $H$ strata, and within stratum $h$ we have $n_h$ respondents out of a known population total $N_h$.

**Step 1 — Define the design weight (inverse of inclusion probability):**

$$d_{hi} = \frac{1}{\pi_{hi}} = \frac{N_h}{n_h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the post-stratification adjustment factor so the weighted total in stratum $h$ equals the population total:**

$$w_{hi} = \frac{N_h}{\sum_{i \in s_h} d_{hi}} = \frac{N_h}{\hat{N}_h}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the post-stratified estimator for population mean:**

$$\bar{y}_{PS} = \sum_{h=1}^{H} \frac{N_h}{N} \cdot \bar{y}_h$$

where:

$$\bar{y}_h = \frac{1}{n_h} \sum_{i \in s_h} y_i$$

**Step 4 — Variance estimator under post-stratification:**

$$\hat{V}(\bar{y}_{PS}) = \sum_{h=1}^{H} \left( \frac{N_h}{N} \right)^{2} \cdot \frac{s_h^{2}}{n_h} \left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$$

where $s_h^{2}$ is the within-stratum sample variance. This is **smaller** than SRS variance when strata differ in mean — a direct demonstration that proper design reduces both bias and variance.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Calculating AAPOR RR3 Response Rate

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A researcher contacts 800 sampled households. Results: 480 complete interviews ($I$), 40 partial ($P$), 80 refusals ($R$), 60 non-contacts ($NC$), 20 other eligible non-interviews ($O$), 80 unknown households ($UH$), and 40 unknown eligibility ($UO$). Assume the eligibility rate among unknowns is $e = 0.7$. Compute RR3.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$\text{RR3} = \frac{I}{(I + P) + (R + NC + O) + e(UH + UO)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\text{RR3} = \frac{480}{(480 + 40) + (80 + 60 + 20) + 0.7(80 + 40)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each bracket:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 480$$

$$\text{Partial bracket} = 480 + 40 = 520$$

$$\text{Refusal bracket} = 80 + 60 + 20 = 160$$

$$\text{Estimated unknown eligible} = 0.7 \times (80 + 40) = 0.7 \times 120 = 84$$

**Step 4 — Sum the denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 520 + 160 + 84 = 764$$

**Step 5 — Final computation:**

$$\text{RR3} = \frac{480}{764} = 0.6283 \approx 62.83\%$$

**Interpretation:** Response rate of **62.83%** meets the AAPOR minimum threshold of 60%, indicating a methodologically acceptable survey.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Weighting Adjustment for Non-Response

Suppose district-wide we know $N = 100{,}000$ households. Stratum A (urban) has $N_1 = 40{,}000$; Stratum B (rural) has $N_2 = 60{,}000$. We sampled $n_1 = 200$ and $n_2 = 300$ but got responses from $r_1 = 120$ urban and $r_2 = 270$ rural.

**Step 1 — Compute base weights:**

$$d_1 = \frac{N_1}{n_1} = \frac{40000}{200} = 200$$

$$d_2 = \frac{N_2}{n_2} = \frac{60000}{300} = 200$$

**Step 2 — Compute response-propensity adjustment factor:**

$$\hat{\phi}_1 = \frac{r_1}{n_1} = \frac{120}{200} = 0.60$$

$$\hat{\phi}_2 = \frac{r_2}{n_2} = \frac{270}{300} = 0.90$$

**Step 3 — Compute non-response-adjusted weights:**

$$w_1 = \frac{d_1}{\hat{\phi}_1} = \frac{200}{0.60} = 333.33$$

$$w_2 = \frac{d_2}{\hat{\phi}_2} = \frac{200}{0.90} = 222.22$$

**Step 4 — Verify population reproduction:**

$$\hat{N}_{\text{total}} = w_1 \cdot r_1 + w_2 \cdot r_2 = 333.33 \times 120 + 222.22 \times 270$$

$$\hat{N}_{\text{total}} = 40000 + 60000 = 100{,}000 \checkmark$$

**Interpretation:** Rural households are upweighted (responded well), while urban non-responders are downweighted (responded less). This corrects the **non-response bias** that would have favored rural voices.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Bias-Avoidance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Target Population] --> B[Construct Sampling Frame]
    B --> C{Frame Complete?}
    C -->|No| D[Update and Clean Frame]
    D --> B
    C -->|Yes| E[Choose Probability Sampling Design]
    E --> F[Select Sample Using Random Mechanism]
    F --> G[Design Pre-Tested Questionnaire]
    G --> H[Train Field Interviewers]
    H --> I[Collect Data via Standardized Protocol]
    I --> J{Multi-Mode Follow-Up Needed?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Reminder and Conversion Strategy]
    K --> I
    J -->|No| L[Compute Response Rate AAPOR RR3]
    L --> M{RR3 >= 60 percent?}
    M -->|No| N[Apply Non-Response Weighting]
    M -->|Yes| O[Post-Stratify to Known Totals]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Estimate with HT or PS Estimator]
    P --> Q[Compute Design Effect DEFF]
    Q --> R[Report Estimates with Confidence Intervals]
    R --> S[Final Bias-Adjusted Survey Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Sources of Survey Bias and Their Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PreCollection[Pre-Collection Stage]
        SB[Selection Bias]
        CB[Coverage Bias]
        NRB[Non-Response Bias]
    end
    subgraph Collection[Collection Stage]
        SDB[Social Desirability Bias]
        AB[Acquiescence Bias]
        RB[Recall Bias]
        IB[Interviewer Bias]
        QWB[Question Wording Bias]
    end
    subgraph PostCollection[Post-Collection Stage]
        DEB[Data Entry Bias]
        CB2[Coding Bias]
    end
    subgraph Analysis[Analysis Stage]
        CMB[Confirmation Bias]
        PB[Publication Bias]
        SPB[Simpsons Paradox]
    end

    PreCollection -->|Mitigation| M1[Probability Sampling]
    Collection -->|Mitigation| M2[Validated Instrument and Trained Staff]
    PostCollection -->|Mitigation| M3[Double Data Entry and Coding Rules]
    Analysis -->|Mitigation| M4[Pre-Registered Analysis Plan]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Response Rate Computation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Survey Field Operation Ends] --> Cat1[Categorize All Sampled Units]
    Cat1 --> I[Complete Interviews I]
    Cat1 --> P[Partial Interviews P]
    Cat1 --> R[Refusals R]
    Cat1 --> NC[Non-Contacts NC]
    Cat1 --> O[Other Eligible O]
    Cat1 --> UH[Unknown Households UH]
    Cat1 --> UO[Unknown Eligibility UO]
    I --> Form[Apply AAPOR RR3 Formula]
    P --> Form
    R --> Form
    NC --> Form
    O --> Form
    UH --> Form
    UO --> Form
    Form --> Result[Final Response Rate]
    Result --> Bench{Benchmark Check}
    Bench -->|Below 60 percent| Flag[Flag for Weighting Adjustment]
    Bench -->|At or Above 60 percent| Accept[Accept as Methodologically Sound]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define survey bias. Differentiate between random error and systematic error in survey research.**

**Model Answer:**

> Survey bias is the **systematic deviation** of a survey estimate from the true population value, caused by flaws in sampling, measurement, processing, or analysis.

| Feature | Random Error | Systematic Error (Bias) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Both sides of truth | One-sided |
| Effect of larger $n$ | Decreases | Does **not** decrease |
| Source | Sampling variability | Frame, non-response, instrument, interviewer |
| Correction | Increase sample size | Redesign, train, weight |

**(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List four major sources of non-response bias in a household survey and state two methods to minimize it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of non-response bias:**
1. Refusal to participate
2. Inability to contact the sampled household
3. Language or literacy barrier
4. Inability to participate due to illness or travel

**Methods to minimize:**
1. Multi-contact follow-up (callbacks at different times/days)
2. Trained interviewers with multilingual capability
3. Pre-notification letters and incentives
4. Switch to alternative modes (phone to face-to-face)

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in 14-mark questions on this topic:
> 1. **Confusing bias with variance** — they are not the same; bias persists with $n$, variance decreases.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute eligibility rate $e$** in AAPOR RR3 — examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Writing general "use random sampling" without specifying** the actual probability method (SRS, stratified, cluster).
> 4. **Omitting the design effect** when computing effective sample size for cluster designs.
> 5. **Ignoring the formula for post-stratification** when asked to correct non-response bias.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Define survey bias. Explain the major types of survey bias with at least one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A researcher plans a stratified survey of 1,200 respondents across 3 strata with sizes $N_1 = 4{,}000$, $N_2 = 6{,}000$, $N_3 = 2{,}000$. The within-stratum sample standard deviations (from a pilot) are $s_1 = 8$, $s_2 = 10$, $s_3 = 6$. Using proportional allocation, compute:
  - (i) The sample size in each stratum
  - (ii) The variance of the stratified mean estimator
  - (iii) The Design Effect (DEFF) compared to SRS with same total $n = 1{,}200$, where the overall $s = 9$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:**
Survey bias is a systematic error in the design, conduct, or analysis of a survey that causes the estimated value to deviate consistently from the true population parameter.

**Major Types with Examples:**

1. **Coverage Bias** — sampling frame misses part of the population. *Example:* Using landline directory excludes mobile-only households.

2. **Non-Response Bias** — non-respondents differ from respondents. *Example:* Wealthy individuals refusing income surveys.

3. **Selection Bias** — non-random inclusion. *Example:* Online voluntary poll about internet speed.

4. **Measurement Bias** — instrument measures something other than intended. *Example:* Loaded wording "Don't you support our brave soldiers?"

5. **Interviewer Bias** — interviewer influences answers. *Example:* Same-sex interviewer for sensitive gender topics.

6. **Recall Bias** — inaccurate memory. *Example:* Dietary recall over 1 week vs 24-hour diary.

7. **Social Desirability Bias** — answers skewed toward cultural norms. *Example:* Under-reporting of alcohol use.

8. **Processing / Coding Bias** — inconsistent categorization of open-ended responses.

**(7 marks — 1 for definition + 6 for types with examples)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Proportional Allocation Formula:**

$$n_h = n \cdot \frac{N_h}{N}$$

Total population $N = 4{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 12{,}000$.

**Step 2 — Compute each stratum's allocation:**

$$n_1 = 1200 \cdot \frac{4000}{12000} = 400$$

$$n_2 = 1200 \cdot \frac{6000}{12000} = 600$$

$$n_3 = 1200 \cdot \frac{2000}{12000} = 200$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance of stratified mean estimator:**

$$V(\bar{y}_{ST}) = \sum_{h=1}^{3} \left( \frac{N_h}{N} \right)^{2} \cdot \frac{s_h^{2}}{n_h} \left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$$

**Stratum 1:**

$$\frac{4000^{2}}{12000^{2}} \cdot \frac{8^{2}}{400} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{400}{4000}\right) = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \frac{64}{400} \cdot 0.9 = 0.0160$$

**Stratum 2:**

$$\frac{6000^{2}}{12000^{2}} \cdot \frac{10^{2}}{600} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{600}{6000}\right) = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{100}{600} \cdot 0.9 = 0.0375$$

**Stratum 3:**

$$\frac{2000^{2}}{12000^{2}} \cdot \frac{6^{2}}{200} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{200}{2000}\right) = \frac{1}{36} \cdot \frac{36}{200} \cdot 0.9 = 0.0045$$

**Total Variance:**

$$V(\bar{y}_{ST}) = 0.0160 + 0.0375 + 0.0045 = 0.0580$$

$$\text{SE}(\bar{y}_{ST}) = \sqrt{0.0580} = 0.2408$$

**Step 4 — Variance of SRS Mean:**

$$V(\bar{y}_{SRS}) = \frac{s^{2}}{n} \left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right) = \frac{9^{2}}{1200} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1200}{12000}\right) = 0.0675 \cdot 0.9 = 0.06075$$

**Step 5 — Design Effect:**

$$\text{DEFF} = \frac{V(\bar{y}_{ST})}{V(\bar{y}_{SRS})} = \frac{0.0580}{0.06075} = 0.955$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{DEFF} < 1$ means stratification **improved** precision by approximately 4.5% compared to SRS — a clear gain, demonstrating the bias-and-variance reduction power of stratified designs.

**(7 marks — 2 for allocation, 3 for variance, 2 for DEFF interpretation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the AAPOR Standard Definitions and the RR3 response rate formula. Describe how a low response rate introduces bias, with a numerical example of computing the non-response adjustment factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A college wants to assess student satisfaction with the new NEP 2020 curriculum. The administration samples 800 students but obtains:
  - 520 complete interviews
  - 30 partial interviews
  - 100 refusals
  - 50 non-contacts
  - 40 other eligible non-interviews
  - 40 unknown households
  - 20 unknown eligibility

  Assume eligibility rate $e = 0.8$. Compute RR3, classify the response rate as per AAPOR standards, and recommend two corrective actions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**AAPOR Standard Definitions:**
The American Association for Public Opinion Research (AAPOR) publishes standardized formulas to compute response, refusal, cooperation, and contact rates so that surveys are comparable across studies and organizations.

**RR3 Formula:**

$$\text{RR3} = \frac{I}{(I + P) + (R + NC + O) + e(UH + UO)}$$

- $I$ = Complete interviews
- $P$ = Partial interviews
- $R$ = Refusals
- $NC$ = Non-contacts
- $O$ = Other eligible non-interviews
- $UH$ = Unknown if household
- $UO$ = Unknown if eligible
- $e$ = Estimated eligibility rate among unknowns

**How Low Response Rate Introduces Bias:**
If the non-respondents differ from respondents on the key variable of interest, the sample mean is biased. The bias is:

$$\text{Bias} = \bar{y}_R - \bar{y}_{NR}$$

where $\bar{y}_R$ = respondent mean and $\bar{y}_{NR}$ = non-respondent mean.

**Numerical Example:**
Suppose 200 of 1000 sampled respond. The response propensity $\hat{\phi} = 200/1000 = 0.20$. The non-response adjustment weight is:

$$w_{NR} = \frac{1}{\hat{\phi}} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5$$

Each respondent's contribution is multiplied by 5 to represent 5 similar non-respondents, partially correcting the bias. However, this adjustment assumes non-respondents are exchangeable with respondents on unobserved dimensions — a strong but standard assumption.

**(7 marks — 3 for AAPOR + RR3, 4 for bias + adjustment example)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify all categories:**
$I = 520$, $P = 30$, $R = 100$, $NC = 50$, $O = 40$, $UH = 40$, $UO = 20$, $e = 0.8$.

**Step 2 — Apply RR3 formula:**

$$\text{RR3} = \frac{520}{(520 + 30) + (100 + 50 + 40) + 0.8 \times (40 + 20)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each bracket:**

- Numerator: $I = 520$
- Partial bracket: $520 + 30 = 550$
- Refusal bracket: $100 + 50 + 40 = 190$
- Estimated unknown eligible: $0.8 \times (40 + 20) = 0.8 \times 60 = 48$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 550 + 190 + 48 = 788$$

**Step 5 — Compute RR3:**

$$\text{RR3} = \frac{520}{788} = 0.6599 \approx 65.99\%$$

**Step 6 — Classify per AAPOR:**
AAPOR classifies response rates. A rate of **65.99%** is above the 60% minimum threshold and is rated as **acceptable** (Category 2 of AAPOR's "Response Rate Rating" system).

**Step 7 — Recommend two corrective actions:**
1. **Post-stratification weighting** — weight respondents to match the known student population distribution by department, year, and gender, correcting residual non-response bias.
2. **Multi-mode follow-up** — conduct callbacks via email, WhatsApp, and in-person visits to convert partials and non-contacts into completes, especially in strata with lower response.

**(7 marks — 3 for computation, 2 for classification, 2 for recommendations)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Avoiding the Problems of Biased Survey**

- **Survey bias is systematic**, not random — larger samples do not eliminate it.
- The **Total Survey Error = Bias + Variance**; both need attention.
- **Sampling frame must match the target population** to prevent coverage bias.
- **Always use probability sampling** (SRS, stratified, cluster, multi-stage) for inferential research.
- **Avoid non-probability methods** (convenience, purposive, snowball) when generalizing to a population.
- **Pre-test the questionnaire** through cognitive interviews and pilot tests.
- **Train field staff** to read questions verbatim and use neutral probes.
- **AAPOR RR3** is the gold-standard response rate formula for surveys.
- **Response rate $\geq 60\%$** is the minimum acceptable benchmark.
- **Non-response bias** is addressed by callbacks, incentives, and propensity weighting.
- **Post-stratification weights** align sample totals to known population totals.
- **Design Effect (DEFF)** quantifies the variance inflation due to complex sampling.
- **Effective sample size** $n_{\text{eff}} = n / \text{DEFF}$ is the basis for inference in cluster designs.
- **The Horvitz-Thompson estimator** $\hat{\tau}_{HT} = \sum_{i \in s} y_i / \pi_i$ is the unbiased foundation for probability samples.
- **Stratification usually reduces both bias and variance** — $\text{DEFF} < 1$ is a gain.
- **Order effects, social desirability, and acquiescence** are interview-stage biases fixed by questionnaire design.
- **Pre-register the analysis plan** to prevent confirmation bias and post-hoc rationalization.
- **Avoid leading questions**; use neutral wording validated by cognitive testing.
- **Report the response rate, DEFF, and confidence intervals** in every survey-based research output.
- **Beating bias is 80% design and 20% statistical adjustment** — prevention is better than correction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Occupational Health and Safety
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT755 - RESEARCH METHODOLOGY AND STATISTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Occupational Health and Safety

# Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)** is a multidisciplinary field of study and practice that focuses on the **promotion, protection, and maintenance** of the highest degree of physical, mental, and social well-being of workers in all occupations. It is formally defined by the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO)** as the discipline dealing with the prevention of ill-health and injuries caused by working conditions, and the protection of workers from factors adverse to their health.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme research methodology, OHS represents the **systematic framework** through which researchers, engineers, and managers identify, evaluate, and mitigate workplace hazards using both **qualitative assessment protocols** and **quantitative statistical inference** (e.g., accident rates, exposure limits, hazard ratios).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a workplace as a **kitchen where a chef prepares food daily**. Just as a chef wears gloves to avoid burns, a hair net to maintain hygiene, and keeps a fire extinguisher nearby for emergencies, **OHS provides workers with their "gloves, hair nets, and fire extinguishers"** — protective gear, hygiene protocols, and emergency systems. The kitchen's "recipe for safety" is essentially the OHS policy that prevents accidents before they happen, rather than scrambling to fix them afterward.

The **core philosophy** is *prevention over cure*. Rather than treating injuries after they occur, OHS establishes **engineering controls**, **administrative controls**, and **personal protective equipment (PPE)** to prevent hazards from materializing into incidents.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Thresholds

The following standard metrics form the backbone of OHS measurement (all standardized by **ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines** and adopted globally):

| Metric | Standard Reference Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) | OSHA 29 CFR 1910 | ppm or mg/m³ |
| Threshold Limit Value (TLV) | ACGIH | ppm or mg/m³ |
| Time-Weighted Average (TWA) | 8-hour workday | ppm |
| Short-Term Exposure Limit (STEL) | 15-minute average | ppm |
| Acceptable Daily Intake | WHO reference | mg/kg body weight |
| **Fatality Rate (Mining)** | Benchmark **< 0.05** | per 200,000 man-hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **TWA** is the average exposure over an **8-hour workday**, while **STEL** is the maximum allowed average exposure over a **15-minute window**. Both are critical for industrial hygienists interpreting air sampling data.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Hazard Controls

The OHS discipline organizes interventions in a strict **hierarchy of controls**, ordered from most effective to least effective:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a foundational OHS concept: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)**. KTU examiners frequently test this ordering.

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard from the workplace (most effective).
2. **Substitution** — Replace the hazard with a less dangerous alternative (e.g., water-based paint instead of solvent-based).
3. **Engineering Controls** — Isolate workers from the hazard (e.g., machine guards, ventilation systems, sound enclosures).
4. **Administrative Controls** — Change work procedures (e.g., job rotation, training, signage, work-rest schedules).
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Protect workers with personal gear (gloves, respirators, helmets) — *least effective because it relies on worker compliance*.

### 2.2 Classification of Occupational Hazards

Occupational hazards are classified into **five principal categories**, each requiring distinct statistical and engineering mitigation strategies:

- **Physical Hazards** — Noise, vibration, radiation (ionizing & non-ionizing), temperature extremes, electricity.
- **Chemical Hazards** — Dusts, fumes, vapors, liquids, gases (e.g., benzene, asbestos, lead).
- **Biological Hazards** — Bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites (e.g., in healthcare, agriculture).
- **Ergonomic Hazards** — Repetitive motion, awkward postures, manual lifting, poor workstation design.
- **Psychosocial Hazards** — Workplace stress, harassment, violence, long working hours, burnout.

### 2.3 The Risk Assessment Framework

Risk is mathematically defined as the product of **probability** and **consequence**:

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Probability} \times \text{Consequence}$$

Where:
- **Probability** = Likelihood of the hazardous event occurring (qualitative scale: 1–5).
- **Consequence** = Severity of harm if it occurs (qualitative scale: 1–5).

> [!NOTE]
> Risk matrices (typically **5×5 grids**) are used to visualize and rank hazards as Low, Medium, High, or Extreme. This is a **direct application of statistical inference to safety management**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are **statistical measures of OHS performance** and are highly examinable in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern:

| Formula | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Rate (FR)** | $\text{FR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$ | Injuries per million man-hours |
| **Severity Rate (SR)** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of days lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$ | Days lost per million man-hours |
| **Incidence Rate (IR)** | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of injuries} \times 10^3}{\text{Number of workers}}$ | Injuries per 1000 workers |
| **Frequency-Severity Index (FSI)** | $\text{FSI} = \dfrac{\text{FR} \times \text{SR}}{1000}$ | Composite safety index |
| **Mean Exposure (TWA)** | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i \cdot T_i}{\sum T_i}$ | Time-weighted average concentration |
| **Hazard Ratio (HR)** | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{exposed}}}{\lambda_{\text{unexposed}}}$ | Ratio of hazard rates (epidemiology) |
| **Relative Risk (RR)** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{P(\text{disease} \mid \text{exposed})}{P(\text{disease} \mid \text{unexposed})}$ | Probability ratio |
| **Standardized Mortality Ratio** | $\text{SMR} = \dfrac{\text{Observed deaths}}{\text{Expected deaths}} \times 100$ | Comparison vs. general population |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU exam, ensure you retain the **multiplier** (10^6 for FR and SR per ILO convention; 10^3 for IR per OSHA convention). Mixing up these constants is a **3-mark trap**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The OHS framework is essential across industries:
- **Construction** — Fall protection, scaffolding safety, confined space protocols.
- **Manufacturing** — Lockout/Tagout (LOTO), machine guarding, chemical exposure monitoring.
- **Healthcare** — Needlestick prevention, biological exposure control, ergonomic patient handling.
- **Mining** — Methane detection, rock-burst monitoring, respirable dust controls.
- **IT / Office** — Ergonomic workstation design, screen time limits, psychosocial well-being.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Frequency-Severity Index (FSI)

The **FSI** combines two key indicators (FR and SR) into a single composite metric, allowing industries to benchmark safety performance.

**Step 1: Start with the definition of Frequency Rate.**

$$\text{FR} = \frac{N_i \times 10^6}{H}$$

Where $N_i$ = number of injuries, $H$ = man-hours worked.

**Step 2: Start with the definition of Severity Rate.**

$$\text{SR} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{H}$$

Where $D$ = number of days lost due to injuries.

**Step 3: Multiply FR and SR (in their raw ratio form, divided by 10^6 each, so the 10^6 squared = 10^12).**

$$\text{FR} \times \text{SR} = \frac{N_i \times D \times 10^{12}}{H^2}$$

**Step 4: Divide by 10^3 to normalize the FSI to a manageable scale (this is the convention established by the British Standards Institution, BS 2925).**

$$\text{FSI} = \frac{\text{FR} \times \text{SR}}{1000}$$

**Final Composite Form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSI} &= \frac{1}{1000} \cdot \left(\frac{N_i \times 10^6}{H}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{D \times 10^6}{H}\right) \\
&= \frac{N_i \cdot D \times 10^9}{H^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation Logic:**
- Higher FSI indicates **poorer safety performance** (more injuries AND more days lost).
- Lower FSI indicates **better safety performance**.
- A company with high FR but low SR has frequent minor injuries; a company with low FR but high SR has rare but severe accidents.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A factory employs **500 workers** who work **8 hours/day, 300 days/year**. During the year, there were **20 reportable injuries** resulting in a total of **400 days lost**. Compute the Frequency Rate (FR), Severity Rate (SR), and Frequency-Severity Index (FSI).

**Step 1: Compute total man-hours worked.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 500 \text{ workers} \times 8 \text{ hours/day} \times 300 \text{ days/year} \\
H &= 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Frequency Rate formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FR} &= \frac{N_i \times 10^6}{H} \\
&= \frac{20 \times 10^6}{1{,}200{,}000} \\
\text{FR} &= 16.67 \text{ injuries per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Severity Rate formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} &= \frac{D \times 10^6}{H} \\
&= \frac{400 \times 10^6}{1{,}200{,}000} \\
\text{SR} &= 333.33 \text{ days lost per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute the FSI.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSI} &= \frac{\text{FR} \times \text{SR}}{1000} \\
&= \frac{16.67 \times 333.33}{1000} \\
\text{FSI} &= 5.56
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**

A FSI of **5.56** is considered **acceptable performance** in light manufacturing. Compare this against industry benchmarks (e.g., chemical industry FSI ~ 2–4; construction ~ 8–15).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Risk Control

When multiple hazards are identified, OHS professionals use a **risk priority matrix** to allocate resources:

| Hazard ID | Hazard Description | Probability (P) | Consequence (C) | Risk Score = P × C | Control Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Chemical spill | 4 | 5 | 20 (Extreme) | Immediate action |
| H2 | Slippery floor | 5 | 2 | 10 (High) | Short-term action |
| H3 | Repetitive strain | 3 | 3 | 9 (Medium) | Long-term action |
| H4 | Office glare | 2 | 1 | 2 (Low) | Monitor |
| H5 | Noise exposure | 5 | 4 | 20 (Extreme) | Immediate action |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Score Thresholds (5×5 matrix):** 1–4 = Low; 5–9 = Medium; 10–15 = High; 16–25 = Extreme. Hazards with score ≥ 16 require **immediate corrective action**.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Time-Weighted Average (TWA)

The TWA accounts for **variable exposure levels** during a workday. Suppose a worker is exposed to:

- Concentration $C_1$ for time $T_1$,
- Concentration $C_2$ for time $T_2$,
- …and so on.

**Step 1: Define exposure intensity per interval.**

$$E_i = C_i \cdot T_i$$

**Step 2: Sum total exposure across all intervals.**

$$E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot T_i$$

**Step 3: Divide by total time to obtain the time-weighted average.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TWA} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot T_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is mathematically analogous to the **expected value** $E[X] = \sum x_i \cdot P(x_i)$ in probability theory, where concentration $C_i$ is analogous to the random variable value and time $T_i / \sum T_i$ acts as a normalized weight. This is why research methodology students must master TWA — it links **occupational hygiene** directly to **statistical inference**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Controls (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Eliminate Hazard] --> nodeB[Substitute with Safer Alternative]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Apply Engineering Controls]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Apply Administrative Controls]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Provide Personal Protective Equipment]
    nodeA -. Most Effective .-> nodeE
    nodeE -. Least Effective .-> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeF[Examples Machine Guards Ventilation Enclosures]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Examples Helmets Gloves Respirators Goggles]
```

### 4.2 Risk Assessment Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Identify Hazards] --> node2[Assess Who May Be Harmed]
    node2 --> node3[Evaluate Risks]
    node3 --> node4{Risk Acceptable?}
    node4 --> node5[Yes Implement Controls and Monitor]
    node4 --> node6[No Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    node6 --> node7[Re-evaluate Residual Risk]
    node7 --> node4
    node5 --> node8[Document and Review Periodically]
```

flowchart TD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Data Collection Injury Records Exposure Logs] --> nodeB[Statistical Analysis FR SR FSI]

    nodeD --> nodeF[No Implement Corrective Actions]

    nodeE --> nodeA
```
