## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Occupational Health and Safety (OHS). Mention any two occupational hazards with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) is a multidisciplinary field concerned with the promotion and maintenance of the highest degree of physical, mental, and social well-being of workers in all occupations. It aims to prevent ill-health and injuries caused by working conditions and to protect workers from factors adverse to their health (as jointly defined by the **ILO** and **WHO** in 1950).

**Two Hazards (1 mark each):**
1. **Physical Hazard — Noise:** Prolonged exposure to noise levels above **85 dBA (TWA)** can cause noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL). Common in industries such as construction, foundries, and textile mills.
2. **Chemical Hazard — Asbestos Dust:** Inhalation of airborne asbestos fibers causes asbestosis, mesothelioma, and lung cancer. Common in shipyards, insulation work, and older building demolition.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)? List four types of PPE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
PPE refers to protective gear worn by workers to minimize exposure to specific occupational hazards. It is the **last line of defense** in the Hierarchy of Controls and includes all equipment designed to protect the head, eyes, ears, respiratory system, hands, feet, and body.

**Four Types (½ mark each = 2 marks):**
1. **Head Protection** — Hard hats to prevent impact injuries from falling objects.
2. **Eye Protection** — Safety goggles and face shields against flying particles or chemical splashes.
3. **Respiratory Protection** — N95 respirators, half-face or full-face respirators for dust, fumes, and vapors.
4. **Hand Protection** — Gloves (cut-resistant, chemical-resistant, heat-resistant) based on the type of hazard.

### 5.2 Part B: ESE Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always state the formula before substituting values** — students lose 1–2 marks for jumping straight to computation.
> 2. **Retain units throughout** — a final answer without units (e.g., "16.67" without "per million man-hours") is penalized.
> 3. **Mention the formula reference standard** (e.g., "ILO-OSH 2001" or "BS 2925") — this shows conceptual depth and earns partial credit.
> 4. **For 14-mark numerical problems, intermediate calculation steps are mandatory.** Showing only the final answer without derivation will cap your score at ~50%.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define the **Frequency Rate (FR)** and **Severity Rate (SR)** of accidents in an industrial setting. Explain their significance in occupational safety analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing plant employs **800 workers**, each working **8 hours/day for 300 days/year**. During the year, **15 reportable injuries** occurred, resulting in **600 lost workdays**. Calculate the **Frequency Rate (FR)**, **Severity Rate (SR)**, and the **Frequency-Severity Index (FSI)**. Interpret the results. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Definition and Significance — 7 marks:**

**Frequency Rate (FR) — Definition (2 marks):**

$$\text{FR} = \frac{\text{Number of reportable injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$$

Unit: injuries per million man-hours. The factor **10^6** normalizes the rate to a standard scale, allowing comparison across industries of different sizes (per **ILO Code of Practice**).

**Severity Rate (SR) — Definition (2 marks):**

$$\text{SR} = \frac{\text{Number of workdays lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Total man-hours worked}}$$

Unit: lost workdays per million man-hours. It measures the **seriousness** of accidents rather than their frequency.

**Significance (3 marks):**
1. **Benchmarking:** Both rates enable comparison of safety performance across industries, plants, and time periods.
2. **Trend Analysis:** Tracking these rates over time reveals whether safety interventions are effective.
3. **Resource Allocation:** High FR indicates a need for engineering controls; high SR indicates a need for emergency response and trauma care improvements.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Many statutory bodies (e.g., **OSHA**, **Factories Act**) require reporting of these rates.
5. **Insurance Premiums:** Lower FR and SR lead to lower workers' compensation insurance premiums.

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Total man-hours worked (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 800 \text{ workers} \times 8 \text{ hours/day} \times 300 \text{ days/year} \\
H &= 1{,}920{,}000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Frequency Rate (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FR} &= \frac{N_i \times 10^6}{H} \\
&= \frac{15 \times 10^6}{1{,}920{,}000} \\
\text{FR} &= 7.81 \text{ injuries per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Severity Rate (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SR} &= \frac{D \times 10^6}{H} \\
&= \frac{600 \times 10^6}{1{,}920{,}000} \\
\text{SR} &= 312.50 \text{ lost workdays per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Frequency-Severity Index (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSI} &= \frac{\text{FR} \times \text{SR}}{1000} \\
&= \frac{7.81 \times 312.50}{1000} \\
\text{FSI} &= 2.44
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (1 mark):**
- An FSI of **2.44** indicates **excellent safety performance**, well within the acceptable range for light manufacturing (industry benchmark FSI ~ 2–5).
- The relatively low FR (7.81) and moderate SR (312.50) suggest that while accidents are infrequent, those that do occur have moderate severity. The plant should focus on **engineering controls** to prevent minor injuries and on **training programs** to reduce lost workdays.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** in Occupational Health and Safety with suitable examples. Why is elimination considered the most effective control measure? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **Factories Act 1948 (India)** in ensuring occupational health and safety. Mention any four key provisions related to worker welfare. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Hierarchy of Controls — 7 marks:**

The Hierarchy of Controls is a systematic framework used in OHS to determine the most effective ways to control workplace hazards. It ranks interventions from most effective to least effective:

**1. Elimination (1 mark):**
Removal of the hazard entirely from the workplace. This is the most effective because no hazard means no risk.
*Example:* Removing mercury from thermometers and replacing with digital sensors eliminates the risk of mercury poisoning.

**2. Substitution (1 mark):**
Replacing a hazardous substance or process with a less hazardous one.
*Example:* Replacing solvent-based paints with water-based paints reduces VOC (volatile organic compound) exposure and fire risk.

**3. Engineering Controls (1 mark):**
Isolating workers from the hazard through physical modifications.
*Example:* Installing machine guards, local exhaust ventilation, sound enclosures around noisy equipment.

**4. Administrative Controls (1 mark):**
Changing work procedures and policies.
*Example:* Job rotation to limit exposure time, mandatory safety training, work-rest schedules, posted warning signs.

**5. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) (1 mark):**
Providing workers with protective gear as the last line of defense.
*Example:* Hard hats, safety goggles, respirators, steel-toed boots.

**Why Elimination is Most Effective (2 marks):**
1. **Permanent solution:** Once the hazard is removed, it cannot cause harm — no ongoing monitoring required.
2. **No worker compliance dependency:** Unlike PPE, elimination does not rely on workers to remember or correctly wear protective gear.
3. **No residual risk:** Other controls (especially PPE) only reduce exposure; elimination removes the hazard entirely.
4. **Cost-effective long-term:** Although initial redesign may be expensive, eliminating the hazard avoids recurring costs of PPE, training, and medical care.
5. **Hierarchy principle:** All subsequent controls (substitution, engineering, administrative, PPE) implicitly assume the hazard still exists; only elimination removes it.

**(b) Factories Act 1948 (India) — 7 marks:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Factories Act 1948** is the principal legislation in India governing occupational health, safety, and welfare in factories. It was enacted to regulate the working conditions in factories and to protect workers from occupational hazards.

**Role of the Factories Act (3 marks):**
- Establishes **minimum standards** for health, safety, and welfare in factories.
- Empowers **State Governments** to appoint inspectors for enforcement.
- Mandates **registration and licensing** of factories.
- Provides for **penalties and prosecution** for non-compliance.
- Aligns India's OHS framework with **ILO conventions**.

**Four Key Provisions Related to Worker Welfare (1 mark each = 4 marks):**

1. **Section 11 — Cleanliness:** Mandates that factories be kept clean and free from effluvia (noxious gases, dust). Walls and ceilings must be painted/whitewashed periodically.

2. **Section 13 — Ventilation and Temperature:** Requires effective and suitable ventilation to maintain a comfortable temperature and remove dust, fumes, or other impurities.

3. **Section 14 — Dust and Fumes:** Requires effective measures to be taken to prevent inhalation, ingestion, and accumulation of dust and fumes.

4. **Section 42 — First Aid:** Every factory must have a **first-aid box or cupboard** readily accessible, and in factories employing more than **150 workers**, a **trained first-aid attendant** must be present during working hours.

5. **Section 45 — Accommodation for Workers:** Adequate and suitable accommodation must be provided for workers near the factory (especially for migrant and contract workers).

6. **Section 79 — Annual Leave with Wages:** Workers are entitled to annual leave with wages after working for **240 days** in a calendar year.

7. **Section 87 — Notification of Accidents:** Any accident causing death or bodily injury must be reported to the Inspector within **24 hours**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **OHS Definition:** Joint ILO/WHO definition — promote, protect, maintain physical, mental, and social well-being of workers.
- **Hierarchy of Controls (Most → Least Effective):** **E**limination → **S**ubstitution → **E**ngineering → **A**dministrative → **PPE** (Mnemonic: **"E-S-E-A-P"**).
- **Five Hazard Categories:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial.
- **Frequency Rate (FR):** Uses multiplier **10^6**; unit = injuries per million man-hours.
- **Severity Rate (SR):** Uses multiplier **10^6**; unit = lost workdays per million man-hours.
- **Incidence Rate (IR):** Uses multiplier **10^3**; unit = injuries per 1000 workers.
- **FSI Formula:** $\text{FSI} = \dfrac{\text{FR} \times \text{SR}}{1000}$ — lower FSI = better safety.
- **TWA Formula:** $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i \cdot T_i}{\sum T_i}$ — analogous to expected value in probability.
- **TWA Standard:** 8-hour workday; **STEL** = 15-minute exposure window.
- **Noise Threshold:** OSHA permissible limit = **90 dBA** (TWA); ACGIH TLV = **85 dBA**.
- **Factories Act 1948:** Principal OHS legislation in India; requires accident reporting within **24 hours**.
- **Key Acts & Standards to Remember:** Factories Act 1948, Mines Act 1952, OSH Code 2020 (Bills), OSHA (USA), ILO-OSH 2001, ISO 45001:2018.
- **Risk Score:** Risk = Probability × Consequence; thresholds — 1–4 Low, 5–9 Medium, 10–15 High, 16–25 Extreme.
- **PPE is always the LAST line of defense** — frequently tested as a conceptual trap.
- **Hazard Ratio (HR)** and **Relative Risk (RR)** are statistical measures linking OHS exposure to disease outcomes (epidemiology).
- **Always retain units** in numerical answers; **always state the formula before substitution**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute KTU Pitfall Alerts:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse **OSHA** (Occupational Safety and Health Administration, USA, 1970) with **OSHA** (Occupational Safety and Health Act) — same acronym, different referents.
> - **Hazard ≠ Risk.** A hazard is the potential source of harm; risk is the probability × consequence of that harm materializing. This is a **2–3 mark conceptual trap** in KTU exams.
> - **PEL (OSHA)** vs **TLV (ACGIH)** — PEL is legally enforceable; TLV is advisory. Many students lose marks by interchanging them.
